stories.title: Rejected by My Alpha
stories.author: Vera Wilde
stories.blurb: He is the ruthless Alpha every wolf fears — and under the full moon, fate names me, a nobody kitchen omega, as his one true mate. Then he rejects me in front of the entire pack and casts me out to die. But the Severing that should have killed me wakes something a thousand years old, sleeping in my blood. Now the Alpha who threw me away can feel our broken bond roaring back to life — and he has no idea what I'm becoming. He'll kneel before I'm done with him.

chapters.title: The Choosing
chapters.body:
They make the omegas stand at the back, where the firelight doesn't reach.

I've spent eighteen years learning to live in that dark. Tonight, with the whole Blackthorn pack gathered in the Choosing clearing and the moon climbing fat and silver over the pines, I press my spine to the cold bark of an oak and try to make myself smaller than the shadow I'm standing in.

It doesn't work. Mara finds me anyway.

"Still here?" The kitchen-mistress looks me up and down, at the dress I scrubbed three pots to borrow, at the dark hair I actually brushed. Her lip curls. "Sweet of you to dress up, little mouse. The moon doesn't waste a mate on a girl whose own mother was a stray."

"My mother had a name," I say. Quietly. Quiet is the only armor they let me keep.

"Your mother had a grave." Mara smiles. "Go on. Watch the real wolves get chosen. Maybe you'll learn your place by the end of it."

I already know my place. It's here, at the back, in the dark.

But I came anyway. Every wolf in the territory comes to their Choosing — the night you turn eighteen and the Moon Goddess, if she bothers, threads a bond between you and the one fate built for you. You feel it the moment the full moon crests: a pull, low in the chest, like a hook behind the ribs, towing you toward the only person in the world who's yours.

Most of us feel nothing. The unmatched outnumber the chosen ten to one. I've watched it happen for years from this exact patch of dark — the lucky few crossing the clearing with their faces lit up like they've swallowed the moon, and the rest of us pretending not to want it.

I am very good at pretending not to want things.

A horn sounds. The pack quiets all at once, two hundred wolves in human skin turning toward the dais of black stone at the clearing's heart.

And there he is.

Alpha Kane Blackthorn stands above us with the firelight carving him out of the night — black hair, a jaw like a closing door, the old scar splitting his right eyebrow that he earned the night his father died and he took a slaughtered pack and made it feared again. He doesn't look at the crowd the way other alphas do, hungry for it. He looks at us like a man counting weapons. Storm-grey eyes, moving, cataloguing, cold.

I've served him dinner a hundred times and he has never once seen me. To Kane Blackthorn I am a pair of hands that set down a plate. That's fine. Being invisible to a man like that is the safest a girl like me ever gets.

Beside him stands Cassia Ravenscar, and *she* he sees.

Everyone does. She's all silver gown and silvering laugh, the daughter of Alpha Ravenscar, sent here to seal the alliance that keeps two packs from each other's throats. The whisper in the kitchens is that the bond doesn't matter for alphas — that Kane will take Cassia as Luna whether the moon agrees or not, because politics outrank fate when there's a war on the horizon. She has her hand on his arm like she already owns it.

I don't let myself look at him long. Wanting things you can't have is a habit that gets omegas killed.

"Children of Blackthorn." Kane's voice rolls over the clearing, low and even, and the pack leans into it like grass under wind. "The moon is high. Let her choose."

The moon crests the trees.

And the world changes.

It hits me low and sudden, that hook behind the ribs — not a tug, a *yank*, like a fist has closed around something inside me I didn't know was loose and pulled. The breath goes out of me. Heat floods up my throat. Around the clearing I hear the soft cries of the chosen finding each other, the gasps, the laughter, two boys near the fire turning to stare at one another like the ground's gone out from under them.

But my hook isn't pulling me toward any of them.

It's pulling me forward. Toward the firelight. Toward the dais of black stone.

*No.* I dig my heels into the dirt. This is wrong, this is a mistake, the moon has confused me with someone who matters. The pull only sharpens, dragging at my sternum until my eyes water, until I take one helpless stumbling step out of my safe patch of dark and into the light, and another, and the wolves between me and the fire turn to see what's moving at the back where nothing is ever supposed to move.

On the dais, Kane Blackthorn goes rigid.

His head turns. Not lazily, not counting weapons — it *snaps*, the way a predator's does when something it has been ignoring suddenly becomes the only thing in the world. And those cold storm-grey eyes that have looked through me a hundred times find me across two hundred wolves and the leaping fire, and they lock.

The pull between us draws taut as a wire. I feel it land in him. I watch it land — watch the colour drop out of his face, watch his hand close white-knuckled on the black stone, watch the Alpha of Blackthorn look, for the first time in the years I've known his shadow, like a man who has been struck.

Cassia's laugh dies in her throat.

The clearing goes silent. Two hundred wolves following the line of their Alpha's stare, back, back, past the betas and the warriors and the chosen, all the way to the dark at the edge of the firelight where the kitchen omega stands shaking with the moon's hook buried in her chest.

Mara's voice, very small, somewhere beside me: "*No.*"

But the moon has already decided. I feel it singing down the bond, certain as sunrise, terrible as a death sentence.

The Alpha's fated mate is me.

chapters.title: The Words
chapters.body:
For one heartbeat — one — I let myself believe it.

The bond hums between us, bright and certain, and it rewrites everything. Eighteen years at the back of the dark, and the moon looked at all of them and chose *me*. Not the warriors' daughters. Not Cassia in her silver gown. Me, the stray's girl, the pair of hands that sets down a plate.

Something in my chest that's been clenched since before I had words for it begins, very carefully, to open.

Kane comes down off the dais.

He doesn't walk so much as descend, the crowd splitting before him like water, and every step closes the distance the bond is screaming to close. I can see him properly now — the pulse going hard in his throat, the disbelief in those grey eyes warring with something rawer underneath, something that looks almost like *hope* and that, more than anything, undoes me. Because if he feels it too, then it's real. Then I didn't imagine being wanted.

"What's your name?" His voice isn't the one that rolled over the clearing. It's lower. Just for me.

"Wren." It comes out a whisper. "Wren Calloway."

His jaw tightens at *Calloway*. Of course he knows the name. Everyone knows the name — the stray who came out of the woods seventeen years ago, half-dead, with a baby and no pack, and died of it within the year. The shame of the kitchens. He knows exactly what I am, and the bond is making him want me anyway, and I watch that knowledge move across his face like a shadow over the moon.

"The kitchens," he says.

"Yes."

We're an arm's length apart. This close, the pull is almost unbearable, a physical ache to close the last of the distance, to put my hand against his chest and feel the thing the moon has welded between us. His hand lifts. Just slightly. Towards me.

And then Cassia's voice cuts across the clearing like a blade drawn over stone.

"*Kane.*"

He stops.

I see it happen. I see the war end and the wrong side win. The hope shutters out of his eyes the way a door closes, and the cold counting-weapons stranger I've served dinner to for years comes back into his face, and he straightens to his full height, and he is no longer a man the moon has struck. He's the Alpha of Blackthorn, with a war on the horizon and two hundred wolves watching, and a kitchen omega in front of him who is, suddenly, a problem to be solved.

"There's a war coming," he says, and he's not saying it to me. He's saying it to the clearing. To his pack. To the alliance that keeps the Ravenscar wolves out of his throat. "Blackthorn cannot stand on a weak Luna."

The opening thing in my chest stops opening.

"Alpha," I say. I don't know what I'm asking for. Mercy, maybe. A minute. "The moon —"

"The moon doesn't fight our wars." His eyes come back to me, and they're flat now, deliberate, and the cruelty in them is the worst kind — the kind that's chosen, that knows exactly what it's about to cost and has decided the cost is acceptable. "Forgive me," he says, very quietly, so quietly I almost think it's for me.

Then he lifts his voice for all of them to hear.

"I, Kane Blackthorn, Alpha of this pack —"

*No.* The word claws up my throat and won't come out.

"— reject you, Wren Calloway —"

The clearing tilts.

"— as my mate."

"— and as my Luna."

The bond does not fade. I had imagined, in the half-second I had to imagine anything, that it would simply go quiet, that the moon would take back what she gave and leave me as empty as I'd always been.

It doesn't go quiet. It *tears*.

It's not like anything has a right to feel. It's a hook buried behind my ribs and a hand on the other end ripping it out by the roots, except the roots are wound through everything — my lungs, my spine, the place behind my eyes, the marrow of me — and all of it comes up at once, white and screaming. The clearing goes away. The firelight goes away. There is only the tearing, and the sound that comes out of me, which I don't recognize as my own voice until my knees hit the dirt and I realize I'm on the ground and the scream is still going.

I claw at my own chest. There's nothing there to claw at. The wound is inside, and it's vast, and it's killing me, and somewhere very far above me a man with storm-grey eyes is watching the thing he's done with a face like a closing door.

Cassia is smiling.

The pines spin overhead, black against the pitiless moon. Voices wash over me, some of them horrified, most of them already turning away — *an omega, only an omega, the Alpha chose right* — and through the white roar of the Severing I feel the last warm thing I owned guttering out like a candle in a flood.

I had it for one heartbeat. The whole world, threaded to my chest.

And then he said the words, and took it back, and left me screaming in the dirt at the edge of the firelight where nothing is ever supposed to move.

chapters.title: What the Moon Kept
chapters.body:
They don't kill rejected omegas. They don't have to. The Severing does it for them.

I learn this the way I've learned everything — too late, and from someone who enjoys telling me. Mara crouches over me where I've curled in the dirt, close enough that I can smell the wine on her breath, and she says it almost gently.

"You won't last the night, mouse. A bond torn out by the root — there's no living through that. Body forgets how to keep going." She tucks a strand of hair off my sweating face, tender as a mother, and her eyes are bright with the pleasure of it. "Best you go off into the trees and be quiet about it. Nobody wants a corpse in the Choosing clearing."

Two warriors haul me up under the arms. I can't make my legs work. The wound inside me has spread to everything, a cold that isn't temperature, a wrongness in the marrow, my own body unlearning the trick of staying alive. They drag me past the fire. Past the pack that doesn't look. Past the dais of black stone where Cassia has already taken her place at the Alpha's side, her hand sliding back onto his arm, the silver gown catching the firelight like she's the one the moon chose.

Kane is watching me go.

His face hasn't changed — closed door, cold stone — but his hand is white-knuckled on the black rock, and his eyes track me across the whole clearing the way they did when the bond first landed, helpless, as if some part of him is being dragged out with me and he is holding on to the stone to keep from following.

I hold his stare until the trees swallow the firelight. I want him to remember it. If this is what dying is, I want my face to be the last thing in his.

Then the warriors drop me at the treeline and walk away, and I'm alone in the dark with the moon and the cold that's eating me from the inside.

I crawl.

I don't decide to. My body just refuses to stop, some animal stubbornness hauling me deeper into the pines on hands and knees, away from the pack, away from the smell of the fire, until the clearing is a glow behind me and then not even that. Roots tear my borrowed dress. Frost bites my palms. The wound in my chest pulses with every heartbeat, and each beat is a little slower than the last, and I can feel — actually *feel* — the spaces opening between them, the long cold pauses where my heart is forgetting why it bothers.

I get as far as a clearing where the canopy breaks, and the full moon pours down white and enormous, and there I stop, because my arms won't hold me anymore.

I roll onto my back in the frost and look up at her.

"You chose me," I tell the moon. My voice is a wreck. "You reached past every wolf in that clearing and you put your hook in *me*, and then you let him —" The cold swallows the rest. I drag in a breath that rattles. "Why pick me at all, if this is what it was for. Why let me feel it. Why make me want —"

I can't finish that one either.

The moon doesn't answer. The moon never answers. I've prayed to her my whole life — for a mother, for a name, for one single person in the world to look at me and want me to stay — and she has been, every single time, a beautiful cold light that does not care.

My heart skips. Skips again. The pauses are winning.

So I stop asking her for anything. I just look at her, this last cruel beautiful thing, and I feel the cold close over my chin, my mouth, the edges of my sight going soft and dark, and I think, with what's left of me: *fine. Fine. Take it back. I never got to keep anything anyway.*

And the scar on my left palm splits open with light.

It doesn't hurt. That's the first strange thing. The old white line across my palm — the one I've had since before memory, the one my mother's note said was an oath though it never said to whom — cracks like a seam in a curtain, and silver light pours out of it, the exact white-silver of the moon overhead, and it does not hurt at all. It feels like a held breath finally let go.

The light runs up my arm in veins.

The cold stops winning.

I feel it happen — feel the wound in my chest, the vast torn place where the bond used to be, fill with something that is not the bond and is older than the bond, something that has been folded up small and asleep inside me my whole life and is now, slowly, beginning to *unfold*. My heart doesn't skip. It pounds. It pounds like it's making up for eighteen years of beating quietly at the back of the dark.

And in the white roar of it, I hear a voice.

It isn't in the clearing. It's in the light, in the marrow, in the place the unfolding thing is waking. A woman's voice, ancient and amused and terrible and somehow, impossibly, *fond* — the way you'd speak to a child you'd been waiting a very long time to meet.

*There you are,* the voice says. *There you are, little queen.*

The silver streak ignites in my hair. I can see it from the corner of my eye, a lock of dark gone white-bright as moonfire, and I can feel the something-enormous rising in me like a tide turning, like a door I never knew was in me swinging slowly open onto a room the size of the sky.

*Eighteen years they kept you in the dark,* the voice murmurs, and there is love in it, and there is the promise of violence in it, and the two are not separate things. *They put my blood in the kitchens. They let that boy say the words.* A pause, soft as a blade going in. *They should not have done that.*

Across the territory, in the Choosing clearing, on the dais of black stone with the alliance bride's hand on his arm —

Kane Blackthorn doubles over.

He doesn't know why. He'll tell himself, later, that it was nothing, a stumble, the wine. But the bond he tore out by the root and watched bleed into the dark an hour ago — the bond that should be dead, that is *always* dead, that no rejected wolf has ever felt again —

— roars back to life.

It slams into him so hard he goes to one knee on the black stone in front of his entire pack, his hand flying to his chest, his face going white as the moon, because the thing he killed is not dead, the thing he threw away is *waking*, and it is vast, and it is furious, and it is calling a name down a bond that should not exist —

He hears it in his own bones, in the voice of a stranger and the voice of the omega both, ringing through the torn place inside him:

*Wren.*

And in the moonlit clearing where I am dying and not dying, where the silver climbs my veins and the ancient fond terrible voice settles into me like it's coming home, I open my eyes.

They are not grey anymore.

chapters.title: The Wolves Who Knelt
chapters.body:
I come back to the world in pieces.

A piece: cold pine needles against my cheek, and the silver light gone, and the moon lower in the sky.

A piece: voices, low and male, and the sense of bodies moving around me in the dark — wolves, the smell of them strange, not Blackthorn, not anyone I know.

A piece: hands turning me over, careful, and a face above mine I've never seen. A man, maybe mid-twenties, with a close dark beard and amber eyes that catch the moonlight like a wolf's even in his human skin. There's a stillness to him that the warriors back home never had. Warriors are always braced to prove something. This man looks like he proved it a long time ago and got bored.

His gaze moves over me — the torn dress, the bond-wound he can't see but somehow seems to *smell*, the way I'm breathing when by every law of the moon I shouldn't be breathing at all. Then it stops on my left hand, fallen open against the frost.

The scar. The old white line, still faintly lit, a thread of silver cooling in my palm.

The man goes very still.

"Alpha." One of the others, hushed. "She's a rogue. No pack-scent on her — well, torn pack-scent. She's been Severed, look at her. She's dying."

"She's not dying." The man — the Alpha — hasn't taken his eyes off my palm. "She was dying. Now she's not." He reaches out and, with two fingers, turns my hand fully toward the moonlight, and whatever he sees there makes the breath leave him slow and careful, the way you'd breathe near something that might go off. "Goddess," he says, almost to himself. "It's real."

"What's real?"

He doesn't answer right away. When he finally looks up at his wolves, his face has changed — the boredom gone, replaced by something I'll come to know very well in the days ahead, though I don't have a name for it yet. It's the look of a man recalculating his entire life around a thing he didn't believe in until ten seconds ago.

"What do you know about the Moonborn?" he asks them.

A pause. Then the youngest, uneasy: "Bedtime stories. The first wolves. The Luna-blood that could call the moon down out of the sky." A nervous laugh. "They've been dead a thousand years, Rourke. Burned out of every line on purpose. There's no —"

"There's one." Rourke stands, and he's tall, and the moon throws his shadow long across me. "There's one, and she's lying Severed in my territory with the mark on her hand and her heart restarting itself, and half an hour ago every wolf from here to the coast felt something wake up." He says it flat, like a man reading a sentence off a page. "You know what she is. You know what she's worth. There are packs that would burn the whole Crescent to the ground to put a leash on a Moonborn. And there are older things than packs that would do far worse to make sure she never wakes all the way up."

The wolves shift in the dark. I feel their fear — and under it, sharper, their *want*. I know that smell. It's the smell of the kitchens deciding whether to be kind or cruel.

Rourke turns. Looks down at me — and I realize, dimly, through the fog, that my eyes are open, that I've been watching him this whole time, and that he knows it.

"You can hear me," he says. Not a question.

I can't make my mouth work. I manage the smallest movement of my chin. *Yes.*

Something flickers across his face that might be respect. Then he crouches again, close, and lowers his voice so it's just for me, the way Kane's had been just for me an hour and a lifetime ago.

"Then hear this," Rourke says. "You're safe tonight. You have my word, and in Nightriver my word holds. But the moment you can stand, you and I are going to have a very honest conversation about what you are and who is going to find out — because right now you are the most dangerous thing in these mountains, little ghost, and you don't even know it."

He straightens. And then the Alpha of Nightriver does something I have never in my life seen a wolf do for anyone, let alone for a Severed omega bleeding in the frost.

He drops to one knee.

"Down," he tells his wolves. "All of you. Now."

And one by one, with the moon white above us and the disbelief plain on their faces, the warriors of Nightriver kneel in the pine needles around me — around the kitchen mouse, the stray's girl, the pair of hands that sets down a plate.

"Half the world would kill to own her," Rourke says quietly, to them, to the trees, to the moon. "The other half already thinks she's dead." His amber eyes find mine one last time before the dark takes me back under. "Let's keep it that way."

chapters.title: Nightriver
chapters.body:
I wake in a bed for the first time in my life.

Not a pallet on the kitchen floor. Not a borrowed corner. A real bed, wide and soft, in a room with stone walls and a window full of grey morning and a fire someone has kept burning while I slept. For a long moment I just lie there, rigid, certain it's a mistake — that any second now Mara will throw the door open and ask what I think I'm doing in the family quarters.

Then I remember the clearing. The words. The tearing.

The light.

I sit up so fast the room tilts. My hand flies to my chest — and there's no wound, no blood, nothing but the fading ache of something enormous that has settled back into sleep. I drag the collar of the unfamiliar shirt I'm wearing aside and there's only skin. Whole. Unmarked.

Severed wolves don't survive. Mara said it like a fact, because it is one. I have served at three funerals for rejected omegas in my life, watched their bodies carried out before dawn, the bond-wound having quietly stopped their hearts in the night.

Mine didn't stop. Mine restarted.

I get up. My legs hold. I find a basin of water and a square of polished metal hung on the wall for a mirror, and I look at myself, and I go still.

There's a streak of silver in my hair.

It runs from my left temple back, one clean lock gone the white of moonlight, bright against the dark. I touch it. It's just hair. But when I lean close to the metal and meet my own eyes, for half a second — just half — the grey of them swims and brightens, and a thread of that same silver moves through the iris like light under water, and then it's gone, and they're only my eyes again.

*There you are, little queen,* the voice said.

"What are you," I whisper to the girl in the metal.

She doesn't answer either.

The door opens behind me. I spin, my back to the wall, the old kitchen instinct — *make yourself small, the small ones get hit less* — except my body doesn't make itself small. It squares up. Something coils low in my spine, ready, and I feel a flush of heat behind my eyes that I have to consciously push back down.

Rourke stands in the doorway with a tray. He notes my stance, the heat I'm swallowing, the silver streak, all of it in one amber-eyed sweep, and he doesn't comment on any of it.

"You slept two days," he says, setting the tray on the table. Bread, broth, an apple. More food than I'd see in a week at home. "We weren't sure you'd wake. Maeve was sure you would. Maeve is usually right, which is annoying." He pulls out a chair, sits, and gestures at the food. "Eat. We'll talk after."

I don't move. "Why."

"Why eat? Because you're —"

"Why any of it." My voice comes out harder than I mean it to. Two days of safety has done nothing to soften the thing the clearing put in me. "I'm a Severed omega from a rival pack. By every law you have, you should've left me in the frost or sold me back to Blackthorn for a war you don't have to fight. Instead I'm in a bed, in your house, and your warriors knelt to me in the dirt." I lift my chin. "So. Why."

Rourke studies me for a long moment. Then, unexpectedly, he smiles — and it changes his whole face, takes the boredom and the calculation out of it, and underneath there's someone younger and warier than the Alpha who knelt in the pines.

"Because I've spent my whole life being told the Moonborn were a story," he says. "A thing the old packs killed to keep power from concentrating in one bloodline. And two nights ago the story crawled into my territory and restarted her own heart under a full moon, and I would very much like to be on the right side of that when the rest of the world catches up." He pushes the bread toward me. "That honest enough?"

It is. That's the strange part. After eighteen years of Mara's gentleness-with-a-blade-in-it, an Alpha telling me plainly that he wants to use me is almost a kindness.

I sit. I eat. The broth is the best thing I've ever tasted, and I hate how much that says about my life.

"Tell me what a Moonborn is," I say around the bread. "All of it. No bedtime stories."

"I can't." Rourke leans back. "I only know the shape of it. The blood, the silver, the moon. The danger." Something shadows his face. "For the rest, you'll want the one who came down out of the high hollows the morning we brought you in, the moment she felt you wake — who's been pacing my hall for two days waiting to see if the last Moonborn in the world was going to open her eyes."

A floorboard creaks in the corridor.

An old woman stands in the doorway — small, grey-braided, with eyes the pale gold of a winter moon and a stillness even deeper than Rourke's. She looks at me. At the streak in my hair, the heat behind my eyes, the whole impossible fact of me sitting alive at a table.

And the colour drains out of her weathered face, and she reaches for the doorframe to steady herself, and she says, in a voice gone thin with dread:

"Goddess keep us. She's woken." Her pale eyes find mine and hold them, and there's no wonder in them — only fear. "Child. Do you understand what your waking *means*? The seal only ever held while the Moonborn slept." Her knuckles whiten on the wood. "You opening your eyes is the first crack in a door that has been shut for a thousand years."

chapters.title: The Crack in the Door
chapters.body:
Maeve makes me tell it three times.

The Choosing. The pull. The words. The dirt, the cold, the moon, the light pouring out of the scar on my palm. The voice. Each time I get to the voice she goes stiller, and the third time, when I repeat what it called me — *little queen* — she closes her eyes like the words hurt to hear.

"Tell me exactly what else it said," she murmurs.

"That they kept me in the dark. That they put her blood in the kitchens." My throat tightens around the next part. "That they let that boy say the words. And that — they should not have done that."

"*Her* blood." Maeve's pale eyes open. "Not *your* blood. Her."

I hadn't noticed it. Now that she says it, the wrongness of it crawls up my spine. "I don't — it's just a voice. A dream. I was dying."

"You were dying, and the moon answered, and the thing that answered called your blood *hers*." Maeve lowers herself into the chair across from me with the care of an old woman and the gravity of a priestess. "Listen to me, child, because no one alive but me can tell you this, and I am old, and I will not always be here to tell it."

The fire pops. Rourke leans against the wall by the door, arms crossed, listening as hard as I am.

"A thousand years ago," Maeve says, "there was one bloodline that the moon loved above all others. The Moonborn. They could call her light down into the world — heal with it, fight with it, bind with it. They were the first Lunas, and the packs were stronger for them, and the world was, for a while, a kinder place." Her mouth thins. "And then the first Moonborn — the eldest, the strongest, the one they called the Silver Queen — decided that a power that could heal could also rule, and that wolves who would not kneel could be *made* to. She turned the moon's gift into a leash. By the end she wasn't calling the light down. She was calling something else — something from the dark side of the moon, where the light doesn't reach."

The fire throws shadows up the stone, and for a moment I'd swear they lean toward me.

"It took every pack in the world to stop her," Maeve says. "They couldn't kill her — Moonborn blood doesn't die easy, as you've learned. So they *sealed* her. Bound her into the dark she'd reached for, and bound the seal to the bloodline itself — so that the seal would hold as long as a Moonborn slept, unwoken, the power dormant in the blood." Her gold eyes fix on mine. "They spent the next thousand years making sure none ever woke. They hunted the line. Scattered it. Bred the silver out of it where they could, drowned it where they couldn't. Put the last of it, it seems —"

"— in the kitchens of Blackthorn," I finish. The cold is back, but it's a different cold now. "Where no one would ever look. Where no one would ever choose me. Where the power would stay asleep."

"Until the moon made one mistake," Maeve says softly. "She threaded a bond to your chest in front of two hundred witnesses. And a frightened boy tore it out. And a Moonborn dying of a torn bond reached for the only thing strong enough to save her —" She spreads her weathered hands. "— and woke."

Silence. The enormous thing in my chest stirs, just slightly, like it knows it's being discussed.

"So I'm her," I say. My voice doesn't sound like mine. "The Silver Queen. That's whose voice —"

"No." Maeve says it hard and fast. "You are *Wren*. You are the door, child, not the thing behind it. The seal is in your blood, and as you wake, the seal weakens, and *she* — the Queen, the thing in the dark — gets a little louder. That's all the voice is. For now." A pause I don't like. "For now."

I look down at my left palm. At the old white scar that split with light. *An oath,* my mother's note had said, *though it never said to whom.*

Now I think I know to whom.

"Can it be closed again?" I ask. "The seal. Can I — put it back to sleep?"

Maeve and Rourke exchange a look I'm not meant to catch.

"That," Maeve says carefully, "is a conversation for when you're stronger. There are ways. None of them are kind." She rises, joints cracking. "What matters tonight is that you are awake, and the door is open a crack, and you are not the only one who felt it. The dark side of the moon felt it. And so did every wolf with the wit to know what waking means." She moves toward the door. "We have a little time. Days, perhaps. We must not waste —"

A horn sounds.

It's far off, down the mountain, at the edge of Nightriver land — a single long note, then two short, and Rourke is off the wall and moving before it finishes, his face gone hard.

"Border," he says.

A young wolf appears at the door, breathless, and the words tumble out of him in a rush that stops my heart:

"Alpha — riders at the south line. Blackthorn colours. They're not attacking, they say — they say they've come to *talk*." He swallows. "Their Alpha sent them. He says he felt something two nights ago. Something that's his." The boy's eyes flick to me, and away. "He says he's not leaving until we hand over the rogue female."

Down in my chest, the bond I thought was dead gives a single, traitorous pull — south, toward the border, toward *him* — and I understand, with a sick lurch, that Kane Blackthorn already knows I'm alive.

chapters.title: The Pull South
chapters.body:
The bond is a leash, and someone is pulling it.

I stand at the window of my room with both hands flat on the cold stone sill and fight it. South. Always south. Three days since the border riders came, and Rourke turned them away with words smooth as river stone — *we harbour no Blackthorn wolf; search where you like; you'll find no one* — and they went, because they had no proof, only their Alpha's certainty.

But the certainty was right. And the bond knows it. Every hour it tugs at me, low and insistent, toward the man who tore it out and left me in the dirt, and the worst part — the part I'd cut out of myself if I could find it — is that some animal piece of me *wants* to go. Wants to walk down the mountain and find him and put my hand against his chest and finish the thing the moon started.

I press my forehead to the stone and breathe until the want goes quiet.

"It pulls at you." Rourke's voice, from the doorway. He's stopped knocking; we're past that. "I can see it. You go to the south window without thinking."

"It's nothing." I don't turn around. "It's dead. He killed it."

"It's not dead, Wren. I've never heard of a bond surviving a Severing, and yours did, same as you did. Maeve thinks the Moonborn blood won't let it die." A pause. "She thinks that's a problem. A bond is a road that runs both ways. He can feel you down it the same as you feel him." He lets that land. "Which is how he knew to send riders. And how he'll keep knowing."

I turn around then. "Then teach me to close it."

"You can't close a bond. You can only —" He searches for the word. "— get strong enough that it stops being the loudest thing in you." He leans in the doorway, amber eyes steady. "Which is the other reason I came up. Maeve's ready. She wants to start tomorrow, at the dark of the moon, in the high hollow. Training." A thin smile. "Learning to hold what's in you before it decides to use you instead of the other way around."

I should feel afraid. I felt the power surge in the clearing; I felt it nearly take me. Instead, standing at the window with the bond pulling me toward the man who threw me away, I feel something I have no practice with at all.

I feel *hungry* for it.

"Good," I say.

Rourke's brows lift a fraction. He pushes off the doorframe, crosses to the small polished-metal mirror on the wall, and tilts it toward me so I have to look. "Before we start, though, I want you to see something. So you know what we're working with."

I look.

The silver streak. The grey eyes. The girl who set down plates.

"Now get angry," Rourke says quietly. "Think about the clearing. The words. Her hand on his arm while you screamed in the dirt."

I don't want to. And then I do, because he's right that I need to see it, and because the truth is the anger is never far — it lives just under my ribs now, banked like a fire that's only waiting for air. I think about Cassia's smile. I think about Mara tucking the hair off my face and telling me to go die quietly in the trees. I think about Kane's white-knuckled hand on the black stone, watching me dragged away, *choosing* it.

The girl in the mirror changes.

It comes up behind my eyes like the tide I felt in the clearing — the grey flooding out, the silver flooding in, until both irises burn moon-white and the streak in my hair seems to catch a light that isn't in the room. My reflection looks back at me, and she is not a mouse, and she is not small, and she is not anyone's pair of hands.

She looks like something the dark should be afraid of.

I hold it for three breaths. Then I let it go, and the silver drains, and I'm just Wren again, gripping the windowsill, heart pounding.

"There she is," Rourke murmurs — and the echo of the voice in the clearing puts ice down my spine, though he can't know that.

I meet his eyes in the metal. And I make myself a promise, the first real one of my life — not to the moon, who doesn't listen, but to the silver-eyed girl in the mirror who finally does.

"The omega died in that clearing," I tell her. "Let them think she's dead. Let *him* think it." The silver flickers up behind my eyes again, just for a second, and this time I don't push it down. "I'm going to come back the Luna they were all too afraid to let me be. And before this is over —"

In the metal, the silver burns.

"— I'm going to make Kane Blackthorn kneel."

chapters.title: Silver
chapters.body:
I have never shifted.

Every wolf shifts at puberty — it's supposed to come like a fever, painful and inevitable, your bones remembering a shape older than language. Mine never came. The kitchens whispered it was proof of what I was: half a wolf, a stray's mistake, too thin a blood to carry the change. Mara liked to say I'd die in one skin like the human my mother must have rutted to make me.

Now, standing barefoot in the high hollow under a sky scrubbed of moon, Maeve tells me the truth was the opposite all along.

"It didn't come because they bound it," she says. "The same binding that kept the power asleep kept the wolf asleep. They couldn't have a Moonborn shifting in the kitchens and showing the whole pack what she was." She circles me slowly. "The bond tearing cracked the binding. The wolf's been waking ever since, same as the rest of you. She's close to the surface now. I can smell her." A dry look. "You've been growling in your sleep."

"How do I —"

"You don't *do* anything." Maeve stops in front of me. "That's the lesson, child, and you'll spend your life learning it. The power isn't a tool you pick up. It's a tide. You don't push a tide. You learn its pull, and you point it." She steps back, out of my reach, and her pale eyes go flat and hard. "So. Let's find the pull. Get angry."

"Rourke already —"

"Rourke wanted you to *see* it." Maeve's voice cracks like a whip. "I want you to *become* it. There's a difference, and the difference is whether you survive what's coming." She lifts her chin, and there's a cruelty in it now, deliberate, the kind I know in my bones. "Your mother. Tell me how she died."

"Sick. In the first winter. She —"

"She didn't die sick." Maeve says it flat, brutal, a stone dropped in still water. "I knew Saoirse Calloway. I knew her before she ran to Blackthorn with a baby and a secret. She wasn't sick, girl. She was *hunted*. And the cold little fact you've never let yourself look at —" the old woman leans in "— is that she could have run anywhere in the world, and she chose to die in the one pack whose Alpha would later look his fated mate in the eye and throw her in the dirt to please a Ravenscar."

The tide turns.

It doesn't come up behind my eyes this time — it comes up *everywhere*, a roar in the blood, the banked fire under my ribs finding air at last. The hollow goes silver at the edges. My fingers curl and there's pain in them, glorious and clean, the bones remembering, and I drop to all fours in the frost without deciding to and the change takes me the way the Severing took me — all at once, white and total — except this time it isn't something being ripped out.

It's something coming home.

When the silver clears I'm standing on four legs, and the world is louder, and I am enormous.

I can see myself in the still black water of the hollow's pool. I am not a grey wolf, or a brown one, or the dark of the Blackthorn line. I am silver — the whole of me, moon-pale and shining, a coat that throws back the starlight like fresh snow, and my eyes in the water burn that same impossible white. I am bigger than Rourke's warriors. I am bigger than Kane.

Behind me, at the hollow's edge, I hear the Nightriver wolves who came to guard the training go absolutely silent. Then, one by one, the soft sounds of them lowering to the ground. Awe. And under it, the old smell, the one that follows me everywhere now — fear.

*Beautiful,* Maeve breathes, and for the first time there's no cruelty in her, only a kind of grief. *Goddess help us all, you're beautiful. Just like the stories.*

I lift my head and I want to howl — the new throat aching with it, the joy and the rage and the sheer *size* of being finally, fully myself —

— and that's when I catch the scent on the night wind. Faint. Downwind, at the treeline. Not Nightriver. Not Blackthorn.

Something colder. Something that smells of old blood and the dark side of the moon, and of a hunger that has been waiting a very long time.

I whip around, silver hackles rising. At the black edge of the trees, two eyes catch my shine for half a heartbeat — wide, fixed on me, drinking in the sight of a silver wolf where no silver wolf has walked in a thousand years.

Then they're gone, crashing away downhill, fast, *reporting* —

and a voice that is not Maeve's and not Rourke's slides cold and delighted through the back of my waking mind:

*Found you.*

chapters.title: What My Mother Knew
chapters.body:
The scout changes everything.

"Shadowblood." Maeve says the word like she's spitting out a stone. We're back in Rourke's hall before dawn, the fire built high, and the old woman has aged ten years since the hollow. "Rogues. The ones who never accepted the sealing — who've spent a thousand years waiting for a Moonborn to wake so they can use her blood to break it the rest of the way." Her gold eyes are grim. "They've been a ghost story longer than the Moonborn have. If one walked your treeline tonight, then the door cracking open hasn't just woken *her*." She nods at me. "It's woken them."

"Then we move her." Rourke is already pacing, Alpha-mind running. "Deeper into the high country. The old strongholds —"

"Running won't help." Maeve sinks into her chair. "They have her scent now. And worse — they have time, and they have patience, and they have something we don't." She looks at me. "They want her to keep waking. Every day she's stronger, the seal's weaker, and the nearer they come to what they've waited a thousand years for. We need her strong enough to fight them — and the stronger she gets, the more she gives them what they want." A bitter sound. "It's a trap with no floor."

I'm barely listening. One word has lodged in me and won't come loose.

"You said my mother was hunted." I cut across them both, and something in my voice makes them stop. "In the hollow. You said she chose Blackthorn. You knew her." I stand. "You're going to tell me the rest. Now."

Maeve is quiet for a long moment. Then she reaches into her robe and takes out something small, wrapped in oilcloth, worn soft with handling, and sets it on the table between us.

It's a pendant. A thin disc of silver, a crescent moon worked into it, the cord frayed and old.

"She gave me this the last time I saw her," Maeve says quietly. "Eight months before she died. She came to me in secret — I kept the lore even then, in the low hollows — and she told me she'd found a pack that would take her in, where no one would think to look for the silver, because the silver was the last thing anyone would expect in a kitchen." Her hands fold over the pendant. "She knew what you were, child. She'd known since you were born under that blood moon. She bound your power herself, with the last of hers, to keep you asleep and safe. And then she ran to the one place hidden enough to hide a sleeping queen." Maeve's voice drops. "She didn't die of winter. She died because someone found her anyway. Someone who knew exactly what she'd hidden in those kitchens."

The fire pops. The cold in me has gone very still and very deep.

"Who," I say.

"That, I don't know. She didn't live long enough to tell me, and I wasn't there." Maeve slides the pendant across the table until it touches my hand. "But I know this. A stray gets killed by wolves or weather. Your mother was killed by *knowledge*. Someone in or near Blackthorn knew the Moonborn line wasn't dead — knew it years ago — and chose to bury the truth in a kitchen girl's grave rather than let it wake." Her pale eyes hold mine. "The same pack, child. The same pack whose Alpha threw you in the dirt. Whatever rejected you that night, it was older than Kane Blackthorn's cowardice. It's been deciding your life since before you were born."

I close my hand around the pendant. The silver is cold, then warm, then — faintly, just for a breath — it *sings*, a thread of moonlight running up my wrist to meet the scar in my palm like two halves of something finding each other.

I think about the kitchens. The funerals before dawn. Mara's gentle, gloating hands. Eighteen years of being told I was nothing, in a pack that knew — that *someone* in it knew — exactly what I was, and needed me to believe I was nothing so that I'd stay asleep.

The anger that takes me now isn't the hot, clean fire of the hollow. It's colder than that. It's patient. It feels, horribly, like coming into an inheritance.

"Teach me everything," I tell Maeve, and my voice has gone quiet in the way that used to be armor and is, now, something else entirely. "Faster. All of it. I don't care how unkind."

"Wren —" Rourke starts.

"Someone in that pack killed my mother to keep me asleep." I rise, the pendant fisted at my chest, and I feel the silver flicker up behind my eyes and I let it stay. "So I'm going to wake all the way up. And then I'm going to go back and find out who." I look between them, the old keeper of lore and the Alpha who knelt in the pines. "And the moon help every single one of them who watched me crawl into those trees and decided I was already dead."

chapters.title: The Road That Runs Both Ways
chapters.body:
The bond shows me things now.

Maeve was right — it's a road, and it runs both ways, and as I get stronger the road gets clearer, until some nights I close my eyes in my room in Nightriver and open them somewhere else entirely. Not my body. Just my attention, riding the broken bond south, into the heart of the pack that threw me away, into the mind of the man on the other end of it.

I don't choose it. It happens when the moon is high and my guard is down. And tonight, three weeks into my waking, it takes me all the way into Kane Blackthorn's study.

He's not sleeping. He hasn't slept, I think, in a long time — I can feel it down the bond, the grey exhaustion of him, the way the rejected bond gnaws at him too, a wound that won't close because the thing on the other end of it refuses to die. He stands at his window with a glass of something amber he isn't drinking, staring out at the dark line of the mountains. *My* mountains, now. Nightriver's. He doesn't know I'm watching from behind his own eyes.

"You'll wear a track in the floor." Cassia. She's draped in the doorway in something pale, and even through Kane's tired senses I can feel how little he wants her there. "Come to bed, Kane. The alliance is sealed. The pack is yours. The little kitchen problem walked off into the woods a month ago and died like they all do." A pause, edged. "Didn't she?"

"She's not dead." Kane's voice is rough, like it's been a long time since he used it for anything but orders.

"Of course she's —"

"I would feel it if she were dead." He turns from the window, and through the bond I feel the precise weight of what's on his face — not longing, he won't let it be longing, but a haunted, furious helplessness. "A Severed bond goes silent. Mine didn't. It —" He stops himself. Drinks, finally, deep. "It doesn't matter."

"It clearly matters, since you can't stop pacing over a *kitchen girl*." Cassia's beauty curdles when she's threatened; I've seen it. "You rejected her. In front of everyone. You chose correctly — you chose *me*, you chose Blackthorn, you chose not to put a weakling stray on the Luna's seat with a war coming." Her voice climbs. "She is nothing, Kane. She was always nothing. Whatever you think you feel is just guilt, and guilt fades."

For a moment Kane says nothing. Then he sets the glass down with a click that makes her flinch, and when he speaks his voice has gone quiet and terrible, the Alpha voice, the one the clearing leaned into.

"I felt her wake."

Cassia goes still.

"Three weeks ago. The middle of the night." He presses a hand flat against his own chest, over the bond, and through it I feel the ghost of that night slam back into him — the bond roaring back to life, the name ringing through his bones, the way he went to one knee on the black stone in front of his pack. "Whatever I rejected in that clearing, it wasn't nothing. *Nothing* doesn't drop an Alpha to his knees from forty miles away. *Nothing* doesn't wake up." His grey eyes are fixed on the dark, on the mountains, almost on *me*. "I made a calculation that night, with the whole pack watching and your father's riders at my border. I weighed a frightened kitchen girl against a war." His jaw works. "I'm beginning to think I weighed it wrong. And I'm beginning to think she's going to make me pay for it in ways I can't yet imagine."

"Kane —"

"Find her," he says, not to Cassia, to the dark, to the betas he'll wake at dawn. "I don't care what it costs. I will burn every territory in the Crescent to the ground before I let her stay lost." He says it like a vow and like a grief. "I have to know what I threw away."

Cassia's face, behind him, where he can't see it, does something I will remember for a long time. The fear leaves it. Something colder moves in. A decision.

She slips out of the study without another word, and through the fading bond I follow her, just for a heartbeat, just long enough to see her stop in the dark corridor and press a small folded paper into the hand of a wolf I don't know — a lean, grey-eyed man who shouldn't be in Blackthorn's halls at all, whose scent, even secondhand, even through a dying bond, raises every silver hair on the back of my waking mind.

Old blood. The dark side of the moon.

*Shadowblood.*

And Cassia Ravenscar, future Luna of Blackthorn, leans in close to the rogue's ear and murmurs something I can't hear — but I catch the shape of the last word on her lips, and the shape is *her*, and the smile she gives as the rogue melts back into the dark is the same smile she wore while I screamed in the dirt.

I snap back into my own body in Nightriver, gasping, the pendant burning cold against my chest.

Kane is hunting me to atone.

Cassia is hunting me to win.

And she's just made a deal with the one thing in the world that wants me more than either of them.

chapters.title: Worth Staying For
chapters.body:
The skirmish comes at the grey edge of morning, three days later, and it is not Shadowblood — it's worse, in a way, because it's stupid.

A Blackthorn scouting party, six wolves, pushing too deep into Nightriver land looking for the rogue female their Alpha won't stop hunting. They run into a Nightriver patrol on the north ridge, and pride does what pride does, and by the time the horns wake me the two packs are tearing into each other across a frozen slope over a girl neither side has the sense to know is standing right there.

I don't think. I run.

I've been training for weeks — the tide, the pull, the silver wolf I can now call up in three heartbeats instead of dying to find — but I've never used it on anything but Maeve's drills and Rourke's sparring. By the time I crest the ridge in silver, huge and shining in the dawn, the fight has already cost blood. Two Nightriver wolves are down. A young one, barely shifted, is pinned screaming under a Blackthorn warrior twice his size.

The warrior looks up and sees a silver wolf coming down the slope like an avalanche, and I watch the exact moment he understands what he's looking at — watch the rumour become real in his eyes — and then I'm on him, and I don't kill him, because Maeve's voice is in my head (*point the tide, don't drown in it*), but I take him off the boy and pin him in the snow with one paw on his throat and I let him feel the size of me, the wrongness of me, the moon-cold pouring off my coat.

I could end him. He knows it. Every wolf on the slope knows it; the fighting has stopped, both packs frozen, staring at the thing that just walked out of their bedtime stories.

I don't end him. I lean down until my white eyes fill his vision and I let a single thought ride down the air between us — I don't know how I do it, only that the tide does it for me — *Tell your Alpha what you saw. Tell him the silver isn't dead.*

Then I step back and let him run.

They all run, the Blackthorn wolves, scrambling back over the ridge with their wounded, and the Nightriver patrol stares at me as I shift back to skin in the trampled snow, shaking, the boy I saved gaping up at me like I hung the moon I'm named for.

Rourke finds me there a minute later, having run flat-out from the hall. He takes in the downed wolves, the Blackthorn blood, the boy alive, me on my knees in the snow with steam coming off my shoulders — and something in his face I've never seen breaks open.

He crosses the snow and crouches in front of me, and he doesn't ask if I'm hurt, and he doesn't scold me for running into a fight, and he doesn't do any of the things an Alpha does.

He just looks at me for a long moment, amber eyes searching mine, and then he says, very quietly, like the words are being pulled out of him against his better judgment:

"I could love you, you know."

The snow falls between us. I go completely still.

"I've known it for a while." Rourke huffs a breath that's almost a laugh, rueful, helpless. "Since the night we found you and you held my stare while you were dying because you wanted your face to be the last thing somebody remembered. I thought, *there's someone who's been alone her whole life and decided to be terrifying about it instead of broken.*" He shakes his head slowly. "And every day since you've gotten stronger and sharper and more *yourself*, and I've sat in my own hall watching a queen put herself together out of the pieces a coward left in the dirt, and I —" He stops. Starts again, steadier. "I'm not Kane Blackthorn. I will never throw you away to please anyone. There's no war I'd weigh you against. No alliance I'd choose over the look on your face right now."

He reaches out, slow enough that I could stop him, and tucks the silver streak back from my temple — the same gesture Mara used while she told me to go die quietly, and somehow the exact opposite of it.

"Stay," Rourke says. "Not as a weapon I found in the snow. Not as the Moonborn the world's about to come hunting." His thumb brushes my cheek, warm in the cold morning. "As mine. As Nightriver's Luna. Let me be the pack that *chose* you, Wren, instead of the one that chose against you." A breath. "Stay."

And the worst part — the part that tells me exactly how much trouble I'm in — is that for one long, traitorous heartbeat, with this good and honest man looking at me like I'm worth a kingdom, I want to say yes.

And down in my chest, forty miles south, the broken bond to the man who threw me away gives a single, jealous, agonizing pull — as if Kane Blackthorn, even now, even from there, can feel another Alpha's hand against my face.

chapters.title: Not a Prize
chapters.body:
I don't say yes.

I don't say no, either, and I hate myself a little for that — for the way I sit back on my heels in the trampled snow and take Rourke's hand off my face, gently, and say the only true thing I have.

"You're the first person who ever asked me to stay." My voice isn't steady. "Do you understand what that does to me? Eighteen years, and you're the *first*." I press his warm palm between both of mine and then I give it back to him. "Which is exactly why I can't say yes to you in the snow with my mother three weeks in the ground and a coward's bond still hooked in my chest. If I said yes now it wouldn't be a choice. It'd be a drowning person grabbing the first hand in the water." I make myself meet his eyes. "You deserve better than to be something I grabbed. And I deserve to want someone because I *chose* them, not because they got to me first."

Rourke is quiet. Then he huffs that rueful breath again and sits back, and the disappointment is real but so is the respect under it.

"That," he says, "is the most Luna thing anyone's ever said to me." He stands, offers his hand to pull me up — friend's hand now, not suitor's. "The offer doesn't expire, Wren. I'm a patient man. I run a pack; I've learned to wait for good things." A crooked smile. "Just — don't grab the coward either, when the bond gets loud. Promise me that much. He had his hands on a queen and he chose a war. He doesn't get to keep the moon just because the moon won't let go of him."

"I'm not going to grab anyone." I let him pull me up. The silver streak falls back into my eyes and I leave it. "I spent my whole life being chosen *against*. I'm done being something other people decide about. The next time someone gets me, it'll be because I decided. Not the moon. Not him. Not even you." I mean it down to the bone. "Me."

Rourke inclines his head, and there's something almost like pride in it. "Then let's get you strong enough that the deciding is yours to do." He turns toward the hall — and stops.

The wind has shifted.

I smell it the same instant he does, and the silver wolf in me goes rigid from nose to tail: that cold reek of old blood and the dark side of the moon, the scent of the scout from the hollow, but stronger now. Closer. *More of them.*

"Rourke." My voice has dropped. "We're not alone on this ridge."

They come out of the treeline like the dark deciding to walk — six, eight, ten lean grey shapes, wolves but *wrong*, their eyes filmed with something that drinks the dawn instead of catching it. The Nightriver patrol closes ranks around me on instinct, snarling, and Rourke shifts mid-step into a great tawny wolf, and the morning that was a stupid pack squabble ten minutes ago becomes something out of Maeve's worst stories.

I shift too. Silver floods me. I plant myself at the center of the patrol, the tide rising, *point it don't drown in it,* and I face the leader of the grey wolves down the slope.

He doesn't attack. That's the terrifying part. He sits, almost lazily, in the bloodied snow, and he tilts his ruined head at me, and into the back of my waking mind — the same private dark where the Silver Queen's voice lives — slides a new voice. Male. Patient. Delighted. A thousand years hungry.

*There she is,* Malachai says, gentle as a lullaby, and I feel the patrol stiffen because somehow they can almost hear it too. *Look at you. All grown up and shining. They told you you were nothing, didn't they, little queen? Spat on you. Starved you. Threw you in the dirt.* A long, loving pause that turns my blood to ice. *We never would. You belong to the dark that made you. Stop playing house with these mongrels.*

The grey wolves rise as one.

*Come home, Moonborn,* Malachai croons —

and they charge.

chapters.title: The Tide That Drowns
chapters.body:
The first grey wolf reaches me and I take it apart.

I don't decide to. The tide decides — it comes up out of the ridge and the dawn and the thousand years of rage that aren't all mine, and it pours through me, and the Shadowblood that leaps for my throat simply *stops* in the air, caught in a wash of silver light that pours off my coat like water breaking a dam. It hits the snow and doesn't get up.

The second one I break. The third. The power is glorious — clean and enormous and *easy*, easier than breathing, and that's the trap, that's the floor that isn't there, because the easier it gets the more of it I want and the more I take the less of me is steering.

*Yes,* says the older voice, the fond one, the Queen's. *Yes. That's it. Let it run. Why hold back? Look what they did to you. Burn them all.*

The silver stops being something I point and starts being something that points me.

The ridge goes white. I can't see Rourke. I can't see the patrol. I can't see the difference anymore between the grey wolves and the tawny ones, between the things that want to drag me into the dark and the wolf who asked me to stay — there's only the tide and the rage and the bottomless wonderful ease of *more*, and somewhere very far away someone is screaming my name and I can't remember why it matters —

— and a tawny body throws itself across me.

Rourke. He hits me broadside, knocks me down into the snow, into my own four legs, and the contact breaks the white long enough for me to see what I'm doing — to see the silver light scything out in a ring, indiscriminate, catching Shadowblood and Nightriver wolves alike, to see two of Rourke's own warriors flung broken into the rocks by a power that stopped caring whose side they were on.

By *my* power.

The horror of it slams the tide back down my throat. The silver dies. I collapse in the bloodied snow in skin, shaking, naked and freezing and appalled, and the world rushes back in — the whimpers of the wounded, the reek of blood, the grey wolves *gone*, melted back into the treeline the instant their work was done, because their work was never to beat me.

It was to show me what I am.

Rourke is down beside me. He shielded me from my own power at the end, took the edge of that last silver scythe across his flank, and the wound is bad — a long, deep, glowing gash that isn't bleeding right, the silver in it eating at the edges like acid. He's breathing hard, amber eyes fixed on me, and even now, even wounded by me, the first thing he does is reach out and grip my shoulder to make sure I'm here, I'm me, I came back.

"You stopped," he rasps. "You came back. That's the whole — that's the whole battle, Wren. Coming back." He winces. "Maeve warned me. The tide doesn't care. It'll use the rage to drown the swimmer." His grip tightens. "Now you know its pull. Now you can point it. Next time —"

"There can't be a next time." My voice is a wreck. I press my hands to his wounded flank, trying to staunch it, and to my shock the silver in me answers *gently* this time — a thread of it, soft, the same light that restarted my heart in the woods — and the glowing edges of the gash slow, knit, begin to close. *Heal with it. Fight with it.* Both. "I hurt your wolves. I almost — Rourke, I almost killed *you*. The thing they want is for me to keep using it, and every time I use it I get closer to being the thing in the dark, and I —"

I stop.

Because the wind has shifted again, and over the reek of blood and Shadowblood and my own fear comes a scent I would know in my grave.

Pine and storm and the particular cold of the man on the other end of the bond in my chest, which has just, this instant, gone taut as a struck wire.

I lift my head. Across the valley, on the far ridge, dark shapes are massing in the morning light — wolves, many of them, organized, an Alpha's column, the black-and-grey of a pack I served dinner to for eighteen years.

The skirmish, the silver wolf, the message I sent the warrior to carry — *tell your Alpha the silver isn't dead.*

He came.

Kane Blackthorn has crossed into Nightriver land with half his pack at his back, and he is close enough now that I can feel, down the broken bond, the exact moment he catches my scent on the wind and goes utterly, completely still.

chapters.title: The Far Ridge
chapters.body:
Two packs face each other across a valley of bloodied snow, and I am the thing standing between them.

It happens fast, the way these things do. Rourke's horns call Nightriver up the slope to him; within minutes there are forty wolves at my back, hackles high, exhausted from the Shadowblood and spoiling all the same. Across the valley, Kane's column spreads along the far ridge — sixty, seventy, the disciplined dark mass of a war-pack that's been hunting one ghost for a month and has finally found her trail.

Rourke shifts to skin beside me, flank still raw where my own power tore it, and pulls a cloak around my shoulders. "He has the numbers," he says low. "But he doesn't have the high ground and he doesn't have you, and he knows both. He won't charge. He'll talk." His jaw tightens. "Stay behind me. Let me handle —"

"No."

He looks at me.

"You said it yourself." I draw the cloak around me and I don't take my eyes off the far ridge, off the single dark figure standing at the head of the Blackthorn line, the one the bond is screaming toward. "I'm done being something other people decide about. He spent a month hunting a rogue female to drag back and *atone* over. He's going to find out she isn't a rogue, and she isn't lost, and she isn't his to find." The silver rises behind my eyes and I let it flood all the way up, let it burn, let it be the first thing he sees. "I'm done hiding from him, Rourke. He needs to know what he threw away. Today."

I start down the slope alone.

Rourke says my name once, sharp, then bites it off — because he's an Alpha, and he knows the look of someone walking toward a thing they have to do, and he knows better than to put his body between a queen and her reckoning. Both packs go silent as I cross the open valley floor, a single small figure in a borrowed cloak with a streak of moonlight in her hair, the trampled snow crunching under my bare feet, the morning sun throwing my shadow long and strange behind me.

Halfway across, the bond pulls so hard I nearly stumble. I plant my feet and keep walking.

On the far ridge, Kane shifts to skin.

I see him do it — see the great dark wolf fold up into the man, into the storm-grey eyes and the scar through the brow and the jaw like a closing door — and I feel the exact instant he gets a clear look at me across the closing distance. I feel it down the bond like a physical blow. The disbelief. The recognition. And then, beneath both, rising too fast for him to lock behind his Alpha's face the way he locked it in the clearing —

— grief. Naked, bottomless grief, the month of hunting collapsing into the single unbearable fact of *her, alive, here, transformed,* and under the grief something worse for both of us, something the bond carries straight through my own chest so that I have to stop walking and press a hand to my sternum:

*want.* His. Aimed at me. As helpless and total as the moment the moon first threaded us together, and a hundred times worse now for everything he's done.

He comes down off the ridge.

He doesn't descend the way he did at the Choosing, regal and certain, the pack splitting before him. He comes down the slope too fast, half-running, like a man who's stopped being an Alpha and become only the thing on the other end of a bond that should be dead — and every wolf on both ridges watches the feared Alpha of Blackthorn stumble down a bloody hillside toward a girl he threw in the dirt, and I watch it too, and I let him come, and I do not move.

He stops an arm's length from me. Exactly where we stood in the clearing. Exactly the distance the moon first asked us to close.

This close I can see what the month did to him — the grey exhaustion, the bond-wound that wouldn't heal because I wouldn't die, the hand that lifts toward me and shakes. He looks at the silver in my hair, the silver burning in my eyes, the queen standing where the kitchen mouse used to be, and his voice when it finally comes out is wrecked, barely a sound at all.

"Wren," Kane says. Like a prayer. Like the name has been killing him.

And the broken bond detonates between us — visible, the air itself going silver-bright, both packs flinching back from the light of it — a year of want and a month of grief and the whole torn ruin of what the moon made and a coward unmade, all of it roaring up the wire at once, in front of two armies, with nowhere left for either of us to hide.

chapters.title: You Don't Get the Luna
chapters.body:
The silver light fades. The bond still hums between us, taut and screaming, but I am not the girl it pulled across a clearing a month ago, and I have spent every day since learning to point a tide instead of drowning in it.

So I let Kane Blackthorn feel all of it — the want, the grief, the moon's certainty roaring up the wire — and then, with both armies watching, I close my hand around the bond in my chest like a fist around a rope.

And I hold the line.

"Say it again," I tell him. My voice carries across the silent valley, steady, cold. "The thing you just called me. Say it again."

His throat works. "Wren —"

"That's the one." I tilt my head. "Funny. The last time we stood this close, an arm's length apart with the whole pack watching, you didn't call me anything at all. You called me a *problem*. A *weak Luna*. You called me a thing you couldn't afford with a war coming." I take one step toward him — *toward*, deliberate, watching him brace for it like a blow — and I drop my voice so it's just for him, the way his was just for me at the very end. "And then you said the words, and you tore the moon out of my chest, and you stood on your nice black stone and watched them drag me into the trees to die. Do you remember? You watched. You held the rock so you wouldn't follow, and you *watched.*"

"I know what I did." It comes out of him broken. Up close I can see his hands are shaking. "Every hour since. The bond didn't go quiet — it *woke*, it's been screaming in me for a month, and I deserved every second of it. I told myself I rejected you to save the pack and I was lying. I was afraid. Afraid of what your father's wolves would do, afraid of what I felt, afraid of —" His grey eyes search mine, desperate. "I made the worst calculation of my life with two hundred people watching, and I have come to —"

"To find me." I finish it for him. "To atone. To drag the rogue female home and feel better about yourself." The silver flickers up behind my eyes and I let him watch it burn. "That's the part you got wrong, Kane. You came looking for a girl you could rescue. A debt you could pay. Something small enough to fit back into the shape of the mistake you made." I spread my arms, the cloak falling open, the silver streak catching the morning sun, the silver wolf coiled and enormous just beneath my skin where both armies can feel it. "Do I *look* small to you?"

He has no answer. The whole valley has no answer. On the far ridge, his own pack — the wolves who threw funerals for rejected omegas before dawn — stares at the thing the kitchen mouse became, and I watch the rumor finish dying and the truth take root: *Moonborn. Silver. Queen.*

"You rejected the omega," I say, and now I say it loud, for all of them, because they all watched me crawl into those trees and I want every last one of them to hear it. "You stood up in front of your whole pack and you threw her in the dirt because she wasn't worth a war." I let the silver fill my eyes completely, moon-white, and I see Kane's breath catch and his pack flinch and Rourke, up on the high ground behind me, go very still. "She's dead. You killed her in that clearing. You don't get her back, and you don't get to feel better, and you do not —"

I close the last of the distance, until we're a hand's breadth apart, until the bond is a white wire between our chests, and I look up into the storm-grey eyes of the most feared Alpha in the Crescent, and I finish it soft as a blade going in.

"— get the Luna."

I turn my back on him.

I turn my back on the Alpha of Blackthorn in front of two armies — the single most dangerous thing a wolf can do, baring your throat and your spine to a predator and trusting the whole watching world to see that he doesn't dare — and I start walking, unhurried, back across the bloody valley toward Rourke and the high ground and the pack that knelt to me in the snow.

And behind me, in the silence, I hear it.

A sound the Blackthorn pack has never heard their Alpha make. A sound *no* pack has heard their Alpha make, not in living memory, not since the old days when the Moonborn walked and even alphas knew when to bow.

The crunch of a knee going down into the snow.

I stop. I don't turn around. I don't have to — I can feel it down the bond, can feel the proud unbendable spine of Kane Blackthorn folding, can feel the shock ripple through both armies like wind through wheat.

"Then I'll earn her," Kane says behind me, low and ruined and absolutely certain, kneeling in the bloodied snow before his entire pack and his enemy's both. "However long it takes. Whatever it costs. I threw away the only thing the moon ever gave me, and I will spend the rest of my life on my knees if that's what it takes to deserve the chance to stand back up beside her." A breath. "I'm not asking you to forgive me, Wren. I'm telling you I'm not going to stop."

The bond sings. The traitor thing in my chest leaps toward the sound of him.

I keep walking. But it costs me — Goddess, it costs me — and I know, with a cold and certain dread, that the hardest battle ahead of me isn't the Shadowblood, or Cassia, or the thing waking in my blood.

It's the man on his knees behind me, and the bond that won't let either of us go.

chapters.title: The Only Card Worth Playing
chapters.body:
Rourke wants to send them home bleeding.

"He came onto my land with sixty wolves." The Alpha of Nightriver paces his hall that night, the fury banked but real. "He knelt, fine, it was a pretty show — but the fact remains he marched a war-pack across my border hunting *you*, and the answer to that is not tea and a parley, Wren. The answer is teeth."

"The answer is the truth." Maeve sits by the fire with the crescent pendant — my mother's — turning in her old fingers. She's barely spoken since the ridge; the Shadowblood attack has frightened her more than she'll say. "Both of you, stop circling like dogs and *listen*. What happened on that ridge this morning wasn't a skirmish. It was a probe. Malachai sent ten wolves to do one thing — make the girl use her power in front of witnesses. And she did." Her pale eyes find me. "Both packs saw the silver now. The secret we've been keeping for a month died on that snow. By tomorrow every wolf in the Crescent will know a Moonborn has woken." She sets the pendant down. "Which means the question is no longer *who hides her.* It's *who stands with her when the dark comes for her in force.* And it is coming. The probe proves it. They're done waiting."

The fire cracks in the silence.

"Two packs," Rourke says slowly, the Alpha in him doing the math he doesn't want to do. "Nightriver alone can't hold against the Shadowblood and a Blackthorn that decides she's *theirs.* But Nightriver and Blackthorn together —"

"— might hold." Maeve nods. "If the Moonborn binds them. Which is exactly what the Moonborn were *for*, child, before the Silver Queen turned the gift into a leash. Not to rule the packs. To unite them." She looks at me with something close to apology. "I know what he did to you. I know the bond is a wound. But the cold truth is that the safest place for the last Moonborn in the world, with the dark waking up to hunt her, is at the head of the two strongest packs in the Crescent — and one of those packs will only follow the wolf its Alpha bends his knee to."

I stare into the fire for a long time.

I think about the clearing. I think about the trees. I think about Kane on his knees in the snow saying *I'm not going to stop.* And I think about my mother, who ran to the one pack hidden enough to hide a sleeping queen, and died there anyway, killed by someone who knew — someone who is, almost certainly, still in those halls.

And I realize that going back to Blackthorn isn't just the strategic move.

It's where the answer is.

"All right," I say.

Both of them look at me.

"I'll go back." I rise, the cloak around my shoulders, the silver streak catching the firelight. "Not as his mate. Not as anyone's prize. As the Moonborn — the only thing standing between the Crescent and the dark — and I'll bind the packs because Maeve's right that the binding might save lives." I meet Rourke's eyes, then Maeve's. "But understand why I'm really going. Someone in Blackthorn murdered my mother to keep me asleep. Someone has spent eighteen years deciding my life from the shadows. I'm walking back into the one place in the world that's been lying to me since before I was born." The silver rises behind my eyes. "I'm not going home to be saved. I'm going home to find out who, and to look them in the face, and to let them see exactly what they failed to keep buried."

Rourke holds my gaze. Then he nods, slow, and there's grief in it and respect in it both. "Then Nightriver rides with you. I'm not letting you walk into that nest alone — and I'm *certainly* not leaving you alone with him." A thin, dangerous smile. "Let the coward grovel with me in the room. It'll do him good."

Maeve rises too, gathering the pendant, pressing it back into my hand. "Then it's decided. We go to Blackthorn." But her old face is troubled, and as she turns to the door she says the thing none of us want to hear, quiet, into the firelit dark.

"Just remember, child — whoever killed your mother is still in those halls. And the moment you walk back through that gate, glowing silver for all the world to see —" she looks back at me "— you stop being the secret they buried. You become the one thing they failed to kill. And cornered things that have hidden for eighteen years do not surrender." Her gold eyes hold mine. "They strike."

chapters.title: The Gate I Crawled Out Of
chapters.body:
I walk back through the Blackthorn gate on my own two feet, in daylight, with a silver streak in my hair and an Alpha's pack at my back.

The last time I passed this gate I was being dragged through it the other way, under the arms, my legs not working, the bond freshly torn out of my chest. Mara walked beside me telling me to go die quietly. The pack didn't look. The same gate. The same scarred stone. I make myself stop under the arch of it for one breath, just to feel the difference, just to let the wolf in me stand exactly where the omega fell.

Then I walk in, and the whole pack falls silent, and they kneel.

Not all at once. It moves through them like a wind — the warriors first, the ones who saw the silver on the ridge or heard it from those who did; then the betas; then the kitchens, the hall-folk, the ones who watched me scrub their pots for eighteen years. They go down into the dirt of the yard with their eyes wide and their faces gray, and I walk between them in Rourke's borrowed cloak, and I do not let a single flicker of the kitchen mouse show on my face.

Inside, though — inside, the mouse is screaming. *Run. Make yourself small. The small ones get hit less.* Eighteen years of instinct, and it takes everything I have to keep my spine straight and my pace even and my eyes forward.

Then I see Mara.

She's at the edge of the yard, half-hidden behind a post, and she's already on her knees because everyone is, but when I turn my head and find her the blood drains clean out of her face. She knows me. Of course she knows me. She tucked the hair off my sweating face and told me to go off into the trees and be quiet about it.

I cross the yard to her. The pack holds its breath. Rourke tenses behind me; I feel it. I stop in front of the woman who made an art of my smallness for eighteen years, and I look down at her, and she looks up at me, and I watch the calculation run behind her eyes — the same gentleness-with-a-blade-in-it she's used my whole life, reaching for a way to make this survivable.
