"Little —" Her voice cracks. She wets her lips. "My lady. Moonborn. I always — I always knew you were special, I *did*, I tried to —" Her hands come up, pleading, the hands that tucked my hair while she told me to die. "I was kind to you. You remember. I was kind."

And here is the thing no one warned me about.

I expected to feel triumphant. I've dreamed this — Mara on her knees, terror where there used to be contempt, the whole yard watching her grovel to the mouse she tormented. I have lain awake building this exact moment out of spite.

But standing over her, watching the fear pour off her, what rises in me isn't triumph.

It's the tide.

It comes up fast and easy and *delicious*, the silver flooding behind my eyes, and for one long heartbeat I can feel exactly how simple it would be — to let it out, just a little, just enough to make her feel one tenth of the cold I felt crawling into those trees. She *deserves* it. Every cruel gentle word. The funerals before dawn. The hand on my face. The tide agrees, eager, *yes, yes, look what she did to you, why hold back —*

— and that's the voice. The Queen's voice. The fond, patient, terrible one, wearing my own rage like a glove.

I shut it down so hard my knees nearly buckle.

I step back from Mara. I let the silver drain out of my eyes. And I find, when I speak, that my voice is perfectly level, and that the levelness frightens her more than the silver would have.

"You were never kind to me, Mara." I say it quiet, just for her, the way the worst things are always said. "You were *careful.* There's a difference, and you taught it to me, and I learned it better than anything you ever meant to teach." I look down at the woman who made me small. "I'm not going to hurt you. Not because you don't deserve it — because the thing that wants to is the same thing that's hunting me, and I'd rather die than let it win an inch using your face as the door." I turn away. "Get up. Go back to your kitchen. Live a long, careful life. That's the only mercy I have, and you have no idea what it costs me to give it."

I walk away from her across the silent yard, and my hands are shaking inside the cloak, and only Maeve — falling into step beside me, her gold eyes knowing — sees it.

"It liked that," I tell her under my breath, sick. "The tide. It wanted her. It used *my* anger to reach for her. Maeve, it's getting easier to call and harder to —"

"I know." Maeve's voice is grim. "The more you wake, the louder she gets. Every time you reach for the power in anger, you reach a little further into the dark for it." She takes my arm, steadying, as we climb the steps into the great hall of the pack that buried my mother. "Which is why you must be most careful here, child. Of all the places in the world." She glances up at me. "This is the one place that knows exactly which griefs of yours to pull on. And someone in these halls has had eighteen years to practice."

chapters.title: What the Ledgers Knew
chapters.body:
Kane grovels the way he does everything — with terrifying focus.

He doesn't crowd me. That's the first surprise. I came back braced for an Alpha who'd use the bond, who'd corner me in corridors and trade on the want roaring up the wire between us. Instead he gives me his own quarters and takes a soldier's room down the hall. He puts Nightriver's wolves wherever I ask. He sits at the foot of the war-table, not the head, and when the betas look to him he looks to me, every time, in front of all of them — the Alpha of Blackthorn deferring to the Moonborn, planting it in the pack's mind one meal at a time.

It would be easier if he were cruel. The grief is so much harder to armor against than cruelty ever was.

But I didn't come back for his grief. I came back for the truth. And on the third night, when the hall is asleep, I find Kane already awake in the records room, surrounded by the pack's old ledgers, his scarred face gray in the lamplight, and I understand he's been looking for the same thing I have.

"You shouldn't be in here," he says, not looking up. Then, quieter: "I've been trying to find the right way to show you this for two days. There isn't one." He turns a heavy ledger around on the table and slides it toward me. "So I'll just stop being a coward about it. For once."

I sit. I read.

It's the pack census from the year my mother came — *Saoirse Calloway, stray, female, one infant, taken in by grace of the Alpha.* My grandfather's pawprint of an approval. And beside it, in a different hand, a notation I don't understand at first: a small crescent moon, inked in the margin, and a single word. *Confirmed.*

"That mark," Kane says. "I found it three more times. On the rolls of every Moonborn the old packs hunted, going back generations. It's a hunter's mark. It means someone identified the line and reported it up." His jaw is tight. "Someone in this pack put that mark next to your mother's name the year she arrived. Eight months before she died." He turns another page, and his hand isn't steady either. "And the order to take her in 'by grace of the Alpha' — the grace was a lie. Look at the date. The mark came *first.* They didn't take her in out of mercy, Wren. They took her in because someone had already confirmed what she was hiding, and they wanted the line where they could watch it." His grey eyes finally come up to mine, full of a horror I feel echo down the bond. "They didn't fail to protect your mother. They *kept* her. Like livestock. Until they decided the secret was safer dead than sleeping."

The lamp gutters. The cold in me has gone very deep and very still.

"Who made the mark," I say.

"I don't know the hand." Kane's voice drops. "But I know who the pack's recorder was that year. And I know whose alliance my father was courting at exactly the time your mother arrived — whose wolves came and went freely in these halls, who had the run of these very ledgers." He says the name like it tastes of ash. "Ravenscar. Bram Ravenscar was a young Alpha then, hungry, and he'd already started buying old lore from the rogue-keepers. If anyone in the Crescent had the knowledge to recognize a Moonborn in a kitchen —" He stops. Makes himself say it. "It was the pack I sealed my alliance with by rejecting you. The pack whose daughter sleeps under this roof tonight as my promised Luna." His hand closes into a fist on the ledger. "I rejected you to please the people who murdered your mother. I didn't know. That's not an excuse. But by every law of the moon, Wren, I swear to you — *I didn't know.*"

I sit very still in the lamplight with my mother's death finally given a shape, and the shape is a crescent moon inked in a margin by a Ravenscar hand, in the halls of the pack that took her in to watch her, in the bed of the Alpha who threw me in the dirt to seal their friendship.

And down in my chest, where the broken bond hums, I feel Kane's guilt and his fury braid together with mine until I genuinely cannot tell, for a moment, whose rage is whose.

"Then we have a problem," I say at last, and my voice is the level one again, the one that frightens people. I close the ledger. "Because Cassia Ravenscar isn't just your political mistake, Kane. She's the daughter of the man who had my mother killed. She's been sleeping under this roof, beside the bond that's mine, smiling at me across your war-table —" I rise, the silver climbing behind my eyes, slow and cold "— and three weeks ago I watched her hand a folded paper to a Shadowblood wolf in your own dark corridor."

Kane goes absolutely still.

"What," he says, "did you just say."

chapters.title: The Storm
chapters.body:
I tell him everything.

The bond-walking. The night I rode the broken wire south into his own study and watched him swear he'd burn the Crescent to find me. The corridor after, Cassia and the lean grey-eyed rogue, the folded paper, the shape of *her* on Cassia's lips as she handed her future husband's enemies the one thing they want most in the world.

Kane listens without moving, and I watch the last of his denial die.

"I can't move on her yet," he says finally, low and furious. "Bram has half the alliance's wolves a day's ride from this gate. If I accuse his daughter of consorting with the Shadowblood with nothing but a bond-vision to show for it, he calls it a lie to break the alliance, and we have a two-front war — the dark on one side and Ravenscar on the other — before you're strong enough to bind anything." His fist is white on the table. "I need proof. Hard proof. The kind that turns *his* wolves against him, not mine against me."

"Then we get proof." The silver has drained from my eyes; this is strategy now, cold and clear. "Quietly. While she thinks she's winning."

It's nearly dawn before we stop. Outside, the storm that's been building over the mountains all night finally breaks — wind slamming the shutters, rain coming sideways, thunder that rolls down off the peaks and shakes the old stone. The lamps gutter. Somewhere in the hall a door bangs.

And the bond, which I've held in a fist all night while we worked, slips.

It's the exhaustion, I think. Three days back in this place, the ledgers, my mother, Cassia, the storm rattling the windows like the night I crawled into the trees — my grip loosens, just for a moment, and the want pours up the wire between us and fills the small lamplit room until there's no air in it that isn't *him.*

Kane feels it land. His head comes up. Across the table our eyes meet, and the whole careful distance he's kept for three days — the soldier's room, the foot of the table, the not-crowding — trembles like the shutters in the wind.

"Wren." My name, rough. A warning, maybe. To himself.

"Don't." I'm already standing. I don't remember standing. The storm howls. "Don't say my name like that. You don't get to —"

But he's around the table, or I am, the distance the moon first asked us to close gone in a breath, and we're a hand's width apart in the gutting lamplight with the bond screaming and the rain lashing the glass, and his hand comes up — slow, shaking, the way it shook in the clearing, the way it shook in the snow — and his fingers brush the silver streak at my temple, and I don't stop him.

I should stop him. He threw me in the dirt. He sealed his peace with my mother's murderers. He's promised, right now, tonight, to the woman sleeping down the hall.

I don't stop him.

His thumb traces my cheekbone, and the bond sings so loud it drowns the thunder, and his grey eyes drop to my mouth, and I feel my own treacherous body lean a half-inch toward his, eighteen years of wanting one person to want me to stay collapsing into the single unbearable fact of how close he is, how warm, how the wire between us has been screaming to close this exact distance since the moon first drew it —

"I never stopped feeling it," Kane breathes against my mouth, almost there, almost. "Not for one hour. I told them you were nothing and I have never wanted anything in my life the way I —"

The door opens.

Cassia stands in it, in her pale nightgown, a lamp in her hand, the storm at her back — and she takes in the records room, the ledgers, the Alpha of Blackthorn with his hand against the Moonborn's face and his mouth a breath from hers, and the want still thick enough in the air to choke on.

For one heartbeat nobody moves.

Then Cassia smiles — the clearing smile, the one she wore while I screamed in the dirt — and she steps into the room, perfectly calm, and sets her lamp down on the table beside my mother's death.

"How fortunate," she says sweetly, "that I came to find you, my love. I couldn't sleep. I wanted you to be the first to know." She turns the smile on me, and it is pure poison wrapped in silk. "I've spoken with my father. The wolves are gathering. We've set the date." Her eyes never leave mine. "Kane and I will be wed at the next full moon — under the very same moon that was foolish enough to choose a kitchen girl." She laces her arm through Kane's, who has gone rigid as struck stone. "You'll come, won't you, Moonborn? I do so want you in the front row."

chapters.title: The Kiss That Carried
chapters.body:
I don't sleep.

I walk. Out of the records room, away from Cassia's poison and Kane's stricken silence, out into the storm, because the silver wolf in me needs the rain and the dark and the wind more than I need a roof. I shift halfway across the yard and run — up into the wet pines above the pack, lungs burning, paws tearing the mud, the wedding date pounding in my skull in time with my heart. *Next full moon. The same moon that chose a kitchen girl. Front row.*

I run until I can't, and then I'm in skin again in a clearing high above the sleeping pack, soaked through, shaking, and I'm not alone.

Rourke is there. He followed me out — of course he did; he's barely left my flank since we rode through the gate, watching the coward grovel with exactly the patient menace he promised. He shrugs out of his own cloak and puts it around my wet shoulders without a word, and we stand in the rain looking down at the lights of Blackthorn, and after a while he says, quietly:

"You don't have to stay for this, Wren. The binding, the proof, the long game — I understand why Maeve wants it. But there's another version where we ride back to Nightriver tonight, the three of us, and we hold the high country, and we let Blackthorn marry its murderers and rot." His amber eyes find mine in the dark. "You don't owe this pack your safety. You owe them nothing. You crawled out of that gate dying and you do not have to walk back into a wedding to watch the man the moon chose marry the daughter of the wolf who killed your mother." His voice gentles. "Come home. Let me give you a place where the worst thing in your life isn't sleeping down the hall."

And the thing is — he's right. Every word. It would be so much simpler. So much kinder to myself.

I look at him. This good, dangerous, honest man who knelt to me in the snow, who asked me to stay, who has never once made me feel small. The first person who ever wanted me to stay. Standing in the rain offering me a way out of all of it, asking for nothing, meaning every word.

And I kiss him.

I don't plan it. It's grief and gratitude and the wild need to choose *something*, to be the one who decides for once — and his mouth is warm in the cold rain and his hands come up to frame my face like I'm something precious instead of something dangerous, and for a moment, a real one, I let myself sink into being wanted by someone who never threw me away.

It's good. It's *kind.* It is everything the bond is not.

And it's wrong, and I know it's wrong the instant it starts, because halfway through it the broken wire in my chest goes white-hot —

— and forty feet down the mountain and a year of grief away, in a soldier's room in the Blackthorn hall, Kane Blackthorn comes off his bed with a sound I feel tear up the bond into my own chest, a raw animal howl of pure betrayal, and shifts mid-stride into a great dark wolf and slams into his own locked door hard enough to splinter it, because he felt it — felt my mouth on another Alpha's, felt it like a blade going in, the bond carrying every bit of it straight through him the same way it carried his want through me.

I break the kiss, gasping.

Rourke reads my face instantly — he always does — and the warmth in him cools into something rueful and sad. "He felt that," he says. Not a question. "Didn't he."

"Yes." I press my hand to my own chest, where Kane's howl is still ringing. "Rourke, I'm sorry, I shouldn't have — that wasn't fair to you, I used you to —"

"You didn't use me." He steps back, gentle, giving me room. "You chose me. For a minute. And it was real, and I'll keep it." A crooked, heartsore smile. "But you flinched at the *exact* moment he felt it, Wren. Not before. Not after. You weren't kissing me to feel my mouth." He says it without cruelty, just truth. "You were kissing me to make him feel it. And the terrible part —" he tips his head, watching me with those steady amber eyes "— is that some part of you knew he would. That you *wanted* him to. Which means whatever you keep telling that ridge full of wolves, and whatever you keep telling me —"

He pulls his cloak a little closer around my shoulders, a friend's hand, and steps back into the rain.

"— the bond already chose for you. You're just not done being angry enough to admit it."

He walks back down the mountain toward the lights, and leaves me alone in the storm with the truth of it, and with the sound of a dark wolf howling my name down a wire that should be dead — and the worst part, the very worst part, is that the traitor thing in my chest is howling back.

chapters.title: The Thing I Won't Say
chapters.body:
"You kissed the wrong Alpha," Maeve says, "to hurt the right one."

It's morning. The storm has blown out, leaving the yard washed and steaming, and I've come to her rooms because she's the only one in these halls who won't flinch from anything I say. She's grinding something pungent in a stone bowl and she doesn't look up while she filets me.

"That's not —"

"It's exactly what you did." The pestle scrapes. "And the boy knew it, which is why he's twice the wolf the other one is, and walked away anyway. And the *other* one knew it, which is why he put his shoulder through a door." She sets the bowl down and finally looks at me, gold eyes flat. "Sit down, child, and let me tell you a hard thing, because we are running out of days for soft ones."

I sit.

"A fated bond is not love." Maeve says it plainly. "People your age think it is. It isn't. The moon doesn't promise you'll be happy, or that he'll be good, or that he won't throw you in the dirt — clearly. The bond is older and stupider than all that. It's recognition. It's the moon looking at two souls and saying *these two are made of the same weather.*" She leans back. "You can hate the weather. You can run from it to the high country and marry a kinder sky. People do. But you'll spend your whole life feeling the pressure drop before every storm, and knowing, in the part of you that doesn't lie, exactly where the lightning lives." Her voice softens, just slightly. "You kissed Rourke and flinched the instant Kane felt it. You've ridden the broken bond into that man's mind a dozen times when your guard was down. You walked back into the one pack on earth that murdered your mother, and yes, you came for the truth — but you could have hunted that truth from Nightriver, child, and you know it. You came back because the bond is here." She holds my eyes. "Say it. Once. To me, where it's safe. Then you can go back to lying to the armies."

The morning light falls across my mother's pendant where it lies on the table between us.

"It's him," I say.

It comes out of me like the silver came out of my palm in the woods — a held breath finally let go, terrible and total. "I hate that it's him. He threw me away. He sealed his peace with the people who killed her. He's marrying her daughter under our moon in three weeks. And it doesn't matter, because the bond doesn't care what he did, it only cares that he's *mine*, and I have wanted exactly one thing my entire life and it turns out to be the one person who proved I was right not to want things." My voice cracks. "Rourke is *better*. He's kinder, he's braver about his own heart, he never made me small. If I had any sense I'd love him and let the lightning live where it lives." I press the heels of my hands to my eyes. "But I don't get to choose where the lightning lives, do I. That's the whole curse of it. I only get to choose what I do while I'm standing in the storm."

Maeve is quiet a long moment. Then she reaches across and folds my mother's pendant into my hand, the same way she did the day she told me Saoirse was murdered.

"Then choose well," she says. "Because the storm is coming whether you've made peace with the lightning or not." She nods toward the window, toward the gathering being raised in the yard below — the festival the two packs are throwing to mark the alliance, the one where I'm meant to stand up and claim my name as Moonborn and bind the wolves of the Crescent for the war ahead. "Today you stop being Blackthorn's secret and start being the Crescent's queen, in front of every pack that came to feast. And somewhere out there in the dark, a man who's waited a thousand years to break a seal is watching you do it, and counting how many of his enemies you've gathered conveniently into one place."

A cold finger traces my spine.

"Maeve." I look up. "Where's Cassia this morning? I haven't seen her since the storm."

The old woman goes still. The pestle stops.

And down in the yard, far off, a horn sounds the open of the festival — bright and festive and entirely unaware — as somewhere beyond the gates, in the dark line of the trees, the daughter of my mother's murderer finishes whispering the time and the place into a Shadowblood ear.

chapters.title: I Claim My Name
chapters.body:
They build a fire in the Choosing clearing for the festival, and the irony is not lost on me.

The same clearing. The same dais of black stone. The packs of half the Crescent gathered to feast the alliance and to see, with their own eyes, the rumor that's run the mountains for a month — the silver wolf, the woken Moonborn, the kitchen ghost who turned out to be a queen. They came for a wedding announcement and a war-binding. They came, most of them, to gawk at me.

I let them.

When the horns call the gathering to order and Kane stands on the dais — at the foot of it, again, deferring, with Cassia rigid and smiling at his shoulder — I climb the black stone alone, to the exact spot where the moon threaded a bond to my chest and a coward tore it out, and I turn and face the packs of the Crescent in the firelight.

I am not standing at the back, in the dark, anymore.

"Most of you know me as a story," I say, and the clearing falls silent. "The silver wolf. The rogue female. The thing that crawled out of these trees a month ago and didn't have the decency to die." A ripple moves through them. "Some of you were *here* that night. Some of you watched them drag me out. Some of you threw the funerals for the omegas who came before me and didn't wake up." I let the silver rise behind my eyes, slow, deliberate, and across hundreds of faces I watch the awe and the fear ignite. "I'm not a story. I'm Wren Calloway. My mother was Saoirse, who they called a stray, and who was the last free daughter of a bloodline this world spent a thousand years trying to bury. And I am the Moonborn — the first to wake since the old days — and the dark that the old packs sealed away is waking with me."

I tell them the truth. All of it. The seal. The Shadowblood. Malachai. The thing in the dark that wants my blood to finish what it started a thousand years ago. I watch the festival curdle into something graver and more honest, watch alphas who came to feast realize they've come instead to a war-council, and I feel Maeve's lesson settle into my bones: *this is what the Moonborn were for. Not to rule the packs. To unite them.*

"I didn't ask for any of this," I tell them. "I asked the moon for a mother, and a name, and one person who'd want me to stay. She gave me a war instead." A few rough laughs, surprised. "But the war is here whether I asked for it or not — and it isn't mine alone. The dark doesn't stop at one pack's border. If it breaks the seal it breaks the world. So I'm not standing up here to be your queen." I look out over them, pack by pack, alpha by alpha. "I'm standing up here to ask you to stand together long enough to keep the lights on. Bind with me. Not under me — *with* me. Let the thing the old packs feared most — a Moonborn uniting the Crescent — be the thing that saves it instead of the thing that —"

The fire goes out.

All of it — every fire in the clearing, the great central blaze and the cook-fires and the torches along the dais — snuffs in the same instant, as if the night took one enormous breath and swallowed the light. The clearing plunges into dark. Screams. The surge of bodies. And into the sudden black, from every edge of the trees at once, pour the grey shapes with the filmed eyes, dozens of them, more than the ridge, a *tide* of them, and the festival of half the Crescent becomes a slaughter in a heartbeat.

"SHADOWBLOOD!" Rourke's roar, somewhere in the dark. "TO THE MOONBORN! HOLD THE CENTER!"

I call the silver and it floods the clearing with cold light — enough to see by, enough to fight by — and I see the packs scrambling into ragged lines, Kane already a great dark wolf tearing into the grey tide, Rourke at his flank, the unlikeliest allies in the Crescent back to back over the people they're trying to save.

And at the treeline, walking out of the dark he made, calm amid the carnage as a man strolling through his own garden, comes the owner of the patient, delighted, thousand-year-hungry voice.

He's tall, and lean, and almost beautiful in a ruined way, with eyes that drink the silver light instead of catching it, and a smile I have heard in the back of my mind for a month and never had a face for. The Shadowblood part around him like water. He steps into the center of the clearing, into my cold light, and he looks up at me on the black stone where the moon once chose me — and he opens his arms wide, as if to embrace the whole burning, screaming clearing, as if it's a gift he's brought just for me.

"*There* she is," Malachai says, aloud this time, in a voice the whole clearing can hear, warm as a homecoming. "Hello, little queen. I'm so glad you gathered everyone you love into one place." His smile widens. "It's going to make this so much easier."

chapters.title: We're the Same
chapters.body:
I come off the black stone like a falling star.

I shift mid-leap, silver and enormous, and I land between Malachai and the burning clearing and I let the tide rise — *point it, don't drown* — and I throw a scythe of moonlight at the thing that's hunted me for a month, clean and controlled, everything Maeve taught me.

He catches it.

He lifts one hand, almost lazy, and the silver light that broke three Shadowblood on the ridge *bends* around him, curves into his open palm, and goes out like a candle pinched between two fingers. He doesn't even stop smiling.

"Oh, little queen." Malachai shakes his head, fond, disappointed. "Did the old woman teach you to *point* it? Like it's a spear?" He takes a step toward me through the carnage, and the grey tide flows around him, and behind me I can hear the packs dying in the dark. "It's not a spear. It's not yours. You're borrowing it from someone who's been waiting a very long time to lend it." His drinking-dark eyes hold mine. "Let me show you how it's actually done."

He raises both hands.

And the silver light — *my* light, the cold moonfire I've spent a month learning to call — turns on me.

I don't have words for it. The tide I reach for simply isn't mine anymore; it answers him, it bends toward him, the power in my own blood straining toward the thing in the dark like a dog hearing its true master's whistle. The light scythes back at me and I throw myself aside and it carves the black stone where I stood. I call it again and again it slews toward him, and the horror of it freezes me worse than any blow: *he can take it. He can reach into my own blood and take it.*

"There it is." Malachai's voice gentles, almost tender, as I stagger. "That look. The one where you realize you were never the queen. You're the *key.*" He spreads his hands, indicating the carnage, the seal, the thousand years. "The seal binds the Silver Queen to the dark. And the seal is keyed to her blood — to the living Moonborn line. As long as one slept, the door stayed shut. But a woken Moonborn —" his smile turns radiant "— a woken Moonborn is a key turning in a lock. Every day you wake a little more, the door opens a little wider, and the thing on the other side reaches a little further into the only blood that can free her." He tilts his head. "Whose voice have you been hearing, little queen? In the dark, when you reach for the power in anger? Does it call you *daughter?* Does it call you *home?*"

*There you are, little queen. They should not have done that.*

The fond, patient, terrible voice. The one that lives in the same dark as his.

"We're the same, you and I," Malachai says softly, and he's close now, close enough that I can see there's no white left anywhere in his eyes, only the drinking dark. "We're both hers. I've served her for a thousand years from outside the seal. You're going to free her from inside it. You can fight it, and lose a little more of yourself to the tide every time you reach — or you can come home, and stop being the weapon they all want to *aim*, and finally be the thing you actually are." He extends one pale hand into my cold light. "She's so proud of you. She's watched them spit on you your whole life. She's the only one who never thought you were nothing." His voice drops to a lover's murmur. "Come home, Wren. Let me take you to her."

And the worst thing — the thing I will not say to Maeve, will not say to anyone — is that for one cold, exhausted, bottomless heartbeat, with the borrowed power failing in my hands and the people who threw me away dying in the dark around me, the offer reaches something in me that eighteen years of cruelty hollowed out and left open.

*Someone* wants me. Someone always wanted me. She was just on the wrong side of a door.

"WREN!"

Kane's voice — and his body, a dark wolf flinging itself between me and Malachai's outstretched hand, jaws closing on air as the Shadowblood lord steps back, unhurried, into his grey tide. Rourke is there too, suddenly, the three of us in a ragged knot in the cold light, and the spell of that pale extended hand breaks, and I'm myself again, gasping, appalled at what almost answered.

Malachai sighs, as if we've been tiresome.

"No matter." He's already fading back into the dark he made, the Shadowblood flowing with him, the attack — I realize too late — never meant to win. Only to take. "You'll come to me. The door's already open in you, little queen; you'll feel it widening every day, and one day soon the wanting will be louder than the fear." His voice comes from everywhere now, and nowhere. "And to make sure you come *quickly* —"

The cold light gutters, and in the last flare of it I see, across the emptying clearing, a knot of grey wolves dragging something small and struggling and grey-braided back into the trees — see a weathered hand reaching toward me, hear a voice I love crack across the dark in fury and warning both —

— and then Maeve is gone, swallowed into the dark with the thing that's hunted me, and Malachai's last words drift back across the ruined festival like the closing of a door:

"— I'll be keeping the old woman. You know where to find us. Come alone, come soon, and come *willing* — or I'll start teaching her everything she ever taught you, in reverse."

chapters.title: The Rite of the Open Bond
chapters.body:
We can't track her.

The Shadowblood leave no trail — no scent, no prints, the grey wolves melting back into a dark that swallows even the silver wolf's senses. By dawn we've sent scouts to every border of the Crescent and they've all come back with the same nothing. Maeve has been gone twelve hours, and every one of them I feel like a hand around my throat.

"There's a way." It's Kane who says it, gray-faced in the war-room, the festival dead and the alliance shaken and Cassia conspicuously, carefully absent. "Maeve told me, weeks ago, in case it came to this. She didn't think I'd ever have the chance to use it." He won't quite look at me. "The Shadowblood hide from the world's senses. But they can't hide from the moon's. And the strongest moon-sense in the Crescent is a fated bond — the road that runs both ways. If we open ours, fully, not the broken trickle we've been living with — if we *reclaim* it long enough to look down it — the moon will show us where the dark is densest. Where they've taken her."

The room goes still. Rourke, at the table, goes very still indeed.

"Open it," I repeat. "The bond you tore out."

"It never closed. You know that better than anyone." Now he does look at me, and the want and the grief and the shame are all there, naked, where the whole war-room can see. "A Severing leaves the wound open. The rite — Maeve's rite — forces it the rest of the way *open* instead of letting it scar. For a few minutes, the bond is whole. Truly whole. Everything I feel, you feel. Everything you've felt —" his jaw works "— I feel. All of it. The moon hides nothing between mates when the bond is open. That's the cost." He says it like a confession. "You'll feel exactly what I felt the night I rejected you. And I'll feel exactly what I did to you. There's no looking away in it. That's how it finds the truth — and the dark."

Rourke speaks, quiet and hard. "And if she doesn't want to be that open with you. After what you did."

"Then we don't do it, and we find another way, and Maeve dies while we look." Kane doesn't flinch from it. "I'm not asking for absolution, Wren. I'm offering you a door, and I'm telling you honestly what's on the other side of it. The choice is yours. It's been yours since the ridge." He sets something on the table — a small clay bowl, a sprig of moon-silver herb, the things Maeve must have left him. "Maeve trusted you to be strong enough to walk through a hard door for someone you love. So do I."

I look at the bowl. I look at the man who threw me in the dirt and then knelt in the snow. I think of Maeve, who filleted me with the truth and then folded my mother's pendant into my hand, who is somewhere in the dark right now with a thousand-year-hungry thing promising to teach her everything in reverse.

There was never a choice.

"Do it," I say.

We clear the room — all but Rourke, who refuses to leave, and who I'm glad refuses, because I trust him to pull me out if the dark reaches up through the open bond and tries to keep me. Kane crushes the herb, lights it, lets the silver smoke curl up between us. He takes my hands. His are shaking. So are mine.

"Whatever you feel from me," he says, low, just for me, the way the worst and truest things are always said, "I want you to know I'm not hiding any of it. Not the cowardice. Not the want. Not the year I've spent unable to look at the black stone without being sick. You're going to see all of me, Wren. The version of me that did it, and the version that hasn't slept since." His thumbs move over my knuckles. "I'd rather you saw the whole truth and still walked away than have you stay for a lie. So. Look. All of it. I'm not going to flinch."

The silver smoke rises. The broken bond, the trickle I've held in a fist for a month, begins — terrifyingly — to *open.*

"Whatever's down there," Rourke says quietly from the doorway, a hand on the frame, ready, "you come back. Both of you. We have an old woman to save and a war to win, and I did not ride into this nest of liars to lose you to your own bond." His amber eyes find mine, steady. "Go look. Then come home."

I close my eyes, and I let the fist around my chest open, and the whole roaring truth of Kane Blackthorn comes flooding up the wire to meet me.

chapters.title: Everything He Felt
chapters.body:
The bond opens, and I am in the clearing again.

Not my memory of it — *his.* I'm behind Kane's eyes on the dais of black stone, and the moon crests, and a hook lands in his chest so hard it nearly drops him, and across two hundred wolves his head snaps toward a girl at the back, in the dark, where nothing is ever supposed to move. And the feeling that comes up in him — I taste it, whole, undiluted by a year of his shame — is *joy.* Blinding, terrified joy. *Her. The moon chose her. Mine.* He doesn't know my name yet and already something in him is on its knees.

Then Cassia's voice. *Kane.*

And I feel the war end inside him, feel the exact machinery of it — not contempt, never contempt, that was the lie I built to survive — but *fear.* A wall of it. Ravenscar riders at the border. The alliance a thread away from snapping into a war his rebuilt pack can't survive. And underneath that fear, the one I never guessed, the one that changes everything:

A voice. Older. From before the Choosing. Bram Ravenscar's voice, oily and certain, in a private room weeks earlier — *I hear the moon may surprise you at the Choosing, young Alpha. A word of friendly counsel: whatever stray she throws at your feet, reject it. Publicly. The old bloodlines have a way of attracting... attention. Claim a nobody and you paint a target on her, and on your whole pack. The wolves who hunt such things are not gentle, and not far.* A pause, smiling. *Reject her, and she stays beneath notice. Safe. Forgotten. It's the kindest thing you could do for her, truly.*

I feel it land in Kane the way it landed then — as a threat dressed as advice, from a man he didn't trust but couldn't yet defy. And I feel the calculation he made in the half-second on the dais, with the moon's joy still ringing in him and Bram's wolves at his gate: *if I claim her, they come for her. If I reject her, she lives. I can find her after. I can fix it after. Just keep her alive tonight.*

He thought he was saving me.

He was wrong — Goddess, he was so wrong, it was cowardice wearing protection's face, and the proof is that he let them drag me into the trees instead of slipping out to follow, chose the safe lie over the brave truth — but it was not *contempt.* He didn't throw me in the dirt because I was nothing. He threw me in the dirt because Bram Ravenscar told him a nobody survives and a chosen mate gets hunted, and he was too afraid, and too young in his power, to see that he was being played by the very man who'd murdered my mother for being exactly what I am.

And then — the part he warned me about, the part with no looking away —

I feel what it did to him *after.* The bond not closing. The wound staying open. The night three weeks later when it roared back to life and dropped him to his knees on the black stone and he understood, all at once, that he'd thrown away something that *woke.* The month of not sleeping. The black stone he can't look at. The hunt, the burning need to find me, not to atone — that was the lie he told the world — but because the bond was screaming and he could not survive its screaming and the only thing on earth that could quiet it was the girl he'd ruined.

It's all true. He hid none of it. I'm inside the whole of him and there's no contempt anywhere in it, there never was, there was only fear and a lie and a year of paying for both.

I come back into my own body with tears pouring down my face and Kane's hands crushing mine and his own eyes wet, because the bond ran both ways — he just lived my crawl into the trees, my heart forgetting how to beat, the cold, the moon, the voice — and he is shaking apart with it.

"I felt you die," Kane says, wrecked, barely a sound. "I felt you *die*, Wren, and I felt you decide to keep your face in my eyes so I'd never be free of it, and you were *right*, I never have been —" His voice breaks completely. "I told myself I was protecting you. Bram told me you'd be hunted. I believed him because believing him was easier than being brave. I let a murderer talk me into throwing my own mate in the dirt and I have hated myself in a way I didn't know was possible every hour since —"

"I know," I whisper. Because I do. I felt it. All of it.

And that's the worst part, the part that ruins the clean righteous anger I've armored myself in for a month: it was never as simple as I needed it to be.

But the bond is open, fully open, and through it — past Kane's grief, past my own — the moon shows us the other thing we opened it for. A pull. A direction. A place where the dark is densest, a wrongness in the north of the Crescent, a deep cold hollow where moonlight goes to die.

"There," Kane and I say at the same time, in the same breath, our hands locked, the bond singing whole between us for the first time since the moon drew it.

"That's where they've taken her."

chapters.title: What Was Found in Her Rooms
chapters.body:
The bond doesn't close again. Not all the way.

That's the thing about the rite — once you've forced the wound open, it doesn't scar back over the way it was. It quiets, but it stays open a crack, the way I imagine the seal in my blood stays open a crack, and now Kane and I move through the war-room's aftermath wrapped in a low constant hum of each other, his grief lapping at the edge of my anger, my anger lapping at the edge of his guilt, neither of us able to fully hide in our own skin anymore.

I hate how much I don't hate it.

"It doesn't make it forgivable," I tell him, because it needs saying, while Rourke organizes the rescue column outside and gives us the room. "Knowing why. It doesn't undo the trees, Kane. You let a murderer's advice talk you into the cruelest thing anyone's ever done to me, and you let them drag me out instead of being brave for one single night, and the fact that you've suffered for it doesn't pay for it."

"I know." He doesn't reach for me. He's learned that much. "I'm not asking it to be forgivable. I'm asking you to know it was *true.* That you weren't nothing. That the moon didn't make a mistake. That a coward made a calculation, and the coward was me, and the worth was always yours." A breath. "That's all I wanted you to see down there. Not so you'd forgive me. So you'd stop carrying the lie I made you believe."

And the terrible thing is that it works — that some knot in me I've carried since the trees, the one that whispered *of course he threw you away, you were always going to be thrown away,* loosens a fraction. Not because he's forgiven. Because I finally know it wasn't true.

That's when Rourke comes back in, and his face is wrong, and he's holding something at arm's length like it might bite.

"We searched the hall while you two were... occupied," he says. "Standard, before we ride — you don't leave a nest of allies at your back without knowing what's in it." He sets the thing on the war-table. "This was in Cassia Ravenscar's rooms. Sewn into the lining of a travel cloak. She left in a hurry sometime in the night — her rooms are half-packed."

It's a token. Black iron, cold even to look at, a crescent moon turned the wrong way — horns down, the way you'd draw the dark side of the moon, the side the light doesn't reach. The same symbol I've seen filmed in Shadowblood eyes. And worked into the iron, fine as a signature, a hunter's mark: a small crescent with a single word stamped beneath it.

*Confirmed.*

The same mark. The same word. The one inked in the margin beside my mother's name the year she came to Blackthorn.

The room goes silent. Kane picks it up, and I feel the cold fury detonate down the open bond before it reaches his face.

"It's a Shadowblood token," I say. My voice has gone level and quiet. "She wasn't just passing them messages, Kane. She *carries* one. She's not a wolf who got tempted into one bad deal." I look at the wrong-way moon. "Her whole family carries them. The mark on my mother's name was a Ravenscar mark. Bram didn't just *recognize* the Moonborn line for the old packs eighteen years ago." The pieces fall, one after another, into a shape I should have seen from the first margin. "He was already serving the dark. The Ravenscars have been Shadowblood all along — inside the packs, alliance by alliance, marriage by marriage, hunting the line, getting close to power, *waiting* for the Moonborn to wake so they'd be standing right next to the key when it turned." I look up at Kane. "You didn't seal your alliance with my mother's murderers. You sealed it with the dark itself. And it's been sleeping down the hall from you, smiling across your war-table, planning to marry you under our moon —"

A horn sounds. Not the festival's. Not the rescue's.

It's the south gate, sounding alarm — and then a runner is in the doorway, breathless and white, with the words that end the last shred of pretense in the Crescent:

"Alpha — Ravenscar's wolves! All of them, in war-paint, massing at the south line! Bram Ravenscar's calling you out by name — says you've stolen his daughter's rightful place, says the alliance is broken, says —" the boy swallows "— says he names Cassia the true Luna of the Crescent by older blood than yours, and that any pack that wants to live had better choose its side by moonrise." His eyes find me, terrified. "Half the alliance packs already rode to *him*."

chapters.title: Choose Your Side by Moonrise
chapters.body:
Bram Ravenscar sits his horse at the south line like a man who's already won.

We ride out to meet him under a parley flag — Kane, Rourke, and me, because hiding the Moonborn now would only confirm I'm a thing to be hidden. Behind Bram spreads his army: his own wolves, and behind them, ragged but real, half the alliance packs that came to my festival to feast and rode to him by dark. And behind *those*, at the treeline, in the deep shadow where the afternoon light goes thin — grey shapes. Filmed eyes. The dark, no longer bothering to hide, standing openly at the shoulder of a pack-alpha for the first time in a thousand years.

Cassia rides at her father's side, in silver still, smiling still.

"Kane Blackthorn." Bram's voice rolls out, genial, theatrical, pitched for every wolf to hear. "I came to your hall in friendship. I gave you my daughter, my finest wolves, my counsel. And how do you repay me? You let a *kitchen stray* climb onto the black stone and call herself a queen. You let her turn your head from your sworn bride." He shakes his head, sorrowful, performing. "The alliance is broken, and you broke it. So I'll be fair, as I have always been fair. I'll let every pack in the Crescent choose." He raises his voice. "Stand with Blackthorn and its silver pet — a woken Moonborn, the very thing the old packs spent a thousand years killing to keep the world safe — or stand with the rightful line, with order, with the wolf who knows what a Moonborn truly is." His smile widens. "Choose your side by moonrise. After that, there are no sides. Only the winners, and the meat."

It's a good speech. I'll give him that. He's spent eighteen years — generations — practicing the lie that the Moonborn are the danger, and half these packs grew up on it.

So I don't argue it.

I dismount. I walk out alone, past the parley flag, into the open ground between two armies for the second time in a month, and I let every pack in the Crescent watch the kitchen stray cross the field toward the man who murdered her mother.

"You're right," I say. Loud. Clear. To all of them. "A Moonborn woke. The old packs feared that for a thousand years, and Bram Ravenscar has just told you the dark is what you should fear least and *I'm* what you should fear most." I stop, ten feet from his horse, and I look up at him, and I let the silver rise behind my eyes slow enough for every wolf on the field to see it kindle. "So ask him this, before you choose. Ask him why the dark is standing at his treeline right now. Ask him about the iron token sewn into his daughter's cloak. Ask him whose mark sits in the margin beside my dead mother's name in Blackthorn's own ledgers — a Ravenscar mark, eighteen years old." I turn slowly, so they all hear it. "The old packs didn't fear the Moonborn because we're the dark. They feared us because we're the only thing that can *unite* you against it — and a divided Crescent is exactly what's been hunting you, marriage by marriage, alliance by alliance, since before any of you were born." I fix Bram with the silver. "He didn't come here to save you from me. He came to break you apart so the door in my blood opens with no one strong enough left standing to stop it. He's not the rightful line. He's the *dark's hand*, and his daughter's been sleeping down the hall from your strongest Alpha carrying its mark."

For one heartbeat, I see it work — see doubt ripple through the alliance packs, see wolves turn to each other, see Bram's genial mask slip into something uglier.

Then Cassia laughs.

She rides forward a length, silver and serene, and she looks down at me with the clearing smile, and she plays the only card that beats the truth: she makes it personal, and she makes it small.

"Listen to her," Cassia says, delighted. "The kitchen girl with a grievance. Of *course* she says we killed her mother. Of course she says we're the dark. She's a Severed omega the moon embarrassed itself over, and she'll say anything to climb out of the dirt she came from." She tilts her head, sweet as poison. "She even kissed two Alphas in a single month to do it — ask the Nightriver wolf if you doubt me. This isn't a queen, packs of the Crescent. This is a stray who got lucky with a bloodline, throwing the whole world into war because one boy hurt her feelings." Her eyes glitter. "Choose the stray, and her *thing in her blood* — or choose order. It's not difficult."

And I feel it tip — feel half the field's doubt curdle back into the old comfortable story — and I understand, cold and clear, that I cannot win this with words, because they have had a thousand years to write the words and I have had a month.

So I stop using words.

I let go of the fist around the tide. Not in anger — Maeve's lesson, finally, fully learned: *not the rage, the rage is her door.* I reach instead for the other thing, the gentle thread that knit Rourke's wound and restarted my own heart, the moonlight that *heals* — and I raise my hands, and I call it down not as a weapon but as a *light*, and the whole field, both armies, all the watching packs of the Crescent, are bathed in cold silver radiance pouring up off my skin and into the darkening sky like a beacon, like a moon come down to stand on the grass.

In its light, the grey shapes at Bram's treeline flinch back, hissing, *burning* —

— and every wolf on the field turns from the kitchen stray to the thing she actually is, and Bram Ravenscar's genial mask falls off entirely, and Cassia stops smiling, and into the vast stunned silence I say, very quietly, in the level voice that frightens people:

"Now. Ask him again why the dark runs from me, and toward him."

chapters.title: The Wolf Who Stepped Aside
chapters.body:
The beacon breaks Bram's lie.

You can't tell a field full of wolves that the Moonborn is the dark and then have the dark itself recoil hissing from her light. Half the alliance packs that rode to Bram by dark ride back to us by dusk, shamefaced, and the other half melt away into the hills to wait and see who wins — which is its own kind of answer. By moonrise it isn't half the Crescent against Blackthorn anymore. It's Bram Ravenscar, his own sworn wolves, and the grey tide at his back, drawn up across the north valley around the cold hollow where moonlight goes to die. Where they're keeping Maeve.

War. Real war, by the next full moon, the same moon Cassia meant to marry under.

We plan it in the Blackthorn war-room until the candles gutter — Kane and I, the bond a low constant hum between us, finishing each other's strategy the way we finished *there* on the field. And Rourke, across the table, watching us do it with an expression I can't quite read until, when the others have filed out to ready the packs, he asks to stay. Just the two of us.

"Nightriver rides with you," he says. "You know that. But I came to say the rest of it plainly, because you taught me that's the brave thing." He turns something in his hands — a Nightriver pledge-cord, the kind an Alpha gives only once. "When this is done, I'm binding my pack to you. Not to Blackthorn. Not to him. To *you*, Wren — to the Moonborn, the way the old packs bound to the Lunas before the world went wrong." He sets the cord on the table between us. "Nightriver chooses you. However the rest of it falls."

"Rourke —"

"Let me finish. I've been practicing." A crooked, heartsore smile. "I asked you to stay, once. In the snow. I meant it, and I'd mean it again." He looks at me, steady, amber eyes clear. "But I've spent a month watching you and the coward finish each other's sentences across a war-table, and I felt you flinch in my arms the instant he felt our kiss, and I'm an Alpha, Wren — I read a field. I know which way the weather's running." He spreads his hands, and there's grief in it, but no bitterness. "He's your fate. The moon-stupid, infuriating, unfair fact of it. And I'm not going to spend the years I've got fighting the moon for a heart that already knows where its lightning lives." 

The candle gutters. Something in my chest aches for him — this good man, choosing the hard true thing again, the way he always does.

"You deserve someone who chooses you first," I tell him, and I mean it down to the bone. "Not second. Not after the lightning."

"I know." He smiles, and it's real. "And I'll go find her, when the world isn't ending. I'm a patient man; I told you that." He picks the pledge-cord back up, loops it once around my wrist, ties it — pack to queen, not suitor to woman. "But tonight there's a war, and an old woman in the dark, and a door in your blood that the enemy wants you to open by going *willing* to that hollow. So here's what your friend the Alpha of Nightriver is going to do." His voice hardens into command. "I'm going to take my wolves and Kane's vanguard and I'm going to throw us at Bram's army across the north valley, loud and bloody and impossible to ignore — and while every grey eye in that hollow is on me holding the line, you and Blackthorn are going to slip in the back and get Maeve out."

The cold finds my spine. "Rourke. Bram has the numbers, and the dark. Holding that line —"

"Might kill me. Yes." He says it lightly, the way only the brave can. "It might. Hold the thought of it for me, would you? It's nice to think someone will." He moves to the door, then stops, and looks back one last time, and the lightness drops away into something I'll carry for the rest of my life. "I'd have been good to you, Wren. Better than he was." A beat. "Make him spend the rest of his life deserving the thing I'd have given you for free. That's the only revenge I want." 

And the Alpha of Nightriver walks out to go and stand in front of an army so I can save the woman who taught me what I am — and I stand alone in the gutted candlelight with his pledge-cord on my wrist and the awful knowledge that the bravest thing about loving someone, sometimes, is being willing to lose them to it.

chapters.title: The Hollow Where Moonlight Dies
chapters.body:
The battle for the north valley opens with Rourke's horns, and under their cover Kane and I go down into the dark.

The hollow is exactly what its name promises — a deep cleft in the mountain's north face where the light never reaches, where even my silver dims to a guttering candle, where the air is cold and old and smells of blood that dried a thousand years ago. The Shadowblood that should guard it have mostly gone to meet Rourke's charge; Malachai never expected me to come for Maeve by force, in the dark, with the coward at my side. He expected me to come *willing.* Alone. Through the front.

That arrogance is the only reason we live long enough to reach her.

We find Maeve in a cell of black iron at the hollow's heart, and she's alive — bruised, gray, but alive, and her gold eyes find me through the dark and go wide with a fury that is mostly terror.

"No," she rasps, before I've even got the iron open. "No, child, you shouldn't have *come* — this is the trap, this was always the trap, he wanted you *here*, in the dark, where the door's already wide —" She grips the bars. "Can you feel it? Tell me you can't feel it."

And the awful thing is, I can.

Down here, in the cold cleft where the light dies, the door in my blood is *open* in a way it's never been. The Silver Queen's voice isn't a whisper at the edge of my anger anymore — it's everywhere, fond and patient and close, *there you are, there you are, you came home after all, little queen* — and the tide in me strains toward the dark of the hollow the way it strained toward Malachai on the ridge, eager, like water finding the drain it's always been looking for.

I get the iron open. Kane catches Maeve as she stumbles out, and the three of us turn to run —

"It can be closed."

Maeve grabs my arm, and there's something in her voice that stops me cold. "I had time to think, in there. Too much time. I worked out the rest of it — the part I was saving for when you were stronger, the part I prayed we'd never need." Her gold eyes are wet. "The seal is keyed to the living Moonborn line. As long as one *slept*, the door stayed shut — but you can't go back to sleep, child, you're too far woken, and every day you wake more, the door opens wider. There's only one way to shut a door that's keyed to living blood." Her grip tightens to the bone. "You close it with the blood. *All* of it. A Moonborn freely spending her life on the threshold — that's the only lock that holds. The first sealing took every pack in the world and they still couldn't kill the Queen. They could only bind her. And the binding has a price, and the price has always, always been a Moonborn willing to die on the door so it stays shut."

The dark of the hollow seems to lean in to listen.

"That's the choice they never tell the woken ones," Maeve whispers. "Live, and become the key that frees her — slowly, helplessly, the wanting getting louder than the fear, until one day you walk into a hollow just like this one and *open it.*" Her voice breaks. "Or die on the threshold, freely, while you're still yourself — and shut her in the dark for another thousand years." She presses her forehead to my arm. "I am so sorry, child. I've known since you woke. I couldn't say it. You asked me, in Nightriver — *can it be closed* — and I told you it was a conversation for when you're stronger." A sob. "There is no stronger. There's only this. The door, and your blood, and a choice no one your age should ever have to —"

"No." Kane's voice, flat and absolute, from behind me. I'd half-forgotten he was there. He's gone the color of ash, and the bond between us is roaring with a refusal so total it's hard to breathe through. "No. We are not — there's another way. There's *always* another way. We get out of here, we win the valley, we find —"

"There isn't, boy." Maeve doesn't even turn to him. "I've spent my whole life keeping this lore so that one day I could tell a woken Moonborn the truth of it, and the truth is cruel, and you don't get to refuse it on her behalf." Her gold eyes hold mine, and there's a thousand years of grief in them. "But she does. It's hers. It's the only thing in this whole story that's ever been entirely, only hers." A whisper. "What do you choose, little queen?"

And in the dark where moonlight dies, with the Silver Queen's fond voice crooning *come home* and a war screaming overhead and the man the moon chose refusing to breathe beside me, I open my mouth to answer —

— and the hollow's entrance explodes inward in a wall of grey, and Malachai's voice rolls down out of the dark, no longer patient, no longer fond, *furious*:

"You came by *force?*" The drinking-dark eyes find me through the rubble. "You came to take her *back?*" And for the first time, the thousand-year smile is gone. "Then I'll stop being patient. If you won't come home willing, little queen — I'll burn down everything you'd die to protect until *willing* is the only door you've got left."

chapters.title: What Maeve Gave Me
chapters.body:
What happens next happens in less time than it takes to tell.

Malachai raises his hand, and the tide in my blood lurches toward him — but I'm ready this time, I've stopped reaching for it in anger, and when he pulls I *don't* feed it. I let the borrowed power go slack in my hands rather than let it bend to him, and his face flickers with surprise. The grey wolves pour in instead, a wall of teeth in the dark, and Kane meets them shifted and roaring, holding the cleft so Maeve and I can reach the broken entrance and the thin daylight beyond.

We almost make it.

We're three strides from the light when Malachai gives up on subtlety and simply throws the dark itself — a lash of cold nothing, the anti-light of the hollow, aimed not at me but at the old woman stumbling at my side, because he's smart, because he knows by now that the way to move a Moonborn is never through her own body.

I see it coming. I throw myself in front of her.

I don't reach the place in time. Maeve does.

She shoves me — small as she is, old as she is, she gets both hands on my chest and *shoves* me out into the daylight, into the light where I'm strong and Malachai's lash can't follow, and she takes the dark that was meant to make me willing full in her own chest, and she folds around it without a sound.

"*MAEVE!*"

I catch her as she falls. The daylight pours over us both at the hollow's mouth, and Malachai's wolves recoil from it, and Kane is suddenly there, dragging us both clear, but it doesn't matter, I can feel it doesn't matter, the cold nothing is already in her and there's no thread of healing silver that can knit a wound made of the absence of light.

"Don't," Maeve breathes, when I reach for the power anyway, frantic. "Don't, child — save it — listen — there isn't *time* —" Her gold eyes are already dimming, but they fix on mine with the last of her ferocious will. "I kept the lore my whole life to give it to you. So *take* it. All of it. Now. While I can still give."

She presses her hand flat to my sternum, over the open door, over my mother's pendant — and the last of Maeve pours into me.

It's not power, not the way the tide is power. It's *knowing.* A thousand years of lore poured down a dying woman's arm into the only vessel left to hold it — the truth of the seal, the shape of the Reclaiming, the names of things older than packs, the whole weight of everything she carried alone in the high hollows waiting for a queen to wake. It floods into me and I gasp under it, drowning, and Maeve smiles through the dimming.

"There," she whispers. "Now you know what I know. Now you're not alone in the dark with it." Her hand slips from my chest. "Your mother would be — she'd be so —"

And the keeper of the lore, the old woman who filleted me with the truth and folded my mother's pendant into my hand and shoved a queen into the light with the last of her strength, goes still in my arms at the mouth of the hollow where moonlight dies.

The grief that comes up in me is not the tide. It's quieter than the tide and infinitely worse. And for one annihilating moment, kneeling in the daylight with Maeve's body cooling against me and the Silver Queen's voice crooning *let me in, let me make them pay, let me out and I'll burn the one who did this* — for one moment I understand exactly how the first Moonborn turned. Grief is the widest door of all.

Kane drops to his knees beside me. He doesn't tell me not to grieve. He doesn't reach for the power, or the strategy, or the war screaming over the ridge. He just puts his arms around both of us, the living queen and the dead keeper, and he holds on, and through the open bond I feel him take some of the unbearable weight of it onto himself the way you'd take half a beam off someone pinned beneath it.

"I've got you," Kane says, low, into my hair. "Whatever you choose — the door, the blood, all of it — you are not going to be alone in the dark with it. Not while I'm breathing. I threw you into the dark once. I will spend every hour I have left walking into it *with* you." His arms tighten. "Grieve her. I'll hold the line for as long as you need. Then we get up, and we finish this, and we make Malachai sorry he ever learned your name."

And in the daylight, over the body of the woman who saved me, with a thousand years of lore burning in my blood and the man the moon chose holding me together — I let myself, for the first time since the trees, be held.

Then I lift my head, and the silver in my eyes has changed — deeper, older, *Maeve's* knowing folded into it now — and I look toward the north valley where Rourke is dying to buy me time, and I get up.

chapters.title: The Reclaiming
chapters.body:
Maeve's lore burns in me all the way back to the war, and somewhere in it, half-buried under a thousand years of older things, is a rite I almost wish she hadn't given me.

The Reclaiming.

I find it the way you find a splinter — by the way it hurts when I move past it. It surfaces while Kane runs beside me toward the north valley, the bond humming, and I nearly stumble at the weight of what it means.

There *is* a way to undo a rejection. The old packs knew it; it's why the Severing was considered a thing that could, in theory, be answered. A rejected bond, torn but not dead — exactly like ours — can be made whole again, fully, permanently, the way it was meant to be before the words were said. But not by the one who was rejected. The rejected wolf can't reclaim what was taken from her; she has no power over the words that broke her.

Only the rejector can.

The Reclaiming is the rejector kneeling — truly kneeling, all the way down, surrendering the alpha pride that let him say the words in the first place — and speaking the reverse of them, and *meaning* it so completely that the moon believes him. And the moon does not give such a thing for free. To pour a torn bond back whole, to reach across a Severing and undo it, the rejector spends his own life-force into the wound. Not a gesture. Not a symbol. His actual life, poured down the bond to heal what his words tore — and the older the rejection, the deeper the Severing, the more it costs.

Ours is a month old, torn down to the marrow, kept screaming alive by Moonborn blood.

It would cost him everything.

I don't tell Kane. I bury it under Maeve's other lore and I run, because there's a war to reach — but the bond runs both ways now, fully open since the rite, and that's the problem with loving someone who can feel the inside of your chest.

He goes quiet beside me. Then, between one stride and the next: "What did you just find."

"Nothing. Lore. Maeve gave me a lot of —"

"Wren." He stops. We're on the ridge above the valley now; below us, Rourke's wolves and Bram's army tear at each other in the dusk, the line holding, barely, bought with blood. Kane catches my arm and turns me, and his grey eyes are reading me the way only the open bond lets him. "You found something that frightened you more than dying on a door. I felt it. You can't hide from me anymore; that was the price of the rite, remember? *No looking away.*" His jaw sets. "What is it."

So I tell him. The Reclaiming. The kneeling. The reverse of the words. The cost.

I watch it land, and I watch — God help me — I watch *hope* ignite in him, fierce and immediate and completely wrong.

"Then that's the answer." He says it like a man grabbing a rope over a cliff. "You think you have to die on the threshold to close the seal — fine, maybe, but the seal's keyed to the *living Moonborn line*, Maeve said, and a bond reclaimed is a bond that —" he's working it out as he speaks, fast, desperate "— Wren, if I reclaim you, if I pour my life into the bond and make it whole, maybe the moon takes *that* as the price instead. My life on the threshold instead of yours. Maybe I'm the lock. Maybe I'm the —"

"*No.*" I grab the front of his shirt, the silver flaring in my eyes. "No. Absolutely not. I just watched Maeve die shoving me into the light, I am not going to stand on a threshold and watch you bleed your life into me to —"

"You'd do it for me." Quiet. Certain. The bond doesn't let him be wrong about it. "If our places were swapped — if it were your life that could close the door and save mine — you wouldn't hesitate. I felt you throw yourself in front of Maeve. You'd already be kneeling." His hand covers mine where it's fisted in his shirt. "So don't ask me to be less than you. I threw you into the dark once because I was afraid to spend myself for you. I am not going to make that mistake twice." His grey eyes are steady, and terrible, and absolutely decided. "If it comes to a door and a price — it's mine. I'm the one who tore the bond. Let me be the one who pays to make it whole."

Below us, a horn screams — Rourke's line breaking somewhere on the left, the dusk swallowing the valley, the war refusing to wait for us to finish deciding who gets to die for whom.

And I look at the man the moon chose, offering to pour his whole life into the wound he made, and I understand with cold dread that we have just become two people each secretly resolved to die before they let the other do it — which is the most dangerous thing two bonded wolves can possibly be, walking into the same war, each one a held breath away from spending everything to save a person equally determined to spend everything back.

"We'll argue about it after we win," I lie, and I shift, silver and enormous, and throw myself off the ridge into the dying light toward the breaking line — and Kane, beside me, shifts and follows, neither of us fooled, both of us lying, the bond between us roaring with everything we're not saying.

chapters.title: The Blade Meant for Me
chapters.body:
We hit the valley like the moon falling.

I come down off the ridge in silver, Kane a dark storm at my flank, and we crash into the broken left of Rourke's line where the grey tide was pouring through. I don't reach for the tide in anger this time — Maeve's last lesson, paid for in blood — I reach for the *light*, the healing radiance, and I throw it out across the whole valley, not as a weapon but as a tide of moonfire that knits the wounded where they've fallen and burns the Shadowblood where they stand and tells every exhausted wolf on our side, in the language older than words, *the Moonborn is here, hold, hold, you are not alone in the dark.*

The line holds. Then it surges.

I find Rourke in the thick of it, bloodied, three wounds I close with a touch as I pass, his amber eyes finding mine with fierce relief. "Maeve?" he shouts over the din. I shake my head, once, and watch the grief cross his face and harden into purpose. "Then we make it *mean* something," he roars, and throws Nightriver forward.

We're winning. For the space of a few minutes, against everything, we're actually winning — the light at our backs, Bram's army buckling, the grey tide thinning as the Shadowblood recoil from the radiance pouring off my coat.

Which is exactly when I feel Kane's attention split — feel, down the open bond, a flicker of something behind us, in the press, where the fighting's thinnest.

Cassia.

She's not in war-paint. She's still in her silver, threading through the chaos like she's walking a garden, and she's not fighting anyone — she's moving with purpose toward the one target worth more to her than any line. Not me. *Him.* The Alpha she was promised, the throne she was going to marry, the prize the kitchen stray took. And in her hand, where the silk of her sleeve hides it, the dark glint of black iron — a blade the color of the lash that killed Maeve, the absence of light given an edge.

I scream his name down the bond and across the valley both.

Kane turns. But he turns toward *me*, toward the alarm, toward the threat he assumes is coming for the Moonborn — he puts himself between me and the press, broad back to the danger, exactly the wrong way, because he has spent a month learning to throw his body between me and the dark and the instinct is bone-deep now —

— and Cassia drives the black blade into his back.

It's meant for me. I know it's meant for me even as it happens — she aimed for the Moonborn and Kane stepped into the line of it the way he always steps into the line of it — but it's in *him*, between the ribs, the dark iron drinking the light right out of him, and I feel it tear through the open bond like it's gone into my own body.

Kane's knees go. The silver in the valley flickers as my control breaks.

"For the record," Cassia hisses, wrenching the blade free, her beautiful face finally, fully ugly, "I was always going to do it eventually. Husbands are so much easier to manage dead." She's already melting back into the chaos, her work done, leaving him falling. "Enjoy what's left of him, kitchen girl. Shadowblood iron doesn't let go."

I catch Kane before he hits the bloodied ground.

The wound is wrong — the same wrongness as Maeve's, the dark eating the light at its edges, spreading, and when I press my hands to it and pour the healing silver in, it *drinks* the silver and keeps spreading, exactly the way Maeve's did, exactly the wound no thread of moonfire can close.

"No." I have him in my arms in the middle of the war, the battle roaring on around us, his blood hot through my fingers and going cold too fast. "No, no, you don't — Kane, look at me, you don't get to do this, you do not get to step in front of *one more* —"

"Worth it." His voice is already thin, his grey eyes already going distant, a small terrible smile pulling at his mouth. "Blade meant for you. Best calculation I've — ever made." His hand finds mine over the wound. "Don't — spend yourself on me. Door needs you. World needs — you save it, Wren. Not me. Leave me, I'm not —"

And down in my chest, the open bond — the road that runs both ways — flickers like a candle in a flood, his life guttering on the other end of it, the cold dark crawling up the wire toward me, and I understand with annihilating clarity that the choice Maeve warned me about, the door and the blood and the dying, isn't waiting for me at some future threshold.

It's here. It's now. It's a dying man in my arms and a power in my blood and a war I'm supposed to save, and only one of them can have everything I am.

chapters.title: What I Spent
chapters.body:
"Leave you," I repeat. "Leave you."

And something in me — the thing the trees made, the thing that learned in the kitchens to want nothing because wanting gets omegas killed — finally, completely breaks. Not into the tide. Into the opposite of it.

"I have spent my *whole life* being left," I tell him, fierce, my hands clamped over the dark wound that's drinking him. "Left at the back of the dark. Left to die in the trees. Left to believe I was nothing while someone who loved me lied to keep me asleep. I am the most *left* person who has ever lived, Kane Blackthorn, and I will burn down the door and the seal and the whole watching world before I add you to the list." The silver floods me, but not the borrowed tide, not the Queen's eager dark — the *other* one, the light that knit Rourke's flank and restarted my own heart, the healing that's mine, only ever mine, the part of the gift the Silver Queen never understood because she only ever wanted to take. "You poured yourself in front of that blade for me. So move over."

I pour myself into him.

Not a thread. Everything. I open the door in my chest that I've spent a month learning to hold shut and I let the light pour through it into the dark wound between his ribs — not the way I heal a cut, careful and measured, but the way you'd empty a lake into a fire, recklessly, all of it, my own strength going out of me and into him in a flood of moonfire so bright the whole valley turns to look.

The dark in the wound fights it. Shadowblood iron doesn't let go, Cassia said — and it doesn't, it drinks and drinks, and I pour faster, deeper, scraping the bottom of myself, spending strength I'll need for the door and the war and my own survival, spending it anyway, because the alternative is a world with him left cold in my arms and I have decided, with the whole of what I am, that I will not live in that world.

The light wins.

The dark wound closes. The cold crawling up the bond reverses, warms, *steadies* — his life catching like a struck flame, his grey eyes focusing, his hand tightening on mine in dawning horror as he realizes what I'm doing, what it's costing, how much of myself is pouring out of me to hold him here.

"Wren — *stop* —" He grabs my wrists. "You're spending too much, you'll have nothing left for the — *stop*, I'm healed, I'm here, *stop —*"

I stop.

And the valley tilts.

I've poured out too much. I knew I was; I did it anyway. The silver drains out of me all at once, the beacon that was holding our line guttering, and I sag against him, hollowed, the world going thin and gray at the edges the way it went in the trees the night they left me to die. Kane catches me — alive, whole, *healed*, his strength back, and mine gone into giving it — and I feel him understand, feel the terror of it through the bond: I traded. I traded my strength for his life in the middle of a war, and the trade has left the Moonborn empty.

The Shadowblood feel it too.

The instant the beacon dies, the grey tide that was recoiling from my light surges back into the valley with a sound like the sea coming in, and somewhere in the dark of it Malachai's voice rises, no longer furious, *delighted* again, because his patient terrible math has just come right at last:

"There it is," he croons, and the grey wolves part for him as he comes, and Kane's arms close around my hollowed body and can't possibly hold against what's coming. "She spent herself on the boy. Of course she did. The healers always do." His drinking-dark eyes find mine, and the thousand-year smile is back, and it is the worst thing I have ever seen. "Empty, and willing to die for someone — that's the most useful a Moonborn ever gets, little queen. You've saved me so much *trouble.*"

The last of my strength gone, the beacon dark, Kane roaring and the grey tide closing over us both —

— pale hands reach through the dark and close on me, and tear me out of Kane's arms, and drag me down into the cold toward the hollow where moonlight dies, and the last thing I hear before the dark takes me is the man the moon chose screaming my name down a bond gone suddenly, terrifyingly thin.

chapters.title: The Thing Behind the Door
chapters.body:
I wake on the threshold.

That's how I know it, before I open my eyes — the door in my blood isn't a crack anymore, isn't a hollow's worth of open. It's *here*, all around me, a vastness at my back like standing at the lip of a cliff that goes down forever, and the Silver Queen's voice isn't a whisper or even a croon now. It's a presence, enormous and patient and fond, filling the cold cleft where Malachai has brought me, close enough to touch.

I'm chained in black iron at the heart of the hollow, on a slab of stone that thrums under me, and I'm still hollowed out, still empty from what I spent on Kane — which is exactly why I'm still alive. Malachai needs me drained. Needs me with nothing left to fight him. Needs me *willing*, or near enough.

"You're awake." He's seated near me, almost companionable, turning a black iron blade — Cassia's twin — in his pale hands. "Good. I want you to understand it. The willing ones close the door behind themselves cleanly; the ignorant ones make such a *mess.*" He sets the blade down on the thrumming stone. "You've been told the seal binds 'an ancient evil.' That a Moonborn dies on the threshold to keep it shut. All true. But the old packs were cowards even in their stories — they never told the woken ones *what's actually behind the door.*"

He leans in, and the drinking-dark eyes hold mine, and he says it gently, the way you'd tell a child the truth about a grave.

"It's her. The first Moonborn. The Silver Queen." His smile widens. "Your grandmother, a hundred times over.
