The strongest of your line that ever lived — strong enough that when the world bound her, it couldn't kill her, could only seal her into the dark side of the moon and key the lock to her own descendants' sleeping blood." He spreads his hands. "She's not an *evil*, little queen. That's the packs' word. She's the truth they buried. A Moonborn who looked at a power that could heal and rule and *make wolves kneel*, and decided that mercy was a choice and so was its opposite. They called it monstrous. I call it *honest.*" His voice drops, reverent, a thousand years of devotion in it. "And she has spent ten centuries in the dark, waiting for a daughter strong enough to open the door from the inside — and to *choose*, when she meets what's behind it, whether to seal it shut again with her own death... or to let her grandmother out, and take her hand, and rule the way the line was always meant to."

The presence at my back leans in, fond and vast. *There you are, little queen,* the Queen murmurs, and now I understand it's not a memory or a madness — it's *her*, reaching through the opening door. *They left you in a kitchen. They threw you in the dirt. They lied to you about me to keep us apart. Open the door the rest of the way. Let me out. And I promise you, child of my blood — no one will ever leave you in the dark again.*

It would be so easy to hate it. To call it temptation and refuse it clean.

But I'm empty, and grieving, and chained, and the thing behind the door is the only ancestor I have ever had, and she is offering me the one thing eighteen years of cruelty hollowed out and left aching: *belonging.* Family. Someone of my own blood who looks at me and sees a queen instead of a stray. The most dangerous offer in the world, because it isn't a lie. She means it. She would let no one leave me in the dark again.

She'd just burn down everyone else to do it.

"You feel her," Malachai says, watching my face. "Good. Don't fight it; you'll only lose, and lose more of yourself each time, same as the tide. Just listen. She loves you. She's the only one in this whole story who never needed you to be small." He rises, takes up the black blade, and his voice gentles toward the killing-tender place I heard at the festival. "Here's all that's left to decide, little queen, and you'll decide it soon, because I've found your one true weakness and I'm going to use it." He smiles. "It isn't pride. It isn't power. You'd die for those who love you without a second's thought — you proved it on the boy." The blade catches what little light there is. "Which means I don't have to make *you* willing at all. I just have to start hurting the people you'd die to save, one at a time, where you can watch — and you'll open the door yourself, with your own blood, just to make me stop."

Far off, up through the cleft, muffled by stone and dark, I hear it — the war, drawing closer, a familiar storm-scent threading down the cold air, a bond gone thin but not gone, pulling, *pulling*, getting nearer.

Malachai hears it too. His smile sharpens.

"Ah," he says. "Right on time. He always comes, doesn't he? They both do — the coward and the kind one, racing each other into the dark for you." He sets the point of the black blade against the thrumming stone and looks toward the sound of Kane Blackthorn fighting his way down into the hollow where moonlight dies. "Let's find out how much of your blood it takes to open a door, shall we — when the price of keeping it shut is watching me gut the man the moon chose, on this stone, in front of you."

chapters.title: They Always Come
chapters.body:
Kane comes down into the dark the way I came down off the ridge — like something falling that has decided to land on a specific person.

I hear them before I see them: the war following him in, the cleft mouth shattering, Nightriver and Blackthorn pouring into the hollow where no pack has set foot in a thousand years. And at the front of it, two Alphas who should be enemies, fighting back to back through the grey tide — the coward and the kind one, exactly as Malachai said, racing each other into the dark for me.

"WREN!" Kane's voice, and the bond between us — thin, frayed, nearly spent — flares at the sight of me chained on the thrumming stone. He's healed. Whole. The life I poured into him carrying him through the grey wolves like a storm. And the horror on his face when he sees the black slab, the iron, the vastness opening at my back, is worth more than I want it to be.

"Stop there," Malachai says, almost bored, and sets the point of his blade against my throat.

Kane stops. The whole rescue stops, jammed in the cleft, grey wolves at their flanks and a blade at my pulse.

"Better." Malachai doesn't even look at the armies. He looks at me, and he draws the black iron — gently, almost a caress — across the back of my chained hand, and the dark blade drinks a thin line of my blood, and the thrumming stone *drinks the blood from the blade*, and the vastness at my back *shudders with pleasure.* "You feel that, little queen? The door likes the taste. One hand's worth, and it's wider already. Imagine what the rest of you would do." He raises his voice for Kane now. "Here's the game, Alpha. She can close this door. The old woman told her how — a Moonborn spends her life on the threshold and the dark stays shut another thousand years. Noble. Tidy." The blade traces another line; the stone drinks; the dark sighs. "*Or* — I bleed her slowly, here, in front of you, and the door opens the rest of the way on its own, and what comes out the other side is the strongest Moonborn who ever lived, who happens to be very fond of this one and very *un*fond of all of you." He smiles. "So. Who wants to make her willing first? Shall I start cutting the kind one? The coward? Shall I send for the kitchen-mistress she spared, and open *that* old wound?" 

"Take me instead." Kane and Rourke say it in the same breath, from opposite sides of the cleft, and then glare at each other for it, and despite everything, chained and bleeding on a slab of evil, I love them both so much in that instant it nearly closes my throat.

"Oh, I intend to take *all* of you," Malachai says pleasantly. "That's rather the point."

And here is the thing Malachai got wrong — the one thing, the thing a creature that's spent a thousand years serving a queen who only knew how to *take* could never understand.

He thinks my weakness is that I'd die for the people I love.

He's right. But he thinks dying is the worst thing he can make me do.

I look across the dark at Kane — at the bond between us, thin and frayed and nearly spent, but *open*, still open from the rite, the road that runs both ways — and I stop trying to hold the last of my strength back. I stop trying to save myself for the door, the war, the future. I let Kane feel, down the open bond, exactly what I'm about to do, so that he understands, so that he's ready, so that for once in this whole story the person I love isn't blindsided by my sacrifice.

*I'm not going to open the door,* I tell him down the wire, without words, the way the open bond lets me now. *And I'm not going to let him cut you to make me. There's a third thing. Maeve gave it to me. I'm so sorry. Be ready to catch what's left.*

I feel his horror bloom down the bond — *Wren, no, NO, whatever you're about to —*

— and I close my bleeding hand around the black iron blade at my own throat, and I drive it down, with the last of my own strength, into the thrumming stone.

Not into me. Into the *door.*

Maeve's lore, the deepest of it, the thing under everything else she poured into me dying: *a Moonborn's blood opens the door — but a Moonborn's blood, given freely, in full will, to the stone itself instead of to the dark — that's the lock. That's the death on the threshold. You don't wait for them to bleed you, child. You give it. Willing. All at once.*

The stone drinks the blade, and my blood with it, and the vastness at my back stops sighing with pleasure and *screams* —

— and the hollow fills with silver light, pouring up out of the slab, out of me, the door beginning to slam instead of open, and Malachai recoils with a howl of a thousand years of fury, and the last thing I see before the light takes everything is Kane Blackthorn tearing across the cleft toward the stone, toward me, his mouth shaping a word I can't hear over the screaming of a queen being sealed back into the dark with the freely given blood of the granddaughter she was so sure she'd already won.

chapters.title: The Dark Devours Its Own
chapters.body:
The door does not close quietly.

A thousand years of the Silver Queen's fury pours up out of the slab as my freely given blood drags her back toward the dark, and the hollow becomes a storm of silver light and screaming shadow. I'm pinned at the center of it on the thrumming stone, the lock turning, my life going out of me into the seal the way I poured it into Kane — except there's no stopping this one, no taking it back, the sacrifice given and the door answering.

Through the storm I see the war finish pouring into the cleft. I see Rourke and the Nightriver wolves throw themselves between the grey tide and the stone, buying the seal time with their bodies. I see Malachai, a thousand years of devotion turned to a thousand years of rage, clawing toward me through his own queen's light, trying to reach the slab, to break the rite, to spill the rest of my blood the wrong way before the door shuts.

And I see Kane reach me.

He hits the stone and gets his arms around me, and the bond between us — thin, frayed, nearly spent — flares at the contact, and I feel him take in the whole truth of it in one breath: that I'm not bleeding to death from a wound, that I *gave* this, that the lock is my life and the life is leaving and there's no blade to pull out, no dark to heal, nothing to fight, only a queen dying willingly on a stone to keep the world from the thing in her blood.

"Why," he says, wrecked, holding me as the light pours out of me. "Why didn't you *tell* me, why didn't you let me —"

"Because you'd have done it instead." I can barely get the words out. "And it has to be Moonborn blood. It was only ever going to be mine. I just — I wanted to choose it. For once. Not the moon, not him, not the kitchens, not even you." I find his eyes through the storm. "Me. I chose. I'm so sorry. But I *chose* —"

"WREN BLOOD OF SAOIRSE."

The voice is Bram Ravenscar's, and it cuts across the hollow like a thrown spear.

He's forced his way in behind the rescue, Cassia at his side, and he's not looking at the war — he's looking at the slab, at the door closing, at a thousand years of his family's work slamming shut in front of him. He has served the dark since before I was born, his whole bloodline a knife pointed at the sleeping Moonborn, waiting, and he is watching his prize seal itself away forever, and the genial mask is gone, replaced by the naked greed underneath.

"The door's still open!" Bram lunges toward the stone, toward me, toward the closing seal, a blade of his own in his hand. "There's time — her blood spilled to the dark, not the stone — *I* can finish it, I can free the Queen, she'll reward the hand that —"

He never reaches me.

Malachai gets there first.

The Shadowblood lord turns from the slab, from his own rage, and seizes Bram Ravenscar by the throat mid-lunge — and the look on Malachai's face is the purest contempt I have ever seen. "*Reward* you?" he hisses. "A thousand years I have served her, in the dark, in the cold, faithful — and you think she'd hand the world to a pack-alpha who learned her name from a *book* and married his way toward power?" His pale hand tightens. "The Queen has no use for the ambitious, Ravenscar. Only the faithful. You were always just a knife she let me sharpen. And knives —" 

Bram's body hits the thrumming stone, and the dark takes him the way it takes everything that isn't useful anymore, and Cassia screams her father's name into the storm.

"— get discarded when the cutting's done," Malachai finishes, and turns back toward me, toward the closing door, his thousand years of devotion burning, ignoring the dead alpha at his feet and the screaming daughter and the whole war, because none of it matters to him but the queen in the dark and the granddaughter sealing her away.

But it costs him a moment. The moment it takes to discard Bram. The moment his back is turned.

And Rourke — bloodied, three wounds deep, fighting his way to the stone the whole time — uses it. He throws himself the last of the distance, not at Malachai, but between Malachai and Kane, who's kneeling defenseless over my dying body with no eyes for anything but me. Rourke takes the Shadowblood lord's reaching strike full across the chest — the strike meant to tear Kane off the slab — and goes down hard between us and the dark, amber eyes finding mine one last fierce time as he falls.

"Told you," Rourke gasps, blood on his teeth, sprawled across the stone, having put his body between the man I love and the thing that would kill him. "Patient man. Worth — staying for." His eyes flutter. "Make it — mean something."

And Cassia Ravenscar, her father dead, her allies turning on each other, her wedding and her throne and her whole poisoned future collapsing into the storm — picks up her father's fallen blade, and looks at the kneeling, defenseless back of the Alpha who was supposed to be hers, and makes her last play.

chapters.title: Cassia's Last Play
chapters.body:
I'm dying, and I see her move, and there's nothing left in me to stop it with.

I've poured my strength into Kane in the valley. I've poured my life into the stone on the threshold. I'm empty in a way I've never been, the silver in me draining into the seal, and Cassia is raising her father's blade over Kane's unguarded back, and Kane is bent over me with no eyes for anything but my dying face, and Rourke is down, and there is no one — no one left between the man I love and the woman who already put one blade in him tonight.

No one but me. And I have nothing left.

So I use the one thing I have that isn't strength.

I use the bond.

It's nearly spent — thin, frayed, almost gone — but it's *open*, the road that runs both ways, and I don't have the power to throw light or shift or move my own failing body. But I can do the small thing. The last thing. I can pour what little is left of my attention down the wire and *scream into Kane's mind* — not a word, not light, just the raw animal warning, the thing a mate feels in the marrow:

*BEHIND YOU.*

Kane moves before he can think.

He's spent a month learning to throw his body into the path of danger; the instinct is bone-deep now, and it doesn't fail him here. He turns, still half-over me, and Cassia's blade — aimed for the center of his unguarded back, for his heart — buries instead in his shoulder as he twists, and he roars, and his hand closes around her wrist, and the two of them grapple over my dying body in the silver storm.

"You were *mine*," Cassia shrieks, wild now, all the silk burned off her, clawing at him. "The throne was mine, the pack was mine, you were *promised* — and you threw it all away for a kitchen stray who's *dying anyway* —"

"She was never a kitchen stray." Kane wrenches the blade from her hand, and his voice is the Alpha voice, the one the clearing leaned into, cold and final. "She was the best thing the moon ever made, and your family spent eighteen years teaching her she was nothing so you could murder her in her sleep. You didn't lose me to a stray, Cassia." He throws her back, away from the stone, away from me. "You lost me the moment I learned what your blood did to her mother. There was never a version of this where you won. There was only ever how long it took me to stop being a coward and see it."

Cassia hits the cleft floor, scrambling, and for one moment I think she'll run — that she'll vanish into the dark the way the Shadowblood do, that she'll be a loose thread in the years to come.

But Malachai has turned back from Bram's discarding, and his thousand-year-patient eyes fall on the screaming, scrambling daughter of the man he just killed, and he sees, the way I see, that she is exactly what he called her family: a knife, no longer useful, with the cutting done.

"You Ravenscars," Malachai sighs, almost gentle, "never did understand. The dark doesn't *reward.* The dark *consumes.*" And he doesn't even break stride toward me as the shadow takes her — Cassia Ravenscar, who smiled while I screamed in the dirt, who poisoned the man she meant to marry, who came to my festival to watch me die in the front row — swallowed by the same dark her family served, her last scream lost in the storm of a queen being sealed away.

The other woman is gone. Bram is gone. The throne they killed my mother for, the wedding, the alliance, the whole patient poisoned plot of the Ravenscar line — gone, devoured by the very thing it served, on the threshold of the door it spent eighteen years trying to open.

But Malachai is still coming.

And the door is still closing.

And I am still dying on the stone, the silver draining out of me into the seal, the lock turning, my life the price and the price almost paid — and the vastness at my back, the Silver Queen being dragged back into the dark with my freely given blood, gathers herself for one last thing, because a thousand years of patience does not go quietly, and she has one card left that Malachai's blades and Cassia's poison never had.

She has love. The same weapon I used.

*Don't die, little queen,* the Silver Queen whispers, fond and vast and desperate now, her voice pouring up out of the closing dark. *You don't have to. Look — your blood opened me a door wide enough for both of us. Take my hand. Take my power — all of it, a thousand years of it — and you don't have to die on this stone. You can stand up. You can save the kind one bleeding beside you. You can heal the coward you love. You can walk out of this hollow a goddess, and no one — no one, ever again — will leave you in the dark.* The dark reaches for me, warm and certain. *I am the only family you have ever had. Don't seal me away and die alone. Let me out, and live, and let us never be alone again.*

And the terrible thing — the thing that nearly ends the world — is that I am dying, and grieving, and so very tired of being left, and she is not lying, and her hand is *right there.*

chapters.title: Not As a Queen
chapters.body:
Here is what the Silver Queen never understood, in a thousand years of waiting in the dark.

She thinks belonging is a hand that holds you so no one can take you away. She thinks family is a power so great that you never have to be left again. She spent ten centuries believing that the answer to being thrown in the dirt is to become the thing that does the throwing — to climb so high that no one can ever reach up and pull you down.

And she's offering me exactly that. A goddess's power. A family of my own blood. A world where Wren Calloway is so strong that the kitchens and the trees and the clearing could never happen again, because no one would dare.

It's the most tempting thing I have ever been offered, because it's built out of the exact shape of the wound.

I reach for her hand.

I feel Kane go rigid beside me — feel him feel it, down the open bond, my fingers lifting toward the dark, my dying body leaning toward the one offer that could save it. He's bleeding from Cassia's blade in his shoulder, his life pouring out beside mine, and he has no power to stop me, no light, no strength, nothing but a bond gone nearly to nothing and the voice the moon gave him.

So he uses it. The small thing. The last thing. The same weapon I used to warn him.

"Wren." Just my name, ragged, down the wire and out of his bleeding mouth both. "Don't take her hand."

"She's the only family I've ever —"

"I know." He drags himself closer across the stone, into my fading sight, storm-grey eyes finding mine through the storm of silver and dark. "I know what she's offering. I can feel it. A power so big nobody could ever leave you in the dark again." His hand finds my failing one — not to stop it, he hasn't the strength, just to hold it. "But that's not the opposite of being left, Wren. That's just being alone at the top instead of the bottom. That's what she *did.* She got so strong no one could reach her, and a thousand years later she's still in the dark, still alone, still trying to use a frightened girl's grief to get one hand to hold." His grip tightens, the last of him in it. "Don't be the queen she made herself into. I don't want the queen. I never wanted the queen."

"Then what," I breathe, the dark warm at my back, her hand a hair from mine, "do you want."

"You." Simple. Final. The truest thing in the hollow. "Wren. The kitchen girl who held my stare while she was dying because she wanted her face to be the last thing somebody remembered. The one who spared Mara because she'd rather die than let the dark win an inch through her own anger. The one who poured her whole life into a man who didn't deserve it because she decided no one she loved would be left." His voice breaks completely. "Come back to me. Not as a goddess. Not as a queen. Not as the strongest thing in the world." His forehead drops against mine, both of us dying on the stone, the bond a thread, the dark a breath away. "As Wren. Just come back as Wren. I'll spend the rest of my life — however much of it I've got left, even if it's the next five minutes — making sure you are never, ever left in the dark again. Not because I'm powerful. Because I *chose* you, and I'm not going to stop."

The Silver Queen's hand hangs in the dark, offering me a thousand years of never being left.

And the man the moon chose holds my dying hand on a stone and offers me the next five minutes, and his whole heart, and a choice.

It's not even close.

"No," I tell my grandmother, the strongest Moonborn who ever lived, the queen who became the dark because she couldn't bear to be small. I say it gently, because I understand her, because the only difference between us is one stone-cold dying man who learned to be brave too late and just in time. "I'm not going to be alone at the top. I'd rather be Wren in the dark with him than a goddess in the dark with you." I close my reaching hand — into a fist, away from her, around Kane's instead. "You were right about one thing, though. No one's ever going to leave me in the dark again." The silver in me flares, the last of it, pouring into the seal, the lock turning the final turn. "Because I'm not going to *be* in the dark. I'm taking the door with me."

The Silver Queen *screams* —

— and I give the stone the last of my life, freely, willing, all of it, and the door slams shut on a thousand years of dark with the granddaughter's blood as the lock and the granddaughter's choice as the key, and the hollow detonates with silver light, and the seal holds, and the Queen is gone, sealed away for another thousand years —

— and Malachai, mid-stride, his thousand years of devotion ending in the same instant as the queen he served, throws back his head and lets out a howl of pure annihilating loss as the dark he's served all his life is torn out of the world —

— and I feel my own heart, on the stone, in Kane's hands, forget how to beat for the second time in my life.

Only this time, there's no ancient fond voice rising up to save me.

This time the door is shut. The Queen is gone. The power is sealed. And the price has come due, exactly as Maeve said it would: a Moonborn, freely spending her life on the threshold, to keep the world from the dark.

I gave it. All of it.

The silver fades. The storm dies. And Wren Calloway lies still on the stone in the arms of the man the moon chose, in a hollow gone suddenly, terribly quiet, with the door shut and the world saved and her life the price that bought it.

chapters.title: The Words Reversed
chapters.body:
*(Kane)*

She stops breathing in my arms, and the world ends.

I have been an Alpha since the night my father died. I have held a slaughtered pack together with my bare hands. I have made myself into the most feared wolf in the Crescent so that no one would ever do to my people what was done to them. And none of it — none of it — matters, kneeling on this stone with Wren Calloway gone still against my chest and the silver fading out of her like a tide going out forever.

I felt her die. The bond carried it. One moment she was there, blazing, *choosing* — and then the door slammed and the price came due and the wire between us went slack, and the silence where she used to be is the loudest thing I have ever heard.

"No." I'm bleeding from Cassia's blade in my shoulder, my own life going out of me onto the stone, and I don't care. "No. Not like this. You don't — you spared *Mara*, you poured yourself into *me*, you don't get to give everything to everyone and keep nothing for yourself, you don't get to *leave* —"

And then I remember.

The ridge. The lore Maeve poured into her, the thing she found that frightened her more than dying. *The Reclaiming.* The rejector kneeling, surrendering the pride that let him say the words, speaking their reverse, and pouring his own life down the bond to heal what he tore. It can't be done by the rejected. Only by the one who rejected. *Only by me.*

She told me the cost. She told me it would take everything. She made me promise we'd argue about it after we won.

We won. There's nothing left to argue about.

I lower her onto the stone, and I do the thing I have never done for anyone, the thing my whole rebuilt life was structured to never require — I kneel. All the way down. I let go of the Alpha, the pride, the feared thing I made myself into, the weapon-counting stranger who stood on a dais and weighed a frightened girl against a war and chose wrong. I set all of it down on the stone beside her body.

And I say the reverse of the words.

"I, Kane Blackthorn." My voice shakes. The hollow has gone silent around me — the war, the wolves, even Malachai's howling — all of it falling away until there's only the stone and the still girl and the moon somewhere far above the dark. "Who rejected you in front of my pack because I was a coward and a murderer's fool — who threw the only thing the moon ever gave me into the dirt to buy a peace with the people who killed your mother —"

The bond stirs. Faint. A thread where a thread shouldn't be.

"— I *reclaim* you." I press my bleeding hand to her cold chest, over the place the bond used to live, and I pour. Not light — I have no light, that was always hers. I pour *myself*. My life, my strength, the years I have left, down the thread of the bond and into the wound my own words tore, the way she poured herself into me in the valley, recklessly, all of it, scraping the bottom. "I claim you, Wren Calloway, as my mate. As my Luna. As my better, and my equal, and the bravest soul the moon ever made. I take back every word. I give you everything I have left to take them back."

The thread thickens. Warms. *Pulls.*

And somewhere far down it, in the dark place between living and dead where she's gone, I feel her — faint, fading, but *there* — and I pour harder, my own sight going gray at the edges, my own heart laboring, Cassia's wound and the Reclaiming both bleeding me white.

"Come back," I beg her, down the bond, out loud, both. "Not as a queen. Not as a goddess. I told you. Just come back as Wren. I'm not asking the moon. I'm asking *you*. Choose it. Choose me. Choose to *stay* — you've spent your whole life wanting one person to want you to stay, and I am here, on my knees, wanting it more than my own life — so *stay* —"

The hollow shudders.

Malachai's howl cuts back in — closer now, the Shadowblood lord mad with the loss of his queen, hurling himself across the cleft toward the stone, toward us, because if he can't have his queen then he will at least make sure the granddaughter who sealed her stays dead, will tear me off her body before the Reclaiming can finish —

— and Rourke Vane, bleeding from a dozen wounds, who has held the line this whole time on a life he should have already lost, throws himself one last time between us and the dark, and catches Malachai, and holds.

"Finish it," Rourke roars at me over his shoulder, grappling the thing that wants us dead, his amber eyes blazing with the last of him. "Bring her back, Blackthorn — I held the line so you could — so *FINISH IT* —"

And down the warming, thickening bond, in the dark between life and death, I feel Wren Calloway hear my voice.

And I feel her stop.

And I feel her — impossibly, against the door and the price and the silence — *turn around.*

chapters.title: The Turning
chapters.body:
The dark between living and dead is quiet, and it is not unkind.

I've done the thing. The door is shut. The price is paid. And here, in the after, there's no kitchen and no clearing and no trees — just a great soft silver quiet, and I am so tired, and it would be so easy to let it carry me out the way the tide goes out, gentle, final, *done.* I gave everything. I'm allowed to rest. I earned the rest.

I am, for the first time in my whole life, not being left.

I'm the one leaving.

And then, from very far away, down a thread I thought I'd spent to nothing, a voice.

*Come back. Not as a queen. Not as a goddess. Just come back as Wren.*

Kane.

*I'm not asking the moon. I'm asking you. Choose it. Choose me. Choose to stay.*

And under his voice, threaded through it, his *life* — pouring down the bond into me, warm and reckless and total, the same way I poured mine into him in the valley, scraping the bottom of himself, spending the years he has left to drag me back from the soft silver quiet. I can feel what it's costing him. I can feel him dying to do it.

*You've spent your whole life wanting one person to want you to stay,* he says, *and I am here, on my knees, wanting it more than my own life — so stay —*

And I understand, in the quiet, the thing the Silver Queen never learned and the thing the trees tried to teach me and the thing Maeve died believing:

I spent my whole life wanting someone to want me to stay.

And someone does. Not a power. Not a hand that holds you so high no one can reach you. A man on his knees on a stone, who threw me away once and learned, too late and just in time, how to be brave — pouring his own life into the dark after me, not to make me a goddess, not to make me strong, just to make me *stay.*

I am done being left. And I am done being the one who leaves.

I turn around.

It costs more than the dying did. The soft silver quiet doesn't want to let go, and the door is shut and the price is paid and by every law of the seal I should be the lock now, dead on the threshold forever. But Maeve poured a thousand years of lore into me, and somewhere in the deepest of it is the thing the old packs never knew — because no woken Moonborn ever had both the complete lore *and* a mate willing to reclaim her in the same hour:

*A life freely given seals the door. But a bond freely reclaimed — given back, both ways, in full will — the moon will take* that *as the lock instead. Not one death. Two lives, bound, holding the dark shut between them. Love as the seal. It has never been done, because it has never been possible, because it requires a Moonborn willing to die and a mate willing to die to bring her back, choosing each other across the threshold at the very same moment.*

We're both dying. We're both choosing. At the very same moment.

So I reach back down the thread toward the man pouring his life into me, and I stop letting the quiet carry me out, and I grab the bond with both hands, and I *pull* —

— and I take his life that he's pouring into me, and instead of letting it just keep me from dying, I pour my own back the other way, the two of us spending everything into each other across the threshold at once, a circle instead of a sacrifice, a bond instead of a death —

— and the moon, which has watched this whole long story from above the dark, which threaded us together and watched a coward tear us apart and watched a queen choose the world over godhood and a feared Alpha kneel and reverse his own cruelty with his dying breath —

— the moon answers.

Silver light erupts up out of the stone — not the draining, dying silver of the sealing, but something *rising*, something being born — and in the dark between life and death I feel the bond catch fire and weld whole, truly whole, the way it was meant to be the night the moon first drew it, and I feel myself stop being the lock-by-death and become something the world has not seen in a thousand years:

A Moonborn, fully woken, fully ascended, *alive* — with the dark sealed not by her death but by the living bond at the center of her, love made into the thing that holds the door shut.

I open my eyes on the stone.

They are not grey. They are not the borrowed silver of the tide. They are my own — moon-bright, ascended, *mine* — and the hollow is full of dawn-colored light pouring off my risen body, and Kane is collapsing beside me with the last of his life spent, and across the cleft Malachai turns from Rourke's failing grip to see the thing he was promised would stay dead rising off the stone in full ascension, the Moonborn Luna, alive and blazing and *furious* —

— and I look at the creature who hunted me my whole life, who took Maeve, who served the dark that wanted to use my grief as a door, and I do not reach for the tide in anger, because I don't need to anymore.

The light is mine now. All of it. Freely. Forever.

"You wanted to know whose voice I'd been hearing," I tell Malachai, rising to my feet in a hollow full of dawn, the Luna the old packs spent a thousand years trying to bury. "It's mine. It was always going to be mine." I raise my hand, and the moon comes down into it. "Go be with your queen."

chapters.title: The Cost Comes Back Around
chapters.body:
The light I bring down on Malachai is not the tide.

The tide was always borrowed — the Silver Queen's power, reached for in anger, costing me a little more of myself each time. This is different. This is mine, the way the healing was always mine, the part of the gift the Queen never understood because she only knew how to take and I have spent my whole life learning how to give. It pours out of me clean and dawn-bright and *kind*, even now, even as a weapon — and it doesn't tear Malachai apart.

It simply ends him. The way dawn ends a shadow. The dark he served is sealed, the queen he loved is gone, and a creature made of devotion to a thing that no longer exists has nothing left to be. He looks at me in the rising light — a thousand years of patience, of hunger, of faith — and at the very end there's something almost like relief in the drinking dark of his eyes as the moon's light fills them and they are, for the first time in ten centuries, not empty.

"Thank you," Malachai says, surprised — and is gone.

The hollow falls silent. The grey tide, leaderless, queenless, the dark torn out of the world, breaks and scatters into a dawn they can no longer bear. The war is over. The door is shut. The Silver Queen is sealed for another thousand years, and this time the lock is not a dead girl on a stone.

This time the lock is a living bond, blazing at the center of me, holding the dark shut the way a keystone holds an arch — and I am alive, ascended, the Moonborn Luna, standing in a hollow full of the first dawn that's reached this place in a thousand years.

And it isn't enough. Because Kane is dying.

I drop to my knees beside him on the stone — the same stone where I died, where he reversed the words, where he poured his whole life down the bond to bring me back. And that's the cruelty of it, the mirror I should have seen coming: the Reclaiming cost him everything, exactly as Maeve's lore promised. He gave his life to bring me back from the dark. The moon took our bond as the seal and *spared* me — pulled me back ascended and whole.

But the price the Reclaiming demands is the rejector's life. And Kane Blackthorn paid it in full.

"No." I gather him up the way he gathered me. His grey eyes are going distant, the way mine went, his life poured out into the bond and into me and into the sealing. Cassia's wound and the Reclaiming both, bleeding him white on the stone. "No, no — you don't get to do this, this is *my* line, I'm the one who gives everything away, you don't get to —"

"Worked, though." A small, terrible, satisfied smile, the same one he wore taking Cassia's blade for me. "You're — back. Ascended. Alive. I can feel it." His hand finds the silver streak at my temple, like the very first time, like the rite, like the storm. "Reclaimed you. Took back — every word. Worth — every —"

"*Stop saying it was worth it.*" The dawn-light pours off me, frantic now, and I press my hands to him and *pour* — the healing, the real healing, mine, all of it — the way I poured into him in the valley. "I'm ascended, I have all of it now, I can heal anything, I can —"

But the healing pours into him and does not catch.

Because a life spent in the Reclaiming isn't a wound. It isn't dark iron or poison or a torn bond. It's a price already paid, given freely to the moon, and you cannot heal back a thing that's been *given* — I know this, Maeve's lore knows this, the same way my freely given life couldn't be taken back from the stone. He didn't lose his life. He *spent* it. On me. And the moon does not give refunds, not even to a risen queen.

"Maeve," I sob, "Maeve, you gave me everything you knew, give me *this*, tell me how to —"

And Maeve's lore answers. The deepest of it. The thing the old packs never knew because it was never possible until this exact hollow, this exact dawn, this exact pair of fools who each chose to die for the other at the very same moment.

*Two lives, bound, holding the dark shut between them. Love as the seal. It requires both. The lock is not one of you. It is the bond. And a bond is not a bond if one end of it is dead.*

The moon took our bond as the seal. The seal needs *both* of us alive to hold — a living wire, both ends burning. The moon spared me to be one end of it.

It cannot let the other end die.

I look up into the dawn pouring down through the broken roof of the hollow — the first moonlight-turned-sunlight to reach this dead place in a thousand years — and I understand what I have to offer, and what I have to risk, and that for the first time in this whole long story the choice that saves us isn't a sacrifice at all.

It's a claim.

chapters.title: I Claim You Back
chapters.body:
He reclaimed me. Now I reclaim him.

That's the thing the old packs never knew, because no rejected wolf was ever a risen Moonborn with a thousand years of lore and a freshly welded bond burning at her center. The Reclaiming runs one way — the rejector kneels and pours his life to take back his words. But a bond is a road that runs *both* ways, and the moon has just taken ours as the lock that holds the dark, and a lock with one dead end is no lock at all.

So the moon cannot let him die. It only has to be *shown* the other end of the wire is willing to burn.

I lower Kane onto the stone, the way he lowered me. I press my hand to his chest, over the bond, the way he pressed his to mine. And I — the Moonborn Luna, ascended, blazing, the strongest thing the world has seen in a thousand years — I kneel.

All the way down. Over the man who threw me in the dirt and learned, too late and just in time, how to be brave. I set down the queen, the power, the ascension, all of it, the way he set down his pride. And I say the words that have never been said, because they have never been possible: not the rejector reclaiming the rejected, but the *risen* claiming the *dying*, life flowing back up the wire he poured it down.

"I, Wren Calloway." The dawn pours off me into him. "Moonborn, Luna of the Crescent, who you threw into the dirt and who chose, anyway, against the whole dark and a thousand years of being left — to come back for *you.*" My hand burns silver over his fading heart. "You spent your life to reclaim me. So I'm spending my ascension to reclaim *you.* I claim you, Kane Blackthorn, as my mate. As my equal — not my owner, never again my owner — as the fool who knelt in the snow and the coward who learned to be brave and the man who poured his whole life down a bond just to make me *stay.*" The light pours, two-way now, the wire burning at both ends. "I take you back. I'm not asking the moon. I'm telling her. This bond is her lock, and I will not hold the dark shut with a dead man's name. *Both ends burn, or neither does.*"

And I pour my ascension down the bond into him — not to heal a wound, you can't heal a price, but to *match* it. He gave his life to the moon to bring me back. So I give the moon my newly risen life right back, the whole blazing thousand-year-woken weight of it, and I lay it on the scale beside his, and I make the moon choose: take both of us, and lose your lock, and let the dark back into the world —

— or let us both live, bound, burning, the living seal you just made us, and keep your door shut the way it has never been shut before: not with a grave, but with a marriage.

For one endless moment, the moon weighs it.

I feel the soft silver quiet reach for us both, the threshold opening, the price calling its due — and I hold the line, the way Rourke held the line, the way Kane held me, the way Maeve held a thousand years of lore for a queen who hadn't been born yet. I hold both ends of the bond burning in my fists and I do not let the quiet take either of us, and I pour, and I claim, and I *will not let go* —

— and the moon, which loves a bond above all things, which made the Moonborn to unite and not to die, which has watched her own gift twisted into a leash and a weapon and a tide for a thousand years and ached to see it become, just once, the thing she meant it to be —

— the moon lets us live.

The light changes. The draining stops. And I feel Kane's life, on the other end of the wire, catch — not the borrowed flicker I gave him in the valley, but a true flame, fed from the bond itself now, from the lock the two of us have become. His heart steadies under my hand. His grey eyes focus. The gray edges pull back from his sight, and from mine, and the dawn pouring through the broken hollow falls on two living wolves kneeling on a stone where, by every law the old packs knew, there should be only graves.

Kane's hand comes up, shaking, and covers mine where it burns silver over his heart.

"You came back," he breathes. The exact words he said over my body. "Not as a queen." A wrecked, disbelieving, joyful sound. "You came back to *kneel* for me. You absolute —"

"Don't get used to it." I'm laughing and sobbing at once, the dawn pouring off both of us, the bond blazing whole and two-way and *ours* at last. "I've knelt for exactly one person in my entire life and you're looking at the last time it happens." I press my forehead to his, both of us alive, the dark sealed, the door shut, the lock a living thing made of the most unlikely love in the Crescent. "From here on, Kane Blackthorn, we stand up. *Together.* That's the whole deal. That's the only deal I'll ever take again."

"Together," he agrees, against my mouth, the word a vow, the bond singing it back from both ends at once.

And in a hollow where moonlight died for a thousand years, in the first dawn to reach it since the world went wrong, the Alpha who rejected the omega and the Luna who came back from the dead for him — kneel on the stone in each other's arms, alive, bound, the living seal of a shut door, and finally, *finally*, close the last of the distance the moon first asked them to close in a clearing a lifetime ago.

chapters.title: What Climbed Out of the Dark
chapters.body:
We climb out of the hollow into the first real dawn either of us has seen in what feels like a hundred years.

Kane leans on me, and I lean on him — the bond between us doing what bonds were always meant to do, each end holding the other up — and we come up out of the cleft where moonlight died, into the grey light of a morning that has no idea the world nearly ended in the dark below it. The north valley spreads out before us, churned and bloodied and quiet, the war over, the grey tide gone, the packs of the Crescent scattered across the slopes in the exhausted aftermath of having survived a thing they'll spend the rest of their lives failing to explain.

Rourke is alive.

I find him first, because the bond isn't the only thing in me that's been afraid — he's propped against a boulder where his wolves dragged him clear, a dozen wounds closed badly and bleeding through, gray-faced, more dead than alive, having held Malachai off the stone with the last of himself so Kane could finish. But his chest rises. His amber eyes crack open as I drop to my knees beside him, and the dawn-light pours off my risen skin onto his ruined body, and this — *this* I can heal, this is a wound and not a price, and I pour the moon into him until the bleeding stops and the gray pulls back and the Alpha of Nightriver draws a breath that doesn't rattle.

"Told you," Rourke rasps, the ghost of his crooked smile. "Patient man. Worth — staying alive for." His eyes move from me to Kane, leaning on my shoulder, alive, the bond blazing between us plain enough for any wolf to feel. And whatever it costs him, he lets it go — I see him let it go, the last of the wanting, traded for something cleaner. "You came back ascended and your first move was to heal the man who lost the girl. Goddess, Wren." He huffs a laugh that turns into a wince. "He'd better deserve you. I'll be checking."

"You'll be checking from very close," I tell him, and I mean it. "Nightriver bound itself to me, remember? You put the cord on my wrist yourself. You don't get rid of me now, Rourke Vane. You're my pack. You're my *friend.* That's a thing I get to have now, apparently — friends — and I'm keeping the first one I made."

Something eases in his face that I don't think anyone's eased in a long time. "Yeah," he says quietly. "All right. Friends." And then, because he's Rourke: "I still think I'd have been better at the romance."

"You absolutely would have," I agree. "He knows it too. It's good for him."

And then the rest of them come.

They come up the valley in the grey dawn — Blackthorn and Nightriver and the alliance packs that doubted, the survivors of a war fought over a kitchen stray, drawn to the thing climbing out of the hollow trailing dawn-light like a banner. They've felt it. Every wolf in the Crescent felt the dark torn out of the world, felt the moon answer, felt something rise off a stone that hasn't risen in a thousand years. And they come, and they see her — see *me* — silver-eyed and blazing and *alive*, with the most feared Alpha in the Crescent leaning on her shoulder, and they understand what they're looking at before anyone has to say it.

The kitchen mouse. The stray's girl. The omega they made stand at the back, in the dark, where the firelight doesn't reach. The one they dragged out the gate to die.

The Moonborn Luna.

And the packs of the Crescent kneel.

Not the way the yard knelt when I walked back through the gate — that was fear, the instinct to bow to a dangerous thing. This is different. This is the bone-deep thing that goes through a pack when it recognizes, all at once, the wolf the moon meant to lead it — the old recognition, the one the Silver Queen poisoned a thousand years ago and the one I just spent everything to win back clean. They go down into the bloodied grass of the valley, pack by pack, alpha by alpha, and they bow their heads to the omega who climbed out of the dark with the dawn on her shoulders.

And at the edge of the crowd, gray-faced and trembling, I see Mara go down with the rest of them — and this time, when our eyes meet, there's no calculation in hers, no gentleness-with-a-blade. Just a frightened old woman kneeling to a queen, finally, completely understanding the size of what she spent eighteen years calling a mouse.

I don't gloat. The tide that wanted her is gone with the door it came through. I just hold her eyes for one long moment, long enough that she'll remember it, and then I look away, out over the kneeling Crescent, over the survivors, over the bond-bright man at my side and the friend I healed in the grass and the whole impossible morning.

"Get up," I tell them — all of them, the only mercy and the only command I have, the same words I once gave Mara, meaning something entirely different now. "All of you. Get up." The dawn pours off me into the valley. "I spent my whole life at the back of the dark, and I am *done* with anyone kneeling in it — including me." I find Kane's hand, and Rourke's eyes, and the faces of a hundred packs who survived the end of the world. "We don't kneel anymore. Not to me, not to the dark, not to the old lie that one bloodline gets to stand while the rest stay small." I lift my chin, silver-eyed, alive, *theirs.* "We stand up. Together. That's the only deal the Moonborn's offering."

And in the grey valley, in the first dawn after the long dark, the packs of the Crescent rise.

chapters.title: The Hollow We Bury Her In
chapters.body:
We bury Maeve in the high hollow, where she lived alone for thirty years keeping a thousand years of lore for a queen who hadn't been born yet.

It takes a week to climb up there, through the early snow, the whole strange new order of the Crescent rearranging itself around me as we go. I carry her myself, in the end — small, light, the keeper of everything — and we lay her in the high cold place she loved, with my mother's crescent pendant in her folded hands, because the two of them never got to meet in life and I cannot stand the thought of either of them being alone in the dark.

"She filleted me with the truth and then folded my mother's pendant into my hand," I tell Kane, standing over the cairn in the falling snow. "She told me I was the door, not the thing behind it. She died shoving me into the light." My voice doesn't break; I'm past the breaking, into the quieter grief that lives after. "Everything I am that isn't the kitchens or the trees, she gave me. Including the lore that brought you back. She's the reason we're standing here." I press my hand to the cold stones. "I didn't get a mother. I got eight months too few of her. But I got her." The dawn-light, mine now always, warms the snow on the cairn. "Rest, Maeve. You're not alone in the dark with it anymore. I've got it. All of it. I promise."

The new order takes shape slowly, the way real things do.

I don't take a throne. The Silver Queen took a throne, and a power that could heal, and turned it into a leash, and I spent everything I had to seal the consequence of that away — I'm not about to rebuild it with my name on it. Instead I do the thing Maeve always said the Moonborn were *for*, before the world went wrong: I bind the packs. Not under me. *With* me. Nightriver, Blackthorn, the alliance packs that doubted and the rogues that hid — I bring them together the way the dawn comes into a hollow, the living seal at the center of me a reminder to all of them of what a divided Crescent nearly cost the world.

Rourke binds Nightriver to me with the cord he tied on my wrist, and then, healed and whole and entirely himself again, he goes home to run it — my first friend, my sworn ally, checking on Kane with exactly the menace he promised, riding the high country between our packs like a man who's finally at peace with where the lightning lives. He'll find her someday, the one who chooses him first. I've decided to make sure of it. Queens can do that, it turns out. Meddle. It's one of the better perks.

And Blackthorn — the pack that took my mother in to watch her, that threw funerals for omegas before dawn, that dragged me out its gate to die — Blackthorn I do not punish, because punishment is the tide and the tide is the Queen's door and I am done reaching for it. I just *change* it. No more omegas at the back, in the dark. No more careful cruelty dressed as grace. The kitchens get a new mistress; Mara lives out her careful life scrubbing the pots she once made me scrub, which is its own quiet justice, and I find I don't need any louder kind.

Kane rebuilds it with me. That's the part I didn't expect — that the Alpha who weighed a frightened girl against a war would turn out to be, when he finally stopped being afraid, the most relentless wolf in the Crescent for *getting it right.* He doesn't grovel anymore; I told him I was done with kneeling, his and mine both. Instead he just shows up, every single day, and does the work of deserving the thing he threw away, and never once asks me to call it even.

"You know you don't have to keep proving it," I tell him one night, months in, on the wall above the Choosing clearing where the moon first drew us together and a coward tore us apart. The clearing's just a clearing now. We made sure of that. "The bond's whole. I came back from the dead and knelt on a rock for you. I think we've established I'm staying."

"I know." He pulls me back against his chest, the bond a warm two-way hum, the living seal of a shut door beating quiet between us. "I'm not proving it to keep you. You're not going anywhere; I can feel that, same as you can." His mouth finds the silver streak at my temple. "I'm doing it because I spent one night being the kind of man who throws away the best thing the moon ever made, and I'd rather spend every night after being the other kind. Not for you. *Because* of you. There's a difference. You taught it to me." A beat. "Mara taught you careful. You taught me brave. I'm keeping the better lesson."

I turn in his arms, under the moon that chose me, the moon I can now call down into my own two hands, and I look at the man who rejected the omega and came back from the brink to claim the Luna, and I find that the wound the trees made — the one that whispered *of course he threw you away, you were always going to be thrown away* — has finally, completely, gone quiet.

"Together," I say.

"Together," he says.

And the moon, above the clearing where it all began, shines down silver on two wolves who stood back up.

chapters.title: Epilogue: The Girl at the Front
chapters.body:
One year later, they hold a Choosing in the Blackthorn clearing, and I make them move the omegas to the front.

It's the first thing I changed, and the change that scandalized the old wolves most — more than binding the packs, more than sealing the dark, more than a kitchen stray sitting at the head of the war-table where a Ravenscar was supposed to sit. *The omegas at the front.* In the firelight. Where everyone can see them, and where they can see, when the moon crests, whether she's reached for them.

Because I stood at the back once, in the dark, where the firelight doesn't reach, and I learned there that the cruelest thing a pack can teach a wolf is that some of them belong in the dark by birth. I am the proof of how wrong that is. I am the Moonborn Luna of the Crescent, and I came out of the back of the dark, and I am never letting another frightened kid stand where I stood believing the moon doesn't waste a mate on a girl whose own mother was a stray.

So they stand at the front now. All of them. And tonight, when the moon crests fat and silver over the pines, I watch from the dais — the dais of black stone, the same one, the one I climbed to claim my name and the one Kane knelt off of to chase me into the dark — and I watch a skinny kitchen girl at the *front* of the firelight feel the hook land in her chest and turn, wide-eyed and disbelieving, toward a young warrior across the clearing who's turning toward her at the exact same moment.

Chosen. In the light. Where everyone can see that she was worth choosing.

The clearing erupts. And the girl bursts into tears, the way I would have, the way I never got to, and I have to look away for a moment because the dawn-light pricks behind my own eyes and a queen is allowed exactly so much public crying.

"You did that," Kane says beside me, his hand finding mine on the black stone. The bond hums between us, warm and whole and two-way, the living seal of a door that's stayed shut a year now and will stay shut a thousand more. "Moved them to the front. She'd have been at the back, a year ago. Nobody would have seen the moon choose her. She'd have spent her whole life thinking she imagined it."

"I know." I lean into him, watching the chosen girl get swept up into a clearing full of wolves who are, for once, all looking. "That's the whole point of the job, I've decided. Not the binding, not the power, not the sealing. That." I nod at the girl. "Making sure the ones at the back get to stand in the light long enough to find out they were never nothing."

Rourke's up here too, of course — he rode in for the Choosing, and he's brought someone, a sharp-eyed Nightriver tracker who keeps making him laugh in a way I've never heard him laugh, and who chose *him* first, plainly, completely, the way he deserved all along. I catch his eye across the dais and he gives me the crooked smile and a small nod, *see, told you, patient man,* and I send back the dawn-light flicker that means *I meddled and I regret nothing,* and he laughs again.

Below us, the festival roars on. The packs of the Crescent, bound and whole and standing up. The omegas at the front. The dark sealed, the lore kept, Maeve and my mother resting in the high hollow under a single crescent pendant. A year of peace, new and watched and fragile, the world still learning what it means that the Moonborn came back not as a tyrant or a sacrifice but as a girl who moved the unwanted into the light.

It won't last forever. Nothing does. There are old powers stirring beyond the Crescent that have noticed a Moonborn walks the world again; there are packs across the water who tell the Silver Queen's story differently; the door is shut but the world is wide, and tonight is peace and tomorrow is anyone's guess.

But that's tomorrow.

Tonight the moon is high and silver over the clearing where it all began. Tonight a kitchen girl is crying in the firelight because she was chosen where everyone could see. Tonight the man who rejected the omega stands beside the Luna he came back from the dead to claim, his hand in hers, the bond singing whole between them, neither of them ever again alone in the dark.

Tonight, for the first time in a thousand years, the moon got it exactly right.

I lift my face to her — my grandmother's prison, my mother's god, my own two hands' worth of light now — and I don't ask her for anything. I spent my whole life asking her for a mother, a name, one person who'd want me to stay. She took her time. She made me earn it through the dark.

But I'm not at the back anymore. I'm not in the dark anymore.

I'm at the front, in the light, with my pack standing up around me and my mate's hand in mine, and the moon shining silver on a queen who was once a mouse, and the whole bright dangerous beautiful rest of it still to come.

"Together?" Kane murmurs, the old vow, the only deal I take.

I lace my fingers through his, and the moon comes down soft and silver into the clearing, and I smile.

"Together."

*— The End —*

stories.title: The Billionaire's Contract Bride
stories.author: Lana Ross
stories.blurb: She spills red wine on the coldest billionaire in the city — and instead of suing, he proposes. Marry me for one year, he says, and your family's debt disappears; refuse, and the bank takes your late mother's shop on Friday. There's only one rule in the contract: never fall in love. But the man who bought a year of her life knew her name before she gave it — and the file locked in his desk says he's been watching her family for a year, for reasons he won't explain. As the lies turn real and the truth turns dangerous, she has to choose what's worth more: the deal that saved her, or the man she was never supposed to want.

chapters.title: A Glass of Red on a Grey Suit
chapters.body:
The first time I ruin Adrian Sterling's night, it's an accident. The second time, three weeks later, will be entirely on purpose — but I don't know that yet, standing in a borrowed black uniform with a tray of champagne I'm not allowed to drink, watching the richest man in the city refuse to smile.

I'm not supposed to be here. I'm supposed to be at the shop tonight, doing inventory I can't afford to replace, but Marco from the catering company called in a favor and the favor pays ninety dollars, and ninety dollars is ninety dollars when your father's debt has a Friday on it.

So here I am. At the Sterling Group winter gala, in a ballroom that costs more to light for one evening than my mother's framing shop made in a year, carrying drinks to people whose shoes cost more than my rent.

And there *he* is. The man the whole room arranges itself around without quite looking at — the way you don't look directly at something cold.

Adrian Sterling stands near the windows with the city glittering forty floors below him, and he is, annoyingly, exactly as beautiful as the magazines say and twice as unpleasant about it. Black hair, a jaw you could cut glass on, a charcoal suit that fits like it was sewn onto him by people who were afraid of him. He's listening to an older man in a tuxedo with the expression of someone calculating how many more sentences he has to endure. He has not smiled once. I've been watching for forty minutes, partly because watching him is more interesting than watching anyone else, and partly because a man who looks that miserable at his own party is the only thing in this room I find relatable.

"Don't stare, sweetheart," murmurs Lena, gliding past with her own tray. "Sterling men eat girls like us for breakfast and don't leave a tip."

"I'm not staring. I'm assessing." I'm staring.

"Mm. Go offer the ice prince a drink, then, assessor. The boss wants the trays moving."

Which is how I end up crossing the ballroom toward Adrian Sterling with a tray of champagne and a glass of red someone ordered and abandoned, rehearsing *good evening sir, may I offer you* in my head, telling myself the flutter under my ribs is nerves about the ninety dollars and not about the slate-grey eyes I can already feel not-looking at me.

I almost make it.

I'm two steps away, lowering into the practiced little dip, *good evening sir* halfway out of my mouth — when a woman in silver pivots hard to laugh at something, her elbow catches my tray, and the whole architecture of my balance comes apart in a single sickening lurch.

The champagne flutes go first. Then, in graceful, unhurried, horrifying slow motion, the abandoned glass of red wine leaves the tray, turns once in the chandelier light like it has somewhere to be, and empties itself down the front of Adrian Sterling's charcoal suit.

The ballroom inhales.

I have, in my twenty-four years, imagined a great many ways my life might end, and most of them were quieter than this. The silver woman gasps. The tuxedoed man steps back like the wine might be contagious. Somewhere a phone is already up, because of course it is, because nothing happens in this city anymore that isn't immediately for sale.

And Adrian Sterling looks down at the red bleeding across three thousand dollars of grey wool, and then he looks up at me — the waitress, the nobody, the girl who just baptized the most feared man in the room in merlot — and I brace for it. The cold fury. The *do you know who I am.* The quiet word to a manager that ends my ninety dollars and possibly my evening in a much smaller room.

It doesn't come.

Instead something moves across his face that I will spend weeks trying to name. Not anger. Something stranger — a flicker of *recognition*, like a man who's been handed a photograph he wasn't expecting. His grey eyes move over my face, my paint-stained hands gripping the empty tray, the chip in my front tooth that shows when my mouth falls open in horror, and something in him goes very still and very focused, the way the whole room goes still around him.

"I'm so sorry," I manage. "I'll get — towels, club soda, my entire life's savings, which won't cover the dry cleaning, but —"

"Cole." He says it quietly. Not a question.

The word lands wrong. Cold all down my spine.

Because I haven't told him my name. I'm a temp in a borrowed uniform with no badge, hired this afternoon, and the man whose suit I just destroyed is looking at me like he already knows exactly who I am — and has for longer than the ten seconds we've been standing here.

"Sienna Cole," Adrian Sterling says, and the faintest, strangest thing happens at the corner of his unsmiling mouth. "You're a difficult woman to get into a room. I was beginning to think I'd have to come to the shop."

chapters.title: The Proposition
chapters.body:
The summons comes by courier the next morning, on paper so heavy it feels like a subpoena, which is what I assume it is.

*Sterling Tower. 2 p.m. Ask for Mr. Sterling's office.*

I spend the cab fare I don't have getting there, because the alternative is not showing up, and a man who knew my name before I gave it is not a man I want to find me at home. The lobby of Sterling Tower is all black marble and silence, the kind of silence money buys. A woman with a headset and a flawless face walks me into a glass elevator that climbs the spine of the building until the whole grey city spreads out below, and I press my paint-stained hands flat against my borrowed good dress and try to look like someone who belongs forty floors above her own life.

His office is the size of my apartment. Maybe two of them. He's standing at the window again — apparently it's his whole personality — in a fresh charcoal suit with no wine on it, and when he turns I'm braced for lawyers, for numbers, for the word *liability.*

"Sit," Adrian Sterling says.

"I'd rather stand for the part where you sue me."

"I'm not going to sue you." He studies me with those slate eyes, unhurried. "Sit down, Ms. Cole. What I'm about to offer you is not the sort of thing one should hear standing up."

I sit. Mostly because my knees aren't sure about the alternative.

He doesn't sit. He picks up a slim folder from the glass desk and doesn't open it, just holds it, like a man deciding how much of his hand to show. "Your father is Daniel Cole," he says. "Your mother was Marian Cole, who ran a framing and restoration shop on Hartwell Street until she passed four years ago. You kept it open. Barely. Eighteen months ago your father took a bridge loan to cover the back taxes — a loan that was bought, through three intermediaries, by Sterling Group's holding arm." He sets the folder down. "It defaults Friday. When it does, my company takes the shop, the apartment above it, and whatever else is pledged against it, which is everything your family has left."

I've stopped breathing. "If this is your idea of an apology for the wine —"

"It's not an apology. It's leverage, and I'd rather be honest about it than pretend otherwise." Something almost like distaste crosses his face — at himself, I'll realize much later. "I have a problem, Ms. Cole, and you have a problem, and it happens that we can solve each other's. So I'm going to make you an offer, and you're going to want to slap me, and I'd ask you to hear all of it before you do."

"...I'm listening."

He comes around the desk and finally — finally — sits, on the edge of it, close enough that I can see the faint scar across his knuckles and smell something expensive and cold, like rain on stone.

"My grandfather is dying," Adrian says, flatly, the way you'd report weather. "His will hands control of Sterling Group to me — but only if I'm married within thirty days of the reading. He believes a man without a wife is a man without a leash, and he would rather hand my company to my cousin Julian than watch me run it 'unanchored.'" His mouth thins. "I have twenty-six days. I do not have a wife. I have no intention of acquiring a real one — I've seen what love does in my family; it's a currency, and I'm done being spent. What I need is a woman who can play the part convincingly for one year, in public, for the cameras and the board and a very sharp old man — and who has every reason to keep her mouth shut and her end of the bargain."

The grey city tilts gently behind him.

"You're not serious," I say.

"I'm extremely serious. I'm rarely anything else." He picks the folder back up, opens it, slides a single page across the glass toward me. It's a contract. My name is already on it, typed, waiting. "Marry me, Ms. Cole. One year. A paper marriage — separate rooms, separate lives, a public performance and a private truce. In exchange, your family's debt disappears on Friday instead of taking your mother's shop. The loan, gone. The back taxes, paid. A settlement on top, enough that you never have to carry a stranger's champagne again." His eyes hold mine, and there's no warmth in them, only a terrible, patient certainty. "Refuse, and I do nothing — which is to say, the bank does everything, on Friday, exactly as scheduled."

I look at the page. At my name, already typed, like he knew. Like he'd known for a while.

"You don't even know me," I whisper.

And there it is again — that flicker at the corner of his mouth, that thing that isn't a smile, that *recognition* from the ballroom that I still can't read.

"Marry me," Adrian Sterling says, "and your family's debt disappears. Refuse, and the bank takes your mother's shop on Friday. You have until then to decide — but I think we both know you decided the moment I said the word *shop.*"

chapters.title: The File With My Name On It
chapters.body:
I hold out until Thursday.

Thursday is when I sit in the back of the shop after closing, under the framed photograph of my mother with her arm around a younger me, both of us paint-smeared and laughing, and I let myself do the math one last time. The loan. The Friday. The apartment above the shop where my father still sleeps in the room he shared with her. There is no version of the arithmetic where pride wins. There never was. Adrian Sterling knew that before I did, which is the part that makes me want to set his beautiful office on fire.

I sign on Friday morning, in a lawyer's office that smells of new carpet, with a man named Adrian who does not gloat, which somehow makes it worse.

"Rule one," he says, sliding the pages across, "this stays between us. Not your father. Not your closest friend. The performance only works if no one knows it's a performance." He turns a page. "Rule two through six: separate rooms, separate finances beyond the settlement, no press without coordination, no contact with my grandfather without me present, and you appear with me whenever I require it — galas, dinners, the occasional photograph. You'll be coached." Another page. His voice doesn't change at all for the last one. "Rule seven. We don't fall in love. This is a contract, Ms. Cole, not a slow song. Sentiment makes people stupid and stupidity is expensive. Keep your heart out of it and we both walk away in a year exactly as we are."

"Rule seven," I repeat. "You actually wrote *don't fall in love* into a legal document."

"I write down everything I intend to enforce."

"Has anyone ever told you you're a deeply strange man?"

"Frequently. Sign here." And there — *there* — is that flicker at the corner of his mouth again, gone before I can be sure it happened.

We're married ninety minutes later.

It's a courthouse, not a cathedral — fast, grey, witnessed by his lawyer and a clerk who's clearly done a hundred of these. I wear the good dress. He wears the charcoal suit. There are no flowers, no music, no family; my father thinks I've taken a live-in art-restoration job for a private collector, which is the lie we agreed on, and it sits in my chest like a swallowed stone. The officiant says the words. Adrian says *I do* like he's confirming a delivery. I say it like I'm jumping off something.

Then the officiant says *you may kiss the bride*, and there are, suddenly, cameras — two of them, arranged by Adrian's people, because the marriage means nothing if the world doesn't see it — and I understand, a half-second too late, that this part isn't on the contract because this part can't be itemized.

He kisses me.

I'd expected it to be like everything else about him: cold, exact, a transaction. It isn't. His hand comes up to my jaw — gentle, careful, like I'm something he's afraid of breaking — and his mouth is warm, and for one absurd suspended second the grey courthouse and the cameras and the contract all fall away and there is only this: a man kissing me like he means it, and the alarming discovery that my whole traitor body has decided to mean it back.

He breaks it first. We're both very still. His eyes, this close, are not slate at all — there's grey, and under the grey something darker and more startled, and for one unguarded instant Adrian Sterling looks at me like a man who has miscalculated.

Then the cameras lower, and the ice comes back down, and he steps away and straightens his cuffs and says, "Good. That'll run by morning," and Rule Seven sits between us like a chaperone.

That night, in his penthouse — in *my* penthouse, technically, for the next twelve months — I can't sleep. The rooms are too big and too quiet and still smell faintly of rain on stone, and my mouth still remembers a kiss that wasn't supposed to be real. So I do the thing I always do when I can't sleep, the thing my mother swore would get me in trouble one day: I get up, and I wander, and I touch things that aren't mine.

His study is unlocked. I tell myself I'm just looking at the art.

There's a folder on the desk. Slim, charcoal-grey, exactly like the one he held in his office. I shouldn't. I absolutely shouldn't. I open it.

It's not a contract.

It's *us.* The Coles. Photographs — my mother's shop, the Hartwell Street storefront, my father at the register, *me*, candid, coming out of the shop with a coffee, my hair a mess, completely unaware of the camera. Pages of notes in a precise hand. The loan, traced back through its three intermediaries to Sterling Group, with dates. And clipped to the front, a single sheet, a timeline — and the first entry on it, the first time anyone at Sterling Group started watching my family, is dated.

A year ago.

A full year before a waitress spilled wine on a stranger at a gala. A year before he said my name in a room I was never supposed to be in. A year before any of it.

My hands have gone cold. The penthouse is very quiet. And from the doorway behind me, where I didn't hear him come, Adrian Sterling's voice — low, careful, unreadable:

"You should be asleep, Ms. Cole."

I turn around with the file still open in my hands, my heart going like a fist on a door, and I ask him the only question that matters.

"Why do you have a file on my family," I say, "from a year before you ever knew I existed?"

chapters.title: Charity Case
chapters.body:
He doesn't answer me.

That's the thing I'll learn about Adrian Sterling in the weeks that follow: the man can build a wall out of silence faster than anyone I've ever met. He crosses the study, takes the folder gently out of my hands — gently, always gently, like the kissing — and closes it, and sets it in a drawer, and locks the drawer.

"Due diligence," he says. "I don't sign contracts with people I haven't researched."

"That's a year of photographs, Adrian. That's my mother's shop before I ever met you. That's not due diligence, that's —"

"It's late." His voice is flat and final, the boardroom voice, the one that ends meetings. "Go to bed, Ms. Cole. We have a brunch tomorrow. You're going to be photographed adoring me over eggs, and you can't do that on no sleep."

And he walks out, and I'm left standing in a locked-drawer silence with my heart still pounding and the absolute certainty that I've just married a man who knows something about my life that I don't.

I don't sleep. Obviously.

In the morning there's a closet of clothes that fit me exactly — *exactly*, which is its own small horror — and a woman named Priya who appears to "coach" me, which mostly means teaching me which fork signals old money and how to say *we're so happy* without sounding like I'm reading it off a card. By the time Adrian appears in the kitchen, freshly armored in grey, I've decided to play this exactly the way he plays everything: like a transaction I intend to win.

"You look furious," he observes, accepting coffee from no one because the coffee simply appears.

"I look *radiant,*" I say. "I'm an adoring newlywed. Watch." I beam at him. It's a very good beam. I practiced.

Something flickers — there it is, the corner of the mouth — and then a knock at the penthouse door interrupts whatever he was about to say, and Priya goes to answer it, and a voice floats in from the hall that makes Adrian go very still over his coffee.

"Don't announce me, darling, I know the way." 

She comes into the kitchen like she owns it, and maybe she used to think she would — a tall, polished woman in cream cashmere, blonde hair like she pays it a salary, the kind of beautiful that's been professionally maintained since birth. Her eyes find me in about half a second, and they do that thing, that *inventory*, head to chipped tooth to paint-stained hands, and her smile is a beautiful knife.

"Adrian," she says, without looking away from me. "You didn't tell me she was so... *authentic.*"

"Vanessa." His voice could frost a window. "We didn't have plans."

"We never need plans, you and I." She sets her bag on his counter like a flag planted on territory. "I came to congratulate the happy couple. The whole city's congratulating you — the photos this morning are *darling.* The waitress and the ice king. People love a fairy tale." Now she turns the full weapon of her attention on me, and lowers her voice to something almost kind, which is worse. "Has he told you about us yet? No? He will. We have *history*, sweetheart — the kind families arrange. His grandfather practically had the announcement drafted before this little... arrangement of yours."

"Vanessa." Sharper.

"I'm only being *friendly.*" She smiles at me, and it's pure venom in a cashmere wrapper. "A word, woman to woman, since no one else will give it to you: I know exactly what you are. The whole right kind of people will know within a month. You're not a bride, darling — you're a *charity case* with a contract. He needed a wife, you needed a check, and that's the entire fairy tale." She picks her bag back up, satisfied, already leaving. "So. How long do you really think this lasts?"

She's gone before I can answer, the door clicking shut behind her, leaving the question hanging in the expensive air.

I look at Adrian. He's gripping his coffee cup hard enough that I can see the scar on his knuckles whiten.

"She doesn't know about the contract," he says. "She's guessing."

"She's guessing *correctly.*"

"Yes." He sets the cup down. For one second he looks almost tired, almost human. "She's also the woman my grandfather wanted me to marry, which means she has a stake in this falling apart, which means—" his grey eyes lift to mine, and there's a warning in them I don't yet understand "—she will spend the next twelve months looking for the exact thing you just found in my study last night.
