To be delivered, by his explicit order, *only* once you'd signed away the company — and only with your wife present." His eyes flick to me. "He was very specific about that, Mrs. Sterling. It seems his last act was about you."

chapters.title: The Old Man's Last Move
chapters.body:
The lawyer lays it out on my mother's worktable, among the framing samples and the gilt, and it takes me a long moment to understand what I'm looking at.

"There are two documents," he says. "Your grandfather was very precise about the conditions. The first is a second will — a *true* will, superseding the one read this morning, sealed and lodged with me personally, to be opened only if Adrian Sterling voluntarily signed away the company rather than defend the marriage with a lie." The lawyer's mouth twitches, almost human. "He bet against you doing it, Mr. Sterling. He told me, and I quote, 'the boy is a Sterling; he'll keep the eleven billion.' He left this in case he was wrong. I don't think he expected to be."

Adrian goes very still. "A true will."

"The one read this morning — the marriage clause, the company to Julian — was a *test.* Your grandfather wanted to know, before he died, whether he'd successfully made you into a man who would spend anything to win, or failed and made a man who'd give up everything for something real." The lawyer slides the sealed document across. "By signing the company away, you triggered the true will. Under it, control of Sterling Group does *not* pass to Julian Sterling. It passes to you — outright, unconditionally, no marriage clause at all." A pause. "Your grandfather's note says: 'If he reads this, he passed the only test that ever mattered, and he failed the only one I wanted him to pass. Give him the company. He's the first Sterling in three generations who deserved to be more than a ledger, and I was too much of a coward to tell him while I lived.'"

The shop is silent. Adrian's hand finds the edge of the worktable, steadying.

"And the second document?" I ask.

The lawyer turns to me, and his gravity deepens. "The second is for you, Mrs. Sterling. Also conditional — to be given to you only if you refused the fortune he offered you last night, and stayed." He slides a thick envelope toward me. "It's a signed, notarized confession. Everything Augustus Sterling did to your mother, Marian Cole, in 2003 — the gallery, the blacklisting, the scandal she was protecting evidence of: a Sterling Group land deal that poisoned a neighborhood's water and was buried under shell companies. All of it. In his own hand, witnessed, legally actionable." The lawyer meets my eyes. "He left it to you, and only you, with full discretion. His note says: 'Marian Cole's daughter wouldn't take my money. So I'll give her the only thing she might actually want — the truth, and the power to do with it whatever her brave foolish mother would have done. Burn us with it, or bury it. Her choice. I owe her that much, twenty-two years late.'"

I open the envelope with shaking hands.

It's all there. The water. The neighborhood. The children who got sick. The shell companies. The decades of cover-up. And my mother's name, in the old man's own writing, named at last as the brave woman who tried to stop it and was destroyed for it. It's everything she lost the gallery protecting. It's a bomb with the entire Sterling empire inside the blast radius.

I could burn them with it. Right now. Hand it to the press downstairs, vindicate my mother, expose a twenty-two-year-old poisoning, watch the name that ruined her name finally pay — the company Adrian just inherited an hour ago crumbling in the same week, the whole rotten ledger of the Sterlings called due at last.

Vengeance. My mother's vengeance, finally, in my brave-handed grip.

I look up. Adrian is watching me — not stopping me, not pleading, just *watching*, the way he watched me decide to leave, the way he's learned to let me choose my own life. He's just inherited everything, again, in the same week, and I'm holding a document that could turn it all to ash, and he is not going to say a single word to stop me, because he decided in a boardroom this morning that he would never again decide anything *for* me.

The old man's last move, I realize, was the cruelest and the truest thing he ever did. He didn't just confess. He handed me the choice he stole from my mother — the power to destroy or forgive, freely, with no one pricing it — and he made Adrian's whole future hang on what I'd do with it.

"You're not going to tell me what to do with this," I say to Adrian. "Are you."

"No," he says quietly. "It's yours. It was always yours. Whatever you decide — even if it's the end of everything I just got back — it's the first thing in this whole story that's entirely, only your choice." A breath. "Burn us, or bury it. I'll stand with you either way."

And I stand in my mother's shop, with her stolen dream finally proven and a bomb in my hands, and I have to decide what kind of daughter — what kind of *woman* — I'm going to be.

chapters.title: My Mother's Daughter
chapters.body:
I think about it for a long time, in the dark, under her photograph.

The easy thing — the *satisfying* thing — is to burn them. Hand the confession to the press, vindicate Marian, watch the Sterling name pay for twenty-two years too late. My mother lost everything protecting that evidence. There's a version of being her daughter that means finishing what she started, no matter what it costs the man I love.

But I keep coming back to the children.

The neighborhood. The poisoned water. That's the part that matters — not the Sterling name, not my mother's gallery, not even my own anger. There are people, real people, who got sick twenty-two years ago and never got the truth or the help they were owed. *That's* what my mother was protecting. Not a grievance. *Them.*

And burning the whole company to the ground doesn't help them. It just feels good. It scatters eleven billion dollars and a hundred thousand jobs and the one Sterling who learned to be a person, and it leaves a pile of ash where there could have been *restitution.*

My mother had brave hands and a foolish heart, Augustus said. But she wasn't foolish. She was trying to *fix something.* The vengeance was never the point. The point was the people the water hurt.

So I decide to be my mother's daughter the way she'd actually want.

"I'm not going to burn it," I tell Adrian. "And I'm not going to bury it either." I hold up the confession. "We're going to *use* it. Not against you — *with* you. You just inherited a company built partly on a poisoning that was never made right. So we make it right. Publicly, fully, on our terms. The truth comes out — the water, the neighborhood, my mother's name cleared — and Sterling Group pays for it. Restitution to every family that got sick. A foundation, run independently, funded by the company that caused the harm. The scandal becomes the thing that *forces* the change instead of the thing that gets buried again." I look at him. "Your grandfather turned everything into a line item. So let's make his worst sin into the biggest line item of all — and spend it making people whole instead of keeping people quiet."

Adrian is staring at me like I've rearranged the world.

"That'll cost a fortune," he says slowly. "And the press will be brutal — inheriting the company the same week we admit it poisoned a neighborhood—"

"Good. Let them be brutal. We'll deserve the brutal and we'll do the right thing anyway." I step closer. "You wanted to make it right, a year ago, with a clever transaction that paid down one woman's wound. This is bigger. This makes it right for everyone your grandfather hurt to keep his ledger clean — and it does it in the open, where it can't be undone." A breath. "*That's* how I want to be Marian Cole's daughter. Not with a bomb. With a foundation."

And the strangest thing happens: Adrian Sterling laughs.

It's not the swallowed almost-smile. It's a real laugh, startled out of him, helpless and bright in the dark shop, the laugh of a man who spent his whole life being taught love was a liability and has just watched the woman he loves turn his family's worst sin into its redemption.

"My grandfather," he says, wiping his eyes, "spent his entire life believing everything was a transaction, and he died handing you the power to prove him right — to take the money, or burn us, or settle the score. And you looked at all of it and decided to spend it on strangers who got sick twenty years ago." He shakes his head, undone. "He never stood a chance against you. None of us did."

There's still the matter of Julian and Vanessa — but that, it turns out, takes care of itself. The boardroom this morning had the press along the wall, recorders running, and they caught all of it: Adrian giving away the company rather than lie, yes — but also Julian and Vanessa's faces when the *true* will is announced the next morning and the company they conspired to steal evaporates from their hands. And when a junior clerk, frightened by the scale of what's coming, comes forward about being paid by Vanessa Hale to leak the contract, the conspiracy unravels in public the way these things do — two ledger-people who priced everything and forgot that the one thing you can't buy is a room full of witnesses who just watched someone refuse to be bought.

Julian gets nothing. Vanessa's merger dies with her credibility. They overreached, the way the ledger-people always do, because they couldn't imagine a Sterling who'd rather lose than lie.

But that's all tomorrow's news.

Tonight there's just my mother's shop, and a confession that's going to become a foundation, and a man laughing in the dark because the woman he loves chose mercy with teeth — and the small matter, still unsettled between us, of a contract with a Rule Seven that we both broke, and what we're going to do about it now that there's finally nothing standing in the way.

chapters.title: Nothing Owed
chapters.body:
"There's still the contract," I say.

The lawyer's gone. The shop is quiet. The confession sits on the worktable beside my mother's framing samples, already becoming something good instead of something buried. And the original contract — the one Julian waved at the board, the one with Rule Seven in it — is still, technically, the document that defines my marriage.

"I'll tear it up," Adrian says immediately. "First thing. It's meaningless now anyway, the will's—"

"No." I go to the worktable, and I find the contract — he brought it from the boardroom, of course he did, the man keeps everything — and I hold it between us. "I want to tear it up. Myself. But not because it's meaningless." I look at him. "Because of what it *means.* This is the thing that started us, Adrian. A cleared debt for a year of my life. A transaction. And as long as it exists — as long as there's a single piece of paper that says you got me in exchange for *anything* — I'll always be able to point to it and wonder. Was I bought. Was I owed. Was I an amends." I hold his eyes. "I don't want to be married to you with a debt sitting in a drawer somewhere. I don't want anything *owed* between us. Not the shop, not my mother, not the wine, not a single thing. I want us to be a choice with nothing underneath it — or nothing at all."

"Sienna—"

"So here's my offer." My voice is shaking but my hands aren't; they're my mother's hands and they're steady. "I'm tearing this up. The contract, the settlement, all of it. The shop's already saved — that's done, that's mine, it stays mine and it has nothing to do with you anymore. And then there's no debt, no deal, no Rule Seven, no clever transaction that explains why I'm standing in this shop with you." I take a breath. "And if you want me after that — when there's nothing owed in either direction, when I can't be bought and you can't be repaying anything, when it's just a wine-stained billionaire and a girl with brave hands and *nothing between us but the choosing* — then you ask me again. Properly. As nobody's amends and nobody's debt."

And I tear the contract in half.

It's a good feeling. I tear it again, and again, Rule Seven and the settlement and the timed-to-the-will architecture of the whole bought thing coming apart in my mother's steady hands, until it's confetti on the worktable, until there's not a single piece of paper left in the world that says Adrian Sterling got me in exchange for anything at all.

"There," I say. "Nothing owed. Now we're just two people in a shop."

Adrian looks at the torn contract. Then at me. And then — this man who gave away eleven billion dollars to prove a feeling, who learned love was a liability and unlearned it in a blue room and a storm and a boardroom — Adrian Sterling gets down on one knee on the sawdust floor of my mother's framing shop, with no cameras and no board and no debt and nothing in his hands to gain, and he looks up at me with the swallowed almost-smile finally, fully unswallowed.

"Sienna Cole," he says. "Marian's daughter. The woman who threw wine on me and was never afraid. There's no contract. There's no will. There's no debt. There's nothing owed between us in either direction, and there never will be again." His voice breaks, and he lets it. "I'm not asking you to save a shop or settle a wrong or play a part for a camera. I'm just asking you to choose me, in an empty room, for no reason at all except that I love you and I'd rather be poor and laughing in this shop with you than rich and frozen in any boardroom in the world." A breath. "Marry me. For real this time. For nothing. For *us.*"

And under my mother's brave ruined smiling face, in the shop where she framed other people's dreams for twenty years and never told me about her own, I look at the man kneeling in the sawdust with nothing to offer me but himself, and I give him the truest thing I've got.

"Yes," I say. "For nothing. For us."

chapters.title: The Foundation
chapters.body:
The press is brutal, exactly as promised, and we deserve it, and we do the right thing anyway.

The week the true will is announced — Adrian confirmed at the head of Sterling Group, Julian and Vanessa's conspiracy unraveling in the same news cycle — we don't hide behind it. We get ahead of it. Adrian stands at a podium, no spin, no lawyers softening it, and tells the world what his grandfather's company did in 2003: the land deal, the poisoned water, the neighborhood, the cover-up, and the woman named Marian Cole who tried to stop it and was destroyed for it.

He says her name from a podium in front of the whole city. *Marian Cole.* Brave woman. Foolish heart. The one who was right.

I watch from the side of the stage, and I think my mother would have liked that part — not the vindication, she was never vain, but the *naming.* The truth she protected, finally said out loud, twenty-two years late, by the grandson of the man who buried it.

And then we make it right, the way I said we would. The **Cole Foundation** — funded by Sterling Group, run independently so the company can't ever quiet it — opens within the month, with one mandate: find every family the water hurt, and make them whole. Medical costs, decades back. Restitution. The help they were owed in 2003 and never got. I run it. Me. The framer, the waitress, the girl who was supposed to be a line item, sitting at the head of the thing that turns the family's worst sin into the closest thing to repair money can buy.

It costs a fortune. Adrian signs it without blinking. "Best transaction I've ever made," he says, dry, and it's the only time either of us ever calls anything between us a transaction again, and we both laugh, because it's finally a joke instead of a wound.

Vanessa Hale's merger collapses with her credibility; she leaves the city for somewhere her name means less. Julian — charming, hungry Julian — gets nothing he conspired for, but Adrian, in a decision that surprises everyone including me, doesn't destroy him. "He grew up in the same house I did," Adrian says. "Same grandfather, same lesson, and no one ever threw wine on him." He gives Julian a real job, a real chance, far from the throne — and tells him, plainly, that the next move against the family will be his last. Julian, for once, has nothing charming to say. I think it might even be the making of him. Time will tell.

And the shop stays mine.

That's the part I insisted on, the part that matters most. Hartwell Street, the framing shop, my mother's life — it's not Sterling money, it's not saved-by-a-billionaire, it's *mine*, free and clear, the way it was before any contract. I keep it open. I hire help so I'm not chained to it, but I keep my hours there, framing other people's art with my mother's hands, because it's where she is and it's where I learned who I am.

And in the back room, the one she used to paint in before the gallery was stolen and the dream got small — I start painting again.

Adrian finds me there one evening, weeks in, paint to the elbows, and he doesn't say anything. He just leans in the doorway the way he used to lean in penthouse doorways, watching me work, with the swallowed almost-smile fully unswallowed now, permanent, like it finally found somewhere safe to live.

"You're staring," I tell him, not looking up.

"I'm assessing," he says.

I throw a rag at him. He catches it, laughing, and the sound of Adrian Sterling laughing in the back of my mother's shop, paint on his ridiculous expensive shirt, the city's coldest man gone warm in a room that smells of turpentine and gilt — that's the moment I stop, sometimes, even now, and have to remind myself it's real.

It's real. There's nothing owed. We checked.

We marry again that spring. Not a courthouse, not cameras, not a performance for a board. Just the shop, and my father, and a handful of people who actually know us, and my mother's photograph watching from the wall. I wear paint under my nails because I'd been working that morning and couldn't be bothered to scrub it all off, and Adrian says it's the most beautiful he's ever seen me, and means it.

There's no contract. There's no Rule Seven. There's nothing owed in either direction.

There's just the choosing.

And we choose. Out loud. In front of her.

chapters.title: The Man Who Learned to Stay
chapters.body:
Married life, it turns out, is the part no contract could have written.

We're terrible at some of it and wonderful at others. Adrian still works too much; eleven billion dollars and a foundation and a company built on sins that need unwinding will do that. I still keep impossible hours at the shop and come home with gilt in my hair. We argue — really argue, the way two stubborn people do — about money and time and whose turn it is and whether he's allowed to "fix" things for me without asking (he is not; we have a rule about it, an actual rule, the only one we kept). 

But here's the thing about Adrian Sterling, the thing his grandfather tried to breed out of him and failed: he *stays.*

The boy in the blue room cried *don't go* because everyone in his life had taught him that love is the thing that leaves. And the man he became spends every day proving the opposite — staying, choosing, showing up, in the small unglamorous ways that no camera captures and no contract could ever require. He learns how I take my coffee and never forgets. He shows up at the shop at closing so I don't drive home alone. He keeps a drawer of my charcoals in his office at Sterling Tower, and his assistants have learned not to ask. He held my hand through the first Cole Foundation meeting with the families the water hurt, and when an old woman who'd lost a child in 2003 took my hands in both of hers and wept and thanked me, it was Adrian who had to leave the room, because the ice king couldn't keep his composure in front of the board's worst sin made human.

He feels things now. Out loud. In public. It terrifies him still, and he does it anyway, every day, which is the only kind of brave that counts.

"Do you ever regret it?" I ask him one night, months in, the two of us on the floor of the shop's back room because we never did learn to use furniture like normal people, my head on his chest, the smell of turpentine and him. "Giving it away. In the boardroom. You'd have kept the company either way — the true will gave it to you. But you didn't *know* that. You signed it away thinking it was gone forever."

"I know."

"You gave up everything for a feeling, not knowing you'd get it back."

"I know." His hand moves through my paint-stained hair. "And no. I don't regret it. Not for a second." I feel him exhale. "Because for one hour, Sienna — between signing it away and that lawyer walking into the shop — I had nothing. No company, no empire, no leverage, no ledger. Just you, in a dark shop, and a thing I felt that couldn't buy me anything." His voice goes quiet. "And it was the first hour of my entire life that I was certain — completely certain — that I was a person and not a position. That what I felt was *mine*, not a move. That I'd chosen something for no reason except that it was true." A breath. "My grandfather spent eighty years rich and never had that hour. I'd give the company away a hundred times to keep it."

I tip my head up to look at him. "You're getting very good at the feelings-out-loud thing."

"I had an excellent teacher. Annoying. Threw wine at me. Wouldn't be afraid."

"Sounds difficult."

"Impossible," he agrees, and kisses the top of my head, and we lie there on the floor of my mother's shop, the billionaire and the framer, with nothing owed between us and everything chosen, and it is — finally, completely, with no asterisk and no Rule Seven and no debt in any drawer — *real.*

My mother framed other people's dreams for twenty years and never told me about the one that got stolen. I think she'd be glad to know her shop ended up being where her daughter learned the difference between a thing you're given and a thing you choose.

I think she'd be glad I have her hands.

And I think she'd have liked him — the cold man who learned to stay — though she'd have made him work for it, and made him laugh, and never once been afraid.

chapters.title: Epilogue: The Painting in the Lobby
chapters.body:
Two years later, there's a painting in the lobby of Sterling Tower, and it scandalizes the board almost as much as the marriage did.

It hangs where a portrait of Augustus used to be — the old man's stern oil likeness having been quietly retired to a basement archive, which I consider one of my finer acts of vengeance, the patient kind, the kind my mother would have approved of. In its place: a painting of a framing shop on Hartwell Street, light coming through the window, gilt and sawdust and a woman's brave ruined hands at work. I painted it. It's the first thing anyone sees walking into the coldest company in the city, and visitors always ask about it, and Adrian always tells them the same thing: *"That's where my wife taught me what things are worth. Including me."*

The Cole Foundation has made four hundred families whole, and counting. My mother's name is on the door. The water scandal that destroyed her became the thing that finally got those families the truth and the help they were owed twenty-two years too late — which means, in the end, she won the fight she lost the gallery over. It just took her daughter's hands to finish it.

The shop still smells of turpentine. I still keep my hours there. I paint in the back room every week, in the room where she gave up her dream so quietly that I grew up thinking the small life was the only one she ever wanted. I know better now. I make sure my own dream stays loud.

Adrian still works too much. He's better at it than he was — the company spends as much rebuilding what it broke as it does growing now, which the board hated until it turned out that being the conglomerate that *owns its sins* is, in the cold ledger Augustus loved, extremely good business. The old man would have been furious to learn that decency paid. I think about that a lot. It makes me laugh.

He still shows up at the shop at closing. He still learns my coffee. He still keeps a drawer of my charcoals in an office worth more than most countries. He still feels things out loud, in public, terrified and brave, every single day.

And every spring, on the anniversary of a courthouse wedding that started as a transaction and a framing-shop wedding that ended as a choice, we go back to Hartwell Street after hours, just the two of us, and we sit on the floor of the back room because we never did learn to use furniture, under my mother's photograph and my own paintings, and we don't say much.

We don't have to. There's nothing owed between us. We checked, a long time ago, and we've kept it that way — no debt, no deal, no ledger, no Rule Seven. Just a wine-stained billionaire and a girl with her mother's hands, choosing each other in an empty room with nothing to gain, the way the only real things are always chosen.

"Was that a transaction?" I ask him, every year, the old joke.

And every year Adrian Sterling — the man who gave away an empire to prove a feeling, the boy who learned to stay — pulls me closer on the floor of my mother's shop and gives me the same answer, the truest one he's got, the one no contract could ever have written:

"No," he says. "That one's just love. No charge."

*— The End —*

stories.title: Captive of the Fae King
stories.author: Isolde Vane
stories.blurb: She's only a village healer — until the dying Fae King rides into her town to collect his tithe and sees the grey blight recede wherever she walks. Dragged across the border in chains, she learns the terrible truth: she's the last of a bloodline the cursed court has been waiting a hundred years to be born — the one thing alive that can mend a kingdom fading into ash. But the cold, beautiful King who took her knows the old law's cruelest catch: her power can only save him if it's freely given, and he would rather fade into the dark forever than force the woman he's falling for to pay the price the last savior paid with her life.

chapters.title: The Tithe
chapters.body:
The grey reaches Ashford the same week the fae come to collect.

I've been watching it crawl closer for a month — the blight, we call it, the greying, the thing that leaches the color and the life out of everything it touches, creeping out of the fae border like a stain spreading through wet cloth. The wheat goes ashen and won't ripen. The river runs the color of a dead sky. And now it's at the edge of my own village, and I'm on my knees in old Halloran's cottage trying to coax breath back into lungs that have gone grey inside him, and there's nothing in my whole healer's kit that touches it.

"It's not a sickness, Eira," Halloran wheezes. "You can't herb your way out of the fae world dying. When *they* fade, we fade with them. That's the old bargain. That's always been the bargain."

"The old bargain can choke." I press dried yarrow to his chest because it's that or admit there's nothing. "Breathe, you stubborn goat. I didn't keep you alive through two winters to lose you to a bad mood in the weather."

He almost laughs. Then the horns sound at the village edge, low and golden and wrong, a sound no mortal throat could make, and the laugh dies in him, and so does the village's noise, all at once, the way a room goes silent when something dangerous walks in.

The fae have come for the tithe.

I've never seen them up close. No one in Ashford under fifty has — the tithe comes once a generation, the old pact, a wagon of grain and a measure of iron and, the stories say, sometimes a person, taken to serve in the courts beyond the border and never seen again. We pay it because the alternative, the stories also say, is worse. We pay it because we always have.

I come out of Halloran's cottage wiping his grey blood off my hands, and I stop.

They fill the village square like a fever dream. A host of them, tall and terrible and beautiful in the way a wildfire is beautiful — ash-pale skin, eyes like coals and frost and deep water, mounts that breathe smoke. The villagers have gone to their knees in the mud. And at the center, on a black horse with an antlered crown on his ash-pale brow and eyes the gold of banked embers, sits a creature the others orient around the way iron filings orient to a lodestone.

I know who he is without being told. There's only one of him.

The Ember King.

He's saying something to the headman — the tithe, the grain, the iron, the old words — in a voice like fire heard through a wall, and the headman is babbling, and I'm not listening, because I'm too busy doing the thing I always do, which is taking stock, healer's habit, reading the room for what's wrong.

And what's wrong is the King.

He's fading. I can see it the way I can see fever or jaundice or the grey creeping under a dying man's skin — there's a thinness to him, an *going-out*, like a coal with the heat draining from its heart. The whole host has it, faintly. But the King wears it like a crown beneath his crown. He is the most alive thing I've ever seen and he is dying, all at once, and no one around him seems to see it but me.

I must make a sound, or move, or something — because the ember eyes find me.

Across the kneeling square, the Ember King looks at the only mortal still standing, the healer with grey blood on her hands and no sense to bow, and something shifts in that ancient terrible face. He goes still. He looks at me the way I was just looking at him — *reading*, startled, like he's found something he wasn't expecting in a village he'd already written off.

"You," he says. The square holds its breath. "Healer. Step forward."

I don't want to. Every sensible cell in my body says kneel, look down, be small, let the beautiful dying thing collect its grain and go. But Halloran's grey blood is drying on my hands and a month of losing patients to a thing I can't herb is hot in my chest, and I've never in my life been good at being small.

I step forward.

And the moment my boot comes down in the mud at the square's edge — the moment I move toward him — something happens that drops the headman's jaw and sends a gasp through the entire fae host like wind through dry grass.

The grey recedes.

Right there, around my feet, in a slow spreading circle — the ashen mud darkens back to brown, a greyed weed at the path's edge flushes green, the blight pulling *back* from me like a tide going out, three full feet of dead world coming quietly back to life in the space of a breath.

I stop. I look down at the green spreading from my boots. I look up at the Ember King, who is no longer looking at me like a healer he found in a village.

He's looking at me like a man who's been dying for a hundred years and just saw, for the first time, something that might be a cure.

"Change of plans," the Ember King says softly, and the whole host turns to hear it. "We'll take the grain. We'll take the iron." His ember eyes don't leave me. "And we'll take *her.*"

chapters.title: The Court That Was Dying
chapters.body:
They take me across the border before I can decide whether to fight.

It's not a journey so much as a *crossing* — the world thins, the colors run, and then we're through, into the fae lands, and even greyed and dying they're more than my eyes were built for. We ride through a forest of trees taller than cathedrals, their crowns gone ashen at the edges, and over a river that should be silver and runs the color of a bruise, and the whole way the Ember King rides ahead of me in silence and the host keeps me boxed in the middle like something precious or dangerous, and I keep my chin up and my mouth shut and I *watch*, because watching is the only weapon I've ever had.

What I watch is a kingdom dying.

The Ember Court rises out of the grey wood like a coal that's mostly ash — a vast palace of red-gold stone and living wood, towers like flame frozen mid-leap, and over all of it the blight, the greying, eating in at the edges. Courtiers drift through halls that must once have blazed and now only smolder. There's beauty here that would stop your heart, and it's all going out, slowly, like a hundred candles guttering in a room with no one to relight them.

They put me in a chamber finer than anything in Ashford and tell me I'm a guest.

"Guests can leave," I point out to the steward who brings me — an ancient, bent fae with bark-rough skin and eyes like deep wells, who introduces himself as Brambleback and looks at me with an expression I can't read, something between hope and dread. "I'd like to leave. Now, ideally. I have patients."

"You can't leave." Brambleback says it gently, almost apologetically. "When the King named you and you crossed the border, a binding settled. The old law. You are bound to the Ember Court until the King who took you releases you, and—" something flickers in the deep-well eyes "—he will not be releasing you. Not when you do *that.*"

"Do what."

He doesn't answer. He just looks, pointedly, at the floor around my feet — where the greyed moss between the flagstones has gone quietly, impossibly green.

I'm still staring at it when the doors open and the Ember King comes in, and the chamber's failing light seems to lean toward him and away from him at once. Up close, without the crown and the host, he's worse — the fading is so clear on him that it makes my healer's hands itch, an ancient terrible beautiful thing going out at the core.

"You'll have whatever you need," he says, without preamble. His voice really is like fire behind a wall — warmth and danger both, banked low. "Food, books, the run of the inner court. In exchange, you'll do one thing." His ember eyes hold mine. "There's a courtier in the east tower. Lord Fenn. He's fading — faster than the rest, almost gone. My court's magic can't touch the blight; we are *made* of the magic that's dying, we can't heal what's killing us any more than a fire can warm itself." A pause, and beneath the cold I hear something he'd never name: desperation. "But the grey ran from your feet in a mortal village. So you'll go to the east tower, healer, and you'll put your hands on a dying fae lord, and we'll both find out what you are."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then Lord Fenn dies tonight, as he would have anyway, and you'll have learned nothing, and so will I." He turns to go. "I'm told you kept a mortal alive through two winters out of spite. I'm gambling that you can't watch a man die in front of you and do nothing, whatever he is." A glance back, ember-gold and unreadable. "Was I wrong?"

He wasn't, and he knows it, and I hate that he knows it.

They take me to the east tower. Lord Fenn is barely there — a fae gone almost entirely grey, ash-skinned, his ember-light down to the last guttering spark, the blight so deep in him that even I can see he's hours from gone. No fae has been able to touch it. The King watches from the doorway, and Brambleback, and a clutch of silent courtiers, all of them looking at the mortal woman with the dread-and-hope expression I'm beginning to understand means *please* and *what are you* in the same breath.

I kneel beside the dying lord. I don't have herbs for this. I don't have anything for this. So I just do what I've always done — I put my hands on him, flat against his greying chest, and I reach for the thing I reach for with every patient, the wordless *stay, stay, not yet, stay* —

— and something *pours* out of me.

It's not like herbs. It's not like anything. It's warm and bright and it comes from the leaf-shaped birthmark on my wrist, up my arms, out through my palms, and where it touches the grey lord the ash *recedes* — color flooding back, the ember-light in him catching, flaring, *holding* — and Lord Fenn draws a breath that isn't his last, and opens eyes gone gold instead of grey, and the entire east tower goes utterly silent.

I sit back, shaking, staring at my own hands like they belong to someone else.

And in the doorway, the Ember King — the cold, dying, ancient thing who took me in chains — stares at the mortal healer who just did what a hundred years of immortal magic couldn't, and on his terrible beautiful fading face is an expression I'd bet my life no one in this court has seen there in a century.

Hope. Naked, desperate, dangerous hope.

"What," he breathes, "*are* you?"

chapters.title: The Woman in the Tapestry
chapters.body:
"What am *I*?" I'm on my feet now, the warmth still draining out of my hands, Lord Fenn breathing gold behind me and a roomful of immortals staring at me like I've grown a second head. "I'm a village healer. *You're* the one who can't heal your own people. So don't ask me what I am — ask yourself what kind of magic dies of a cold and gets cured by a mortal with dirty hands and no idea what she's doing."

It comes out sharp, because sharp is what I do when I'm frightened, and I am very frightened, because what just poured out of my wrist was not herbs and not anything I learned and not anything a mortal is supposed to be able to do.

The Ember King doesn't answer my question. He answers a different one — the one underneath it.

"Brambleback." His ember eyes haven't left me. "Show her the long gallery."

The steward goes very still. "Sire. Are you certain that's—"

"She mended what we cannot mend." The King's voice is quiet and terrible. "She has earned the right to know why. *Show* her."

So the bent old fae leads me out of the east tower and down a long hall I haven't seen — the gallery, he calls it, a corridor of ancient tapestries running the length of the dying palace, a hundred yards of woven history going grey at the edges like everything else here. Kings and courts and wars and bargains, century on century, threads of ember-gold faded now to ash. Brambleback walks me past all of it in silence, his bark-rough hands trembling, until he stops at the very end, before the oldest tapestry of all, and steps aside so I can see.

It's a mortal woman.

She stands in the woven Ember Court, surrounded by kneeling fae, her hands outstretched and pouring light — the same warm gold that just poured out of my wrist — into a court blazing back to life around her. The threads here are brighter than anywhere else in the gallery, as if this one moment was woven by someone who couldn't bear to let it fade. Below her, in the old fae script I can't read, a single line.

"'The mortal who mended the Ember Court,'" Brambleback translates softly. "'Freely. When all our magic failed.' A hundred years ago, the blight came the first time, and no fae could touch it, and a mortal woman walked into this court and healed it with her bare hands." His deep-well eyes are wet. "And then the court betrayed her. Broke the bargain it made with her. And the old law cursed us for it — the Ember Court to fade, slowly, for a hundred years, until—" He stops. He can't say it.

I'm not really listening anymore. Because I've stepped closer to the woven woman, close enough to see the detail the weaver couldn't bear to leave out, and my whole body has gone cold.

On the woman's outstretched wrist, woven in thread gone bright where everything else has faded, is a birthmark.

A leaf.

The exact leaf-shaped mark I've had on my own left wrist since the day I was born. The one my mother said meant nothing, the one I've never thought about, the one that started pouring light an hour ago in the east tower.

I look down at my wrist. At the leaf. At the woman in the tapestry with the same leaf, a hundred years gone, pouring the same light I just poured.

And behind me — I didn't hear him follow us — the Ember King makes a sound I will never forget. Not a word at first. Just an exhale, ragged, like a man who's been holding his breath for a century and has finally, terribly, let it out.

I turn. He's staring at my wrist. At the leaf. His ash-pale face has gone paler still, the ember in his eyes flaring and shaking, and when he finally speaks his voice is barely there, wrecked, reverent, and afraid:

"You're *hers.*"

"...What?"

"Her blood. Her line." He takes a step toward me, and the cold King, the ancient terrible thing who took me in chains, looks at me like I'm something holy and impossible and long, long awaited. "We thought it died with her. We thought the curse was forever — that there was no one left of her blood to ever mend what we broke. For a hundred years this court has been fading toward nothing, and the one thing in all the worlds that could save it was a bloodline we were *certain* had ended." His hand lifts toward my marked wrist, trembling, not quite daring to touch. "And then a healer with a leaf on her arm steps out of a mortal village, and the blight runs from her feet."

The gallery is silent. A hundred years of fading history watches.

"You're hers," the Ember King whispers again, and now there's a hundred years of grief and hope and something far more dangerous breaking open in his ember eyes. "The court has been dying for a century waiting for you to be born." His gaze lifts from my wrist to my face. "And I just dragged the only thing that can save us across the border in chains — without the faintest idea what I'd found. Without knowing the one thing the old law makes brutally, perfectly clear."

"What thing," I breathe. "What does the law make clear?"

And the Ember King, ageless and dying and afraid, gives me the answer that changes everything:

"That you can't be commanded to save us. You can't be bought, or bound, or made. The curse is mended by your blood *only if it's freely given.*" His hand falls. "Which means I've just spent the one move I had. I took you in chains. And the law says chains are the one thing that can never, ever work."

chapters.title: The Brother at the Gate
chapters.body:
I don't sleep. You'd think a leaf-marked savior of a dying immortal court would get a good night's rest, but mostly I sit on the edge of a too-fine bed in a fading palace and stare at the birthmark on my wrist like it might explain itself.

It doesn't. So in the morning I find Brambleback and I make him explain it instead.

"Start at the beginning," I tell him. "The woman in the tapestry. The bargain. The curse. All of it. No more frightened looks — I poured light out of my arm yesterday, I think I've earned the whole story."

So the old steward tells me, in the long grey gallery under the bright-threaded woman who shares my mark.

A hundred years ago, the blight came the first time. The Ember Court was dying then as it's dying now, and no fae magic could touch it, because the fae *are* the magic — they can't heal the thing that's killing them any more than a flame can put itself out. And a mortal woman wandered across the border, leaf-marked, and discovered she could do what they couldn't. She mended the court. Freely — that's the part Brambleback keeps stressing, *freely*, she chose to, she wasn't made to. And in gratitude the court made a bargain with her, a great and binding one.

And then the court broke it.

"How," I ask.

Brambleback's bark-rough face creases with old shame. "That part is... not mine to tell. The King has forbidden it." He won't meet my eyes. "Know only that she was betrayed, and died of the betrayal, and that the old law — which holds bargains sacred above all things — cursed the Ember Court for breaking faith with the mortal who saved it. We would fade, slowly, a century of guttering, until a mortal of her blood freely chose to mend us again." His deep-well eyes lift. "We believed her line had ended. We believed the curse was a death sentence with no appeal. And then you stepped out of Ashford."

"And the King dragged me here in chains," I say slowly, "which the law says can never work. So why am I still here? Why not let me go and find some *other* way to ask?"

"Because there is no other way. You're the only one of her blood left in all the worlds." Brambleback spreads his hands. "And because the King, having realized his mistake, does not know how to undo it without losing you entirely. He is not a creature who has ever had to ask for anything freely. He's a king. Kings command." A sad almost-smile. "Asking is the one skill the Ember throne never taught him, and now it's the only one that can save us."

I'm turning that over — *the cold King who doesn't know how to ask* — when the palace alarm goes up.

It's not horns this time. It's a clamor, fae voices raised, the ring of iron-warded gates, and Brambleback goes rigid and grips my arm. "Stay here," he says, which of course means I immediately don't, and I follow the noise down the fading halls to the inner court's gate, where the Ember King's guards have someone surrounded — someone who fought his way *in*, which I'd have said was impossible.

He's fae, but warm where the others are cold — fire-bright hair, a quick fierce face, eyes the gold of a real flame instead of a banked one. He's bleeding from getting past the guards and he doesn't seem to care, and when his gold eyes find me across the courtyard they fix on my wrist, on the leaf, and his whole face changes.

"So it's true," he says, and there's grief in it. "You found one. You actually found one." He rounds on the Ember King, who's just arrived, ember-eyed and cold. "A hundred years and the second you find the bloodline that can save you, you drag her here in *chains.* Goddess, brother, you haven't changed at all."

*Brother.*

"This doesn't concern you, Aric," the Ember King says, and his voice could freeze the dying river. "You were exiled. Exiles who return—"

"—can be executed, yes, I remember the law, you wrote half of it." The warm fae — Aric — turns back to me, and his fierce face gentles, and he speaks to me like I'm a person and not a cure, the first one here who has. "Listen to me, leaf-marked. Whatever they've told you, whatever pretty desperate things this court promises — remember what they did to the *last* woman who saved them. They used her, and when the using was done, they broke faith and let her die." His gold eyes are urgent. "He'll do it again. He's not capable of anything else; none of them are. They'll take what your blood can give and discard what's left, exactly like they did to your grandmother's grandmother." He steps toward me, ignoring the blades that rise to stop him. "Don't trust the Ember King. Don't trust *any* of them. The only thing they've ever loved is their own court — and they let the last savior die to keep it."

The guards seize him. The Ember King's face is unreadable stone.

And I stand in the fading courtyard between the cold King who took me in chains and the warm exile who fought his way in to warn me, with a hundred-year-old betrayal woven into the walls around me, and I realize I don't know which of these immortal brothers is lying to me.

Maybe both. Maybe neither.

But I know this: somebody in this beautiful dying court let a woman with my mark die once already.

And I have absolutely no intention of being the second.

chapters.title: Two Brothers, One Cage
chapters.body:
They don't execute Aric. The King wants to — I can see it in the set of his jaw — but I make it very clear, in front of the whole court, that the leaf-marked mortal everyone's so desperate to keep happy will not be healing one single fae if the warm brother gets a blade. So Aric is "confined" instead of killed, in a tower near mine, and I have, apparently, just used my new and terrifying leverage for the first time.

It's a strange power, being the thing a dying kingdom needs. I keep waiting to feel safe with it. I don't. Power you didn't ask for and don't understand is just a different kind of cage.

The King finds me in the gallery that evening, under the bright-threaded woman.

"You spared my brother," he says. Not quite a question.

"I spared a man who fought through your guards to warn me you'd use me and throw me away." I don't look away from the tapestry. "He says that's what you did to her. To my blood. Used her, broke the bargain, let her die." Now I turn. "Is he lying?"

The ember eyes shutter. "It's more complicated than—"

"It's always more complicated. That's what people say when the simple version makes them look bad." I fold my arms. "Here's where we are, King. You need me. Freely — your own law says so, your own steward says so, you said so yourself. You can't command this, can't bargain it, can't chain it. Which means for the first time in your ancient life you have to *earn* something." I tilt my head. "And so far your whole strategy has been to drag me here against my will and stare at me hopefully. So forgive me if I'm not feeling moved to save your kingdom."

For a long moment the Ember King just looks at me, and I brace for cold fury, for the king who commands.

Instead, something flickers — surprise, almost amusement, quickly buried. "No one has spoken to me like that in three hundred years."

"Get used to it. I'm told it's the only skill that can save you." I let that land. "Aric talks to me like I'm a person. You talk to me like I'm a cure. If you want me to *choose* this court, King, you might start by deciding I'm worth talking to."

He has no answer. Or he has one and won't give it, which with him I'm learning is the same thing. He turns to go — and the gallery doors bang open and a new fae sweeps in, and the temperature of the whole court drops ten degrees.

This one's different. Cold the way the Ember Court is warm-going-cold — a deliberate, glittering, winter cold. Silver and thorns and a smile like a frost-flower, an envoy who doesn't kneel to the Ember King and clearly never would.

"Forgive the intrusion, cousin," the newcomer purrs, and the King goes rigid. "My queen sends her regards. And her curiosity." Glittering eyes slide to me — to my wrist, to the leaf — and the frost-flower smile widens. "Because the most interesting rumor reached the Thorn Court this week. That the fading Ember King has found a *changeling-blooded mortal.* A leaf-marked one. The kind that hasn't walked the worlds in a hundred years." The envoy inclines its head to me, mockingly courtly. "Lady Nyx, Queen of Thorns, extends an invitation. She would so love to meet the little mortal who can mend what immortals cannot." A beat, soft as a blade. "One way or another."

The threat hangs glittering in the air.

"She's under Ember protection," the King says, and there's iron in it.

"Is she? Under *protection?*" The envoy laughs, light and cruel. "How charming. We heard you brought her in chains." The glittering eyes find mine one last time, and there's a promise in them that turns my blood cold. "Think on it, leaf-marked. The Ember Court is *dying* — you'd be saving a corpse. The Thorn Court is *ascendant.* My queen could offer you so much more than a fading king and his pretty guilt." A frost-flower smile. "We'll speak again. Soon. Whether the Ember King wishes it or not."

It sweeps out. The court breathes again.

And I stand in the gallery between a cold king, a caged warm brother, and the fresh knowledge that a second, *ascendant* immortal court now wants the leaf-marked mortal too — and I understand, with a sinking certainty, that my blood has just made me the most fought-over thing in two kingdoms.

"Well," I say into the silence. "It seems I'm popular."

The Ember King looks at me, and for once there's no cold in it, only a grim warning. "You don't want to be wanted by Nyx," he says quietly. "Whatever I am — whatever my court did — the Thorn Queen is worse. She doesn't want to save anything. She wants to *own* it." His ember eyes hold mine. "Including you."

chapters.title: Freely Given
chapters.body:
The Thorn envoy's visit changes something. The King stops trying to overawe me and starts, grudgingly, to *explain.*

He takes me to the heart of the palace — a vast round chamber where a great hearth once blazed and now holds only embers, the literal heart-fire of the Ember Court, guttering low. We sit before it, the dying king and the leaf-marked mortal, and he tells me the shape of the curse properly for the first time.

"The old law is older than any court," he says, ember-light playing over his ash-pale face. "Older than kings. It holds one thing sacred above all others: a freely given gift, and the faith you keep with the one who gave it. Break faith with someone who gave you something freely, and the law itself turns against you. That's what we did. She gave us her healing — *freely*, asking nothing — and we made a bargain to repay her, and then we broke it." His jaw tightens. "So the law cursed us in the exact shape of our crime. The court that betrayed a freely given gift would fade — and could only be mended by another freely given gift, from her blood. Not bought. Not bargained. Not commanded. The law won't be fooled; it knows the difference between a choice and a chain." He looks at me. "That's why your healing of Lord Fenn worked but didn't *mend* anything. You did it freely, so the magic flowed — but you didn't *choose the court.* Healing one fae is a kindness. Mending the curse means choosing to save *all* of us, freely, with full knowledge. And that—"

"—you can't make me do," I finish. "Or even ask for, really. Because the second you ask, it's pressure, and pressure isn't free." The cleverness of the curse settles over me, cold and perfect. "The law's locked you in a cage you can only escape if someone *chooses* to let you out — and you're forbidden from so much as rattling the bars."

"Yes." He says it bleakly. "Which is why I should never have taken you in chains. The moment I did, I made the one move guaranteed to fail. I was a hundred years desperate and I saw the blight run from your feet and I — I grabbed." Something that might be shame crosses his ancient face. "Kings grab. It's the only thing the throne ever taught me. And it was exactly the wrong thing."

I study him in the dying firelight. "Then let me go."

His head comes up.

"You heard me. If a choice has to be free, then I have to be free to make it. So let me go. Take off whatever binding the border put on me. Open the gates. Tell me I can walk back to Ashford and never look at your court again." I hold his ember gaze. "And then we'll find out what kind of king you are — and what kind of mortal I am — when there are no chains left in the question at all."

It's a test. We both know it's a test. I'm asking the cold King who only knows how to grab to do the one thing his entire nature rebels against: open his hand, and let the only cure his dying kingdom has ever found simply *walk away*, on the gamble that she might choose to come back.

The embers gutter in the great hearth. A hundred years of fading hangs on his answer. I watch him war with himself — the desperate king who needs me, against the law that says needing me is exactly why he can never hold me.

And the Ember King closes his eyes, and exhales, and says: "Brambleback. Release the binding. Open the gates." His voice is barely steady. "Let her go."

The old steward gasps. "Sire — if she leaves, the curse — *you*—"

"I know what it costs." The King's ember eyes open, and they're fixed on me, and there's a terrible naked thing in them I don't have a name for yet. "Open the gates. She's right. It was never mine to take." A breath. "If she ever comes back to this court, it will be because she *chose* it — or it won't be at all. And I would rather fade honestly than save myself with one more stolen thing."

The binding lifts off me like a held breath let go. The gates of the Ember Court, for the first time in my captivity, stand open.

I'm free. I can walk home.

And I stand in the heart of a dying kingdom, staring at a cold king who just opened his hand and let his only cure go free, and I realize, with a lurch, that he's done the one thing guaranteed to make leaving harder — because it's so much easier to walk away from a captor than from a man who unlocks your cage and means it.

chapters.title: The Grey Wood
chapters.body:
I leave at dawn. Of course I leave. I have patients, a village, a whole mortal life on the other side of the border, and a cold king's open hand is not a reason to stay in a dying fae court that took me in chains.

That's what I tell myself, walking out the open gates into the grey wood, not looking back.

The trouble starts a mile in.

The blight is worse than when they brought me through. The grey wood has greyed further — the cathedral-trees ashen now to their roots, the bruise-colored river gone the color of nothing at all, and the air itself thick and wrong, like breathing through wet wool. I push on. Ashford is past the border; I just have to cross the dying wood and I'll be home.

But the dying wood doesn't want to let me.

It's not an attack. It's worse than an attack — it's an *absence*, the blight pressing in, leaching warmth and breath and the color out of the world and, I realize with creeping horror, out of *me.* My hands go grey at the fingertips. My breath comes shallow. The leaf on my wrist, which poured light so easily in the warm court, flickers and dims out here in the heart of the fade, like a candle in a flood. I make it another half mile and then my legs go and I'm on my knees in ashen moss, and the grey is climbing my arms, and I understand the thing no one thought to tell me:

The blight is everywhere now. And the only place it *recedes* is around me — but only when I'm strong enough to push it. Out here, alone, a mile deep in the dying, with no court's heart-fire to lean on, my own light is going out, and the grey is going to take me the way it's taking everything else.

I'm going to die in the fae lands, a mile from a court I chose to leave.

"Stay," I tell myself, the healer's word, the *not yet, stay* — but it's hard to push light out of a wrist gone grey, hard to remember why I bothered, the fade is so *quiet*, it would be so easy to just—

A sound tears through the grey wood. My name — not commanded, not cold. *Shouted.* Ragged. Desperate.

"*EIRA!*"

And then he's there — the Ember King, crashing through the dying wood on foot, no crown, no host, no dignity, just a terrified immortal running flat-out through the blight toward the place where my light is guttering. He drops to his knees in the ash beside me and gathers me up, and the moment he touches me I feel his own ember-warmth pour into me, slowing the grey, his fading life propping up mine.

"I felt it," he gasps. "The moment your light started going out — I felt it across the whole court, like a thread snapping. *Why did you go so deep, the wood's almost gone, you can't cross it alone—*" He's not making sense, the cold king babbling, holding me against the fade. "I shouldn't have opened the gates. I should have *warned* you, I should have—"

"You opened the gates," I manage, grey-lipped, "because I asked you to. Because it was the only way the choice could be free. Don't you dare regret the one decent thing—" The grey surges; I lose the thread.

"I don't care about *free*," the Ember King snarls — and it's the realest thing he's ever said to me, the cold cracking clean through. "I don't care about the curse, or the court, or the law, or what your blood can do. I felt you dying out here and nothing else mattered. *Nothing.*" His ember eyes blaze into mine, terrified, furious, undone. "Let the court fade. Let me fade with it. Just — *stay*, Eira. Not for the curse. Not freely-given-gift, not any of it. Just stay because I'm asking you to, like a person asking a person, the way you said I never could." His voice breaks completely. "*Please.*"

The cold King, who only knows how to command, on his knees in the dying wood, asking.

And the grey recedes — not because I pushed it. Because *he* did, pouring the last warm thing he has into the leaf-marked mortal he ran a mile through the fade to save, asking instead of taking, for the first time in his ancient life.

"There," I whisper, as the warmth holds the grey at bay between us. "Was that so hard?"

"Yes," he says, wrecked, holding me in the ash. "Goddess, yes. It was the hardest thing I've ever done."

And a mile deep in a dying wood, in the arms of a king who'd rather fade than chain me, I understand that walking away from the Ember Court is going to be the hardest thing *I've* ever done — and that I'm no longer at all sure I'm going to manage it.

chapters.title: What the Curse Is Eating
chapters.body:
He carries me back. I'm too grey-weak to walk and he won't hear of waiting, so the Ember King carries a mortal healer through a mile of dying wood in his arms, and neither of us says anything about it, and both of us know everything's changed.

By the time the court's heart-fire is in sight my color's coming back, the leaf on my wrist warming again as we near the last of the Ember magic. But something's wrong, and as a healer I catch it before he'd ever admit it: with every step back toward the court, *I* get stronger — and *he* gets weaker.

I make him set me down inside the gates. I make him sit. And I do the thing he won't let anyone do: I put my hands on him, healer's hands, flat against his chest, and I *look* at him the way I looked at Lord Fenn.

What I find stops my breath.

The blight isn't just in his court. It's in *him.* Deep — deeper than Lord Fenn, deeper than anyone I've touched. The Ember King is greying from the inside, the fade eating its way through his ancient heart, and he's been hiding it under the crown and the cold the way you'd hide a wound under a fine coat. He spent his ember-warmth in the grey wood saving me, and it cost him — I can see the new grey it left behind, like ash where a coal burned out.

"You're dying," I say. "Not the court. *You.* You're the worst of all of them."

"I'm the *King.*" He says it like it explains everything, and it does. "The court fades; I fade fastest, because I'm bound to it deepest. The curse always meant to take me last and hardest — the throne that broke the bargain, paying longest." His ember eyes are tired. "I have, perhaps, a season left. The court a little longer. After that—" He doesn't finish.

"And my blood slows it," I say, the pieces clicking. "That's what happened just now — every step toward the court, your fade slowed, because I was near. That's why the grey ran from my feet in Ashford. I don't just heal fae. I push the *curse* back. I'm—" The horror of it lands. "I'm the only thing standing between you and gone. And you opened the gates and let me walk into a wood that would've killed me, knowing it might cost you the last season you've got."

"Yes."

"*Why?*"

"Because a season of fading honestly is worth more than a century of holding you in chains." He says it simply, like it's obvious, like he hasn't just rearranged my entire understanding of him. "I told you. I'd rather fade than save myself with one more stolen thing. That includes your blood. That includes *you.*" The ghost of something that isn't quite a smile. "You wanted to know what kind of king I am, Eira. Apparently I'm the kind that opens the cage even when the cage is the only thing keeping him alive. I didn't know that about myself until yesterday. You're a terrible influence."

I sit down hard on the gate-step beside him, my hands still warm from the inside of his fading heart, and I don't leave.

I tell myself it's because I'm too weak to cross the wood. I tell myself it's because Nyx is out there and the court is the safer place. I tell myself a dozen practical healer's reasons, and every one of them is true, and not one of them is the real one.

The real one is that I just held a dying king's curse in my own two hands and felt him choose, again, to let me go free rather than use me — and you cannot walk away from that. I can't, anyway. Apparently I'm a terrible influence and he's a worse one.

"I'm not leaving," I say. "Not today. Don't read anything into it. I'm too grey to cross the wood and you're too far gone to argue, and somebody in this ridiculous court has to keep you breathing long enough to figure out how to break this properly." I fix him with my sharpest healer's glare. "This isn't me choosing your court, King. Your law doesn't get to count this. This is me refusing to let a patient die in front of me out of pure spite, exactly like I did with Halloran. Understood?"

The Ember King looks at me, grey-tired and undone, and the thing that isn't quite a smile finally becomes one — small, real, the first I've seen on him.

"Understood," he says. "Spite. Purely spite."

"Purely."

Neither of us believes it. But it's a place to start — and I have a curse to break, and a king to keep breathing, and exactly no idea yet that the lore Brambleback's still hiding is about to tell me the one thing that could make breaking this curse cost me everything.

chapters.title: The Price She Paid
chapters.body:
I stay "out of spite" for a week, and in a week I do more good than the Ember Court has seen in a century.

I work. It's what I do — give me a problem and a pair of hands and I'll wear it down. I go through the court tower by tower, fae by fae, laying my hands on the worst of the faded and pushing the grey back, and where I work the color holds, the ember-light catches, the dying slows. I can't cure the curse — that needs the mending, the free choice, the thing I'm carefully Not Deciding — but I can hold the line. I can buy the court time it hasn't had in a hundred years.

The fae stop looking at me with dread-and-hope and start looking at me with something simpler, something that unnerves me more: gratitude. They leave herbs at my door because someone told them I like herbs. A faded child — there are children here, which I hadn't let myself think about — brings me a flower that's gone grey and asks, shy, if I can make it bright again, and I can, and her face when it blooms gold in her hands undoes me completely.

I'm not choosing the court. I keep saying that. But I'm learning its name, tower by tower, child by child, and that's how it gets you, isn't it. Not all at once. One bright flower at a time.

Caedmon — he finally gives me his name, *Caedmon*, on the third day, like handing over a weapon — gets stronger as I work, the fade in his heart slowing as the court's slows. He stops hiding the grey from me. We argue constantly. It's the best week I've had in years and I'd die before admitting it.

And on the seventh day, I find Brambleback in the gallery, and I make him finish the story.

"The mending," I say. "The first time. A hundred years ago. She mended the whole court, freely, when the blight first came — you keep saying she *saved* us. But the curse is what came *after*, when you betrayed her. So tell me, steward: the first mending, the one that worked — what did it cost her? Before the betrayal. What did saving you the first time take out of her?"

Brambleback won't look at me. His bark-rough hands twist.

"Steward."

"The old way," he says, very quietly, to the bright-threaded woman in the tapestry. "The way she did it. To mend a fading court of this size, all at once, with a single mortal's blood—" He stops. Starts again. "The light has to come from somewhere, child. When you heal one fae, it costs you a little, and you recover. But to mend an entire court, to push back a curse that's eaten a hundred years deep—" His deep-well eyes finally rise to mine, and they're wet. "She poured out everything. All of it. Her whole life's worth of light, in one giving. The court bloomed. And she—"

He can't say it. He doesn't have to.

"She died," I finish. The gallery has gone very cold. "Mending you the first time *killed* her. Before the betrayal. Before the curse. The healing itself was fatal." I stare at the woman in the threads, her hands pouring light into a blazing court, and now I see what the weaver was really memorializing — not a triumph. A sacrifice. "She walked into a dying court and chose to save it knowing it would cost her her life. And then — *then* — after she'd already given everything — you broke faith with her memory somehow, and the law cursed you on top of it." My voice shakes. "Goddess. You didn't just betray a savior. You betrayed a *martyr.*"

"Yes." Barely a whisper.

And the cold truth settles over me, complete and terrible: the mending Caedmon needs — the free choice to save the whole court that would break the curse — is the same mending that killed the last woman who did it.

Free choice. Whole court. A single mortal's blood.

It cost her everything. It would cost me everything.

"Does he know?" I ask. My voice is very quiet now. "Caedmon. Does the King know that mending his court the old way would kill me?"

Brambleback's silence is the loudest answer I've ever heard.

"He knows," I say. The pieces lock into place — the open gates, the *I'd rather fade than use you*, the refusal to ask. "That's why he won't ask me to choose the court. That's why he opened the cage and let me walk into a wood that nearly killed me. It was never just about the law and the free choice." I press a hand to the gallery wall to steady myself. "He won't ask me to mend the curse *because mending it the old way would kill me — and he'd rather fade, him and his whole court and every faded child in it, than watch another woman with my mark pour out her life to save him.*"

And somewhere in the fading palace, a dying king is keeping a secret he thinks is a kindness — and I have never in my life been angrier at being protected.

chapters.title: The Kindness I Didn't Ask For
chapters.body:
I find Caedmon in the heart-fire chamber and I don't bother being gentle about it.

"The mending kills the healer." I say it flat, from the doorway, and watch him go still. "The old way. The free choice to save the whole court — it's the same thing that killed her. You've known since the moment you saw my wrist. That's why you won't ask. That's why you opened the gates. That's why you'd rather fade — you, the court, every faded child in those towers — than tell me the one thing that could save you, because the saving would cost me my life." My voice is shaking with fury. "You've been *protecting* me. Without asking. Deciding my death wasn't mine to risk. How *dare* you."

He doesn't deny it. He never denies things; it's one of the few honest things about him.

"Yes," Caedmon says quietly. "I've known. And yes, I decided." He rises from beside the guttering heart-fire, and the grey in him is plain now, the fade he's stopped hiding from me. "You're angry that I made the choice for you. You're right to be. It's the same arrogance that made me take you in chains — *kings decide.*" He spreads his hands. "But I'll make this decision again, every time. I will fade, and let my court fade, before I tell you something designed to make you walk into your own death to save me. I watched the tapestry my whole reign, Eira. I have spent a hundred years looking at a woman who poured out her entire life for a court that then betrayed her. I will not — I *will not* — be the king who lets it happen twice." His ember eyes blaze. "So be as angry as you like. Hate me for it. I'd rather you hate me and live."

And the terrible thing is I can't even properly argue, because it's not cruelty, it's the exact opposite, it's the most selfless thing anyone's ever tried to do for me and it makes me want to throw something at his beautiful dying head.

"That wasn't yours to decide," I say, but it's lost most of its heat. "My life. My blood. My choice — *freely*, isn't that your whole sacred law? You don't get to protect me out of the freedom your own curse requires."

"I know." Wretched. "Believe me, I know the irony. The law demands you choose freely, and I love you too much to let you choose the thing that—"

He stops.

The word hangs in the heart-fire chamber, enormous, neither of us breathing.

"—the court," he finishes, too late, the recovery fooling no one. "To save the court."

"You said—"

The alarm goes up.

Not horns. Not the clamor of an intruder. This is the deep, world-wrong sound of the Ember Court's wards being struck all at once — and through the high windows I see it, the thing that's been coming since the Thorn envoy smiled at my wrist: silver and thorns and winter-cold pouring out of the grey wood toward the fading court, an army of frost-pale fae with a banner of black thorns.

"Nyx," Caedmon breathes, the almost-confession swept away by war. "She's not waiting for an invitation. She's come to *take* you." He's already moving, the king snapping back over the man. "She knows the court is fading. She knows you can mend it — or that her own court could use your blood. She's decided not to risk you choosing us." He seizes a blade that burns with banked ember-light. "Stay behind the heart-fire wards. Whatever happens, do not let the Thorn Court take you — Nyx wants you alive, which is somehow worse than wanting you dead."

"Caedmon." I catch his arm at the door. The grey wood is full of silver advancing. "What you just said. Before the alarm. Finish it. After. You're going to finish that sentence."

He looks at me, ember and grey and terrified — of the army, of the word he didn't mean to say, of all of it.

"If we live through tonight," the Ember King says, "I'll finish anything you want." And he goes out to meet Nyx's army with a hundred years of fade in his heart and the unfinished word still burning between us.

chapters.title: The Blight Is Mine Too
chapters.body:
I don't stay behind the wards. Of course I don't.

I make it as far as the courtyard wall before the battle finds me — and it's not a battle the Ember Court can win. The fae here are fading; their ember-light gutters even as they fight, while Nyx's Thorn warriors come on fresh and silver and cruel, winter-strong, driving the dying court back across its own greying stones. I see Caedmon at the gate, blazing brighter than he should, spending fade he can't afford to hold the line, and I understand he'll burn out the last of himself before he lets them reach me.

So I do the thing no one's thought to try, because no one here understands what I am yet — including me.

A Thorn warrior breaks through and comes for me across the courtyard, all silver blade and frost-flower cruelty, and I don't run. I throw out my hand the way I throw it toward a dying patient — but this time I don't reach for *stay, heal, hold.* I'm too frightened and too furious for that. This time I reach for the grey. For the blight. For the thing that's been eating this world and runs from my feet.

And I find I can *push* it.

The blight pours off me — not healing, the opposite, the fade itself, leaping off my marked wrist in a wave of grey that I don't *make* so much as *aim* — and it hits the Thorn warrior full in the chest, and his silver winter-strength goes ashen in an instant, his ember—no, his frost-light guttering out, the immortal dropping to his knees greyed and gasping in the space of a breath.

The whole courtyard stops.

Because the leaf-marked mortal didn't heal him. She *faded* him. I can push the grey *back* — and I can push it *out*, into anything fae, friend or foe, the blight answering my blood like a hound that's finally found its master's hand.

I'm horrified. And I use it anyway, because Caedmon is burning out at the gate and there's no time to be horrified.

I wade into the battle and I do not heal. I *unmake.* Every Thorn warrior who comes near me touches my outflung hand and goes grey, the winter-cold leached out of them, the fade I've spent a week pushing back out of the Ember fae now pouring *into* Nyx's army by my will. They break. An immortal host that's never feared anything breaks and runs from a single mortal woman flinging the blight off her wrist like a weapon, and the Ember Court — stunned, saved — holds its ground behind me.

When it's over I'm shaking in the middle of a courtyard full of greyed, gasping Thorn fae, and the whole Ember Court is staring at me with an expression that's left dread-and-hope and gratitude far behind.

Now they look at me like I'm something to be *afraid* of. And they're right.

Caedmon reaches me first, grey-burned and breathing hard. "What did you—"

"I don't *know.*" My voice cracks. "I pushed the grey back all week. Tonight I pushed it *out.* It's the same thing, Caedmon, it's the same gift — I can take the fade away or I can *give* it, and I didn't know, I didn't know I could—" I look at my own hands, at the leaf, at the greyed immortals at my feet. "What am I, that I can do *that?* Heal isn't the word. Whatever runs in my blood doesn't heal the blight. It *commands* it."

"Later." Caedmon's hand closes over mine, warm and grey and steadying. "You saved us. Whatever it costs to understand it, you saved us tonight, and you can be horrified later. We all can." But I see it cross his ancient face too — the new question, the new fear: *what is she, exactly, that the curse itself obeys her?*

It's Aric who answers, limping out of the broken gate where he fought free of his confinement to join the defense, fire-bright and bleeding and looking at me with something between awe and grief.

"I'll tell you what you are, leaf-marked," Aric says quietly, so only Caedmon and I can hear, his gold eyes flicking to his brother with an old, hard warning. "You're something the lore says can mend *or* corrupt any court that's ever existed. And there's a way to break this curse that doesn't cost you your life — a third way, not the old sacrifice." His jaw tightens. "My brother knows it. He's known the whole time. And he will *die* before he tells you what it is — because the third way costs him something he's decided he'd rather lose his court than spend."

chapters.title: The Third Way
chapters.body:
Caedmon won't speak of it. So I go to the one who will.

Aric heals slowly in his tower — he won't leave now, exile or no, not with Nyx's army licking its wounds in the grey wood and his brother's court hanging by a thread — and I bring him salves and sit with him and, between us, we dig into the forbidden lore. He knows where the oldest scrolls are kept. I know how to read a problem until it gives up its shape. And there's something easy between us, warm, uncomplicated in a way nothing with Caedmon will ever be — Aric laughs readily, treats me like an equal, never once looks at me like a cure or a weapon or a sacrifice.

I can see why the lore would call it a triangle. I can see how, in some other story, I might fall for the warm brother instead of the cold king. Aric's the safe choice. The kind one. The one who fought through the gate to *warn* me instead of *use* me.

But safe has never been the thing my heart reaches for, and I'm honest enough to know it.

"Here," Aric says one evening, unrolling a scroll gone soft with age. "I've been circling it for years — it's why I was exiled, really. I found this and confronted Caedmon with it and he banished me rather than discuss it." His gold eyes are grim. "Read."

I read. The old fae script is hard, but Aric translates the part that matters, and the world rearranges itself again.

There are two ways to mend the curse.

The first is the old way — the sacrifice. A mortal of the blood pours out their whole life's light to mend the court all at once. It works. It's fatal. It's what killed the woman in the tapestry.

But there's a second way. A *true bond, freely chosen.* If a mortal of the blood and the Ember King form a true bond — love, freely given on both sides, with no bargain and no coercion — the mending happens slowly, *gradually*, drawing on the bond itself instead of on a single fatal outpouring. The court heals over time. The curse breaks. And the mortal *lives* — because the light comes from the bond, renewed, instead of from one lethal giving.

Love, not sacrifice. A way to break the curse without dying.
