"He knows about this," I say slowly, the scroll trembling in my hands. "Caedmon knows there's a way that doesn't kill me. A way where I *live.*"

"He's known longer than I have." Aric's voice is hard. "He exiled me for bringing it up. So ask yourself, leaf-marked — why would a dying king, who needs this curse broken more than anything in the worlds, hide the *one path that saves both his court and your life?* Why let himself fade, let his people fade, rather than tell you there's a way where everybody lives?" His gold eyes hold mine. "I told you the first day. They use, and they discard. Maybe the third way disgusts him — bonding with a mortal, letting the throne's salvation depend on *love* instead of *power.* Maybe his pride would rather die than be saved by a feeling." He shrugs, bitter. "Or maybe my brother's exactly as cold as the court made him, and a king who can't love simply can't walk the one road that needs it."

I should believe Aric. It's the simplest explanation, and he's been honest with me from the start, and the lore is right there in my hands.

But I keep hearing it. The thing Caedmon said in the heart-fire chamber, before the alarm swept it away. *I love you too much to let you choose the thing that—* The word he caught and buried. The open gates. The mile he ran through the dying wood. The hundred years he spent staring at a woman who poured out her life, swearing he'd never let it happen twice.

"No," I say slowly, setting the scroll down. "That's not why he's hiding it, Aric. I don't think your brother's hiding the third way because he *can't* love." I look toward the heart-fire chamber, where a cold king is fading rather than ask me for anything. "I think he's hiding it because he *does* — and the third way needs me to love him *freely*, and he's terrified that if I knew it would save his life and his whole court, I'd talk myself into the feeling out of pity. Out of duty. To save everyone." The truth of it lands cold and clear. "He's not hiding a path he finds disgusting. He's hiding the one path he wants most in the world — because the moment he tells me my love could save him, my love stops being free. And he'd rather fade than have me love him for any reason but the true one."

Aric stares at me.

"That," he says finally, quietly, "is either the most romantic thing I've ever heard, or my brother has finally found a way to be a martyr and a coward at the same time." A reluctant, crooked smile. "Knowing Caedmon — probably both."

And I sit in the tower with the scroll that says I could live, and love, and save everything — and the unbearable knowledge that the man it depends on will never, ever tell me, because telling me would ruin the only thing that could work.

chapters.title: The Sentence He Finished
chapters.body:
I go to Caedmon that night and I make him finish the sentence.

He's by the heart-fire, as always, where the last of the court's warmth lives and where he goes to fade in private. The grey is deep in him now — the battle cost him a season he didn't have. He looks up when I come in, and he knows, I think, from my face, that the time for hiding is over.

"I read the lore," I say. "The third way. The bond. The path where I *live*, and your court *lives*, and the only price is a love freely given on both sides." I cross to the fire and stand over him. "You've known the whole time. You let me believe the only options were the old sacrifice or letting everyone fade. You let me grieve a death that doesn't have to happen. *Why.*"

"You know why." He says it tiredly. "You've worked it out, or you wouldn't be looking at me like that."

"I want to hear you say it. Finish the sentence you started before the alarm." I crouch down so we're eye to eye, mortal and dying king, the fire guttering between us. "*I love you too much to let you choose the thing that—* Finish it, Caedmon. All of it. No more protecting me from words."

For a long moment the heart-fire pops and the dying king is silent.

Then Caedmon, who only ever learned to command, who took me in chains and opened the cage and ran a mile through the fade rather than hold me, gives me the whole truth at last.

"I love you too much to let you choose the thing that kills you," he says quietly. "And I love you too much to *tell* you about the thing that doesn't — because the moment you know your love could save my life and my court and every faded child in those towers, you'll never be able to trust your own heart again. You'll lie awake wondering if you love me or if you talked yourself into it to save us all. You'll give me a feeling out of *duty* and call it free, and the law might even accept it, and it would work, and the court would bloom—" His ember eyes are wet. "—and I would spend the rest of eternity beside a woman who could never be sure she'd have chosen me if the worlds hadn't been on fire. I would rather fade. I would rather take my whole court into the dark with me than have *that.*" His voice breaks completely. "So yes. I hid it. Because the only version of the third way I could bear is the one where you fall in love with me knowing *nothing* depends on it — and the second I tell you, that version dies forever."

The fire gutters. I can't breathe.

"That's why I opened the gates," he goes on, wrecked. "Why I keep letting you go. Why I'll never ask, never hint, never spend one word trying to win you. Because the only love that can save me is the one I'm forbidden to reach for — and the only way to keep it pure is to act, always, as if it can't happen at all." A bleak ghost of a smile. "My brother thinks I'm a coward who can't love. The truth is the opposite, and it's so much stupider. I love you enough to die rather than manipulate you into saving me. Aric would call that a martyr's vanity. He's probably right. I don't know how to be any other way. The throne never taught me how to want something and *not* take it — except, apparently, this. Except you."

I'm crying. I didn't mean to. The most arrogant, cold, grabbing creature in two kingdoms has found the one thing he refuses to grab, and it's the thing that would save his life, and it's *me.*

"You impossible man," I whisper. "You'd really fade. You'd really let it all go dark. Rather than say one word that might tip me toward you on purpose."

"Yes," Caedmon says simply. "Because if you ever love me, Eira, I need it to be the realest thing you've ever felt — not the kindest, not the most useful. The realest. And I can only have that if I never, ever ask."

And I understand, kneeling by the dying fire, that he's just done the cruelest generous thing imaginable: he's told me the whole truth, *knowing* it now poisons my heart with the exact doubt he was trying to spare me — because I asked, and he won't lie to me, even when the lie would protect the only thing that could save him.

He's chosen honesty over his own survival. Again.

And now I have to figure out whether what I feel for this impossible, fading, honest king is real — or whether, now that I know everything depends on it, I'll ever be able to trust my own heart again.

chapters.title: The Doubt and the Trade
chapters.body:
The doubt does exactly what he feared. It poisons everything.

For days after, I can't be near Caedmon without the question screaming in me: *is this real, or do I just need it to be?* Every time my heart leaps at his dry voice, every time I want to smooth the grey from his fading brow, the doubt is right there — *you'd feel this anyway if it could save a hundred children, wouldn't you? How would you ever know the difference?* He was right. Telling me ruined it. And he chose to ruin it rather than lie, which only makes me feel it *more*, which only feeds the doubt, around and around.

I pull back. I keep healing the court — holding the line, pushing the grey, buying time — but I keep my distance from the king, and he lets me, because of course he does, he'll never reach. We orbit each other through the fading halls, two people in love and forbidden by the cruelest law from saying so, and it's its own kind of slow fade.

It's Aric, healing and blunt, who finally cuts through it.

"You're miserable," he observes, finding me in the gallery under the bright-threaded woman. "Both of you. The whole court can feel it. You've gone cold and he's gone grey and the blight's creeping back in the places you've stopped tending because you can't bear to be near him." He folds his arms. "So I'm going to tell you the thing my idiot brother is too noble to say, because somebody in this family should be useful." His gold eyes hold mine. "The curse can't *make* you love him, Eira. That's the part you've twisted yourself sick over. The third way doesn't conjure a feeling — it can only *use* one that's already, truly there. The law tests for free love precisely because love can't be forced, not even by a curse, not even by a hundred dying children." He says it gently. "If you didn't love him, you could stand in that heart-fire chamber and *want* to save us with everything in you, and nothing would happen. The mending only works if the feeling is *already real.* So the doubt's a liar. If it's there to use — it's yours. Full stop."

I stare at him. "You're helping me love your brother. The brother who exiled you."

"I'm helping you save a court full of people who don't deserve to fade for two stubborn fools' feelings." A crooked, sad smile. "And — yes. Him too. He's an impossible martyr and he wronged me and he's still my brother, and you're the first thing in a century that's made him act like a person instead of a throne." He shrugs. "I told you on the first day not to trust them. I was wrong about him, at least. Took me watching him choose to fade rather than manipulate you to see it. Don't make his idiocy mean something by letting the court die over a doubt that isn't even true."

It's the thing I needed. Permission to trust my own heart — from the one person with every reason to want me to choose otherwise.

And I'm just turning toward the heart-fire chamber, finally, to go and tell Caedmon that the doubt is a liar and the feeling is real and *mine* —

— when the gallery doors blow open on a wave of winter, and a Thorn envoy strides in flanked by frost-pale warriors, and the temperature drops, and in the envoy's grip, bound in thorns, bleeding, is *Aric* —

No. Aric is beside me. I look, confused — and then I understand, and the bottom drops out of everything.

The envoy isn't holding Aric.

It's holding *Caedmon.*

They took the king. While I was in the gallery learning to trust my heart, Nyx's people slipped the fading court's broken wards and took its fading king, and now a Thorn envoy stands in the Ember gallery with the Ember King bound and bleeding in winter-thorns, and the frost-flower smile turns on me.

"Lady Nyx sends terms," the envoy purrs. "The leaf-marked mortal walks into the Thorn Court, freely, alone — and the Ember King lives. Refuse, and we open his throat on the Thorn Queen's steps at moonrise." The smile widens. "She was very clear about *freely*, little healer. She's done her reading too. You come of your own will, or it's worth nothing to her." A glittering pause. "You have until moonrise to choose. Choose wrong, and the king you can't decide whether you love dies before you ever get to find out."

chapters.title: Freely, Into Winter
chapters.body:
"You can't," Aric says. "Eira. You *can't.* The second you walk into the Thorn Court, Nyx has the one thing in two kingdoms that can mend or corrupt any court alive. She'll never let you out. This is exactly what she wants — you, willing, walking to her on your own feet, because she knows your blood is worth nothing to her forced."

"I know." I'm already pulling on my cloak. Moonrise is hours away and the walk is long. "That's the trap. I see the trap. I'm walking into it anyway."

"*Why?*"

I stop at the gallery doors, under the bright-threaded woman who poured out her life for this court, and I finally understand her — really understand her, the woman in the tapestry, the choice she made.

"Because two minutes ago you told me the doubt was a liar," I say quietly. "You told me the curse can't make me love him — that if the feeling's there to use, it's mine. And I was walking to the heart-fire chamber to tell him so, when they brought him in bound in thorns." I look at Aric, and my voice doesn't shake, because I've never been more certain of anything. "I have my answer, Aric. I figured it out the instant I saw it was *him* in those thorns instead of you. I felt the bottom drop out of the world. Not *the court will fade* — *he* will die. That's the difference. That's how I know it's real." I pull the cloak tight. "I'm not walking into the Thorn Court to save the Ember Court. I'd let the whole court fade before I'd let myself be a tool again. I'm walking in there because I love him, and Nyx is going to open his throat at moonrise, and there is no version of me that stands in a gallery and lets that happen."

Aric stares at me. Then, slowly, the fight goes out of him, replaced by something like awe and grief.

"He's going to be *furious*," Aric says. "He spent everything not to manipulate you into saving him, and you're about to walk into a death-trap *for* him anyway, of your own free will, which is the one thing he can't forbid."

"Good. Let him be furious. It's the most honest thing I can do." A breath. "Here's what I need from you, Aric. I can't fight my way out of the Thorn Court alone — but I can buy time, because Nyx needs me *willing*, and willing can be slow. So I walk in the front, freely, and keep her talking. And you—" I grip his arm "—you take whatever's left of the Ember host, and you come in the back, hard, while her eyes are on me. Can you do that? Lead them? For the brother who exiled you?"

Aric looks at me for a long moment. Then the warm fierce grin I first saw at the gate breaks across his face, edged now with something steelier.

"For the brother who exiled me," he says, "and the queen he's too noble to admit he wants? Leaf-marked, I'll burn the Thorn Court to its roots." He clasps my arm back. "Go. Walk slow. Keep her talking. And Eira—" the grin softens "—when this is over, and you tell my idiot brother you love him: make him work for it. He spent a century learning to grab and a season learning to let go. Make him learn to *ask.*"

So I walk into winter.

The grey wood gives way to frost as I cross toward the Thorn Court, alone, on my own two feet, the leaf on my wrist warm against the deepening cold. I'm walking into a trap with my eyes open, to save a king who'd rather fade than ask me to, against a queen who needs me willing and is about to discover exactly how dangerous a *willing* changeling can be.

Behind me, somewhere in the grey, Aric is gathering a dying court for one last desperate ride.

Ahead, in a palace of thorns and frost, Nyx is waiting with a blade at Caedmon's throat and a hundred years of malice in her smile.

And for the first time since I poured light out of my wrist in a dying lord's tower, I am not afraid of what I am.

I'm counting on it.

chapters.title: The Queen of Thorns
chapters.body:
The Thorn Court is everything the Ember Court isn't: ascendant, gleaming, cruelly alive. Where Caedmon's halls fade and gutter, Nyx's blaze with cold silver light, frost-flowers blooming on black thorns, a court at the height of its power feeding, I realize, on everything its rival is losing. They're not just rivals. The Thorn Court is *fattening* on the Ember Court's fade.

They bring me before the throne, and Nyx is worse than her envoy promised — beautiful the way a blizzard is beautiful, silver-haired and frost-eyed, a smile like a knife you don't feel going in. And there, below her dais, bound in living thorns that bite deeper when he moves, grey and fading and furious, is Caedmon.

His ember eyes find me the instant I enter, and the fury in them is exactly what Aric promised — *you came, you impossible woman, you walked into her trap, I spent everything so you'd never have to* — but underneath the fury is something that undoes me: relief, and terror, and a love he's no longer pretending isn't there, because what's the point now.

"You came freely," Nyx purrs, delighted. "I felt it cross my wards — willing, every step. *Excellent.*" She descends from her throne, circling me like the cold circles a flame. "Do you know how long I've waited for one of your blood, little healer? A hundred years. Since I made sure the last one died." 

The court goes still. Caedmon goes rigid in his thorns.

"You—" he starts.

"Oh, did your steward never tell you the *whole* tale, Ember King?" Nyx laughs, a sound like ice cracking. "How could he. He never knew." She turns to me, savoring it. "Your court didn't simply *break* the bargain with the mortal who saved it, a century ago. It was *made* to. I was young then, and ambitious, and I saw a rival court bloom because a leaf-marked mortal had poured out her life to save it — and I knew that as long as the Ember Court kept faith with her memory, it would stay strong." Her frost-smile widens. "So I arranged the betrayal. I whispered the lies that made the Ember King break faith with his dead savior. I *engineered* the broken bargain — knowing the old law would curse them for it, knowing the curse would make them fade, knowing my court would feast on their fading for a hundred years." She spreads her arms at her gleaming, ascendant hall. "Everything you see, Ember King. Everything I am. I built it on a curse I tricked your grandfather into earning. Your hundred years of dying have been my hundred years of *growing.*"

The truth lands like a blow. The Ember Court's whole tragedy — the curse, the fade, the century of dying — wasn't a punishment they simply earned. It was *murder*, slow and engineered, by the queen now smiling at me with a blade in reach of Caedmon's throat.

"And now," Nyx says, turning the full weight of her frost-eyes on me, "the bloodline I thought I'd ended a century ago walks into my court of her own free will. Do you understand what you are to me, little healer? You can mend a fading court — *or corrupt a strong one.* With your willing blood, I don't just feast on the Ember Court's death. I rule *everything.* Every court in every world, mended or ruined at my word, through the leaf on your wrist." She extends one pale, perfect hand. "So here is my offer, and it's better than anything that fading corpse of a king can give you. Be my queen-tool, my changeling crown. Rule the worlds beside me. And all you have to do—" the frost-smile turns to ice "—is choose me freely, the way you chose to walk in here. Because your blood is worth nothing to me forced, and we both know it."

Caedmon strains against the thorns, bleeding. "Eira, *don't*—"

But I'm not listening to him. I'm looking at Nyx, and I'm doing the thing I do best — taking stock, reading the room, finding the wrong.

And I've just found it. *Your blood is worth nothing to me forced.* She needs me willing. She *needs* me willing. Which means, for all her thorns and her army and the blade at Caedmon's throat —

— the Queen of Thorns can't actually make me do anything at all.

"Tell me more," I say to Nyx, pleasant as anything, settling in, buying every second I can. "About this ruling-the-worlds plan. I find I have so many questions."

Somewhere out in the grey, if Aric kept his word, a dying court is riding hard for the back gate.

I just have to keep the most dangerous queen in two kingdoms talking until they get here.

chapters.title: What a Willing Changeling Can Do
chapters.body:
I keep Nyx talking for a quarter of an hour, and a quarter of an hour is a long time when a court is riding to your rescue.

It's not hard. Powerful things love to explain themselves — Augustus would have understood Nyx perfectly; they're the same creature, the ones who price everything and mistake it for wisdom. So I ask, and she expounds, vain and certain, about her century of patient feasting, her plans for the other courts, the worlds she'll mend and corrupt at a whim once she has my willing blood on a leash. And the whole time I'm watching Caedmon bleed in his thorns, and watching the frost-warriors at the edges of the hall, and *counting.*

"You've thought of everything," I tell Nyx admiringly, when I judge the time is right. "Truly. A hundred-year plan, executed flawlessly. Just one small problem."

The frost-smile flickers. "Problem."

"You keep saying my blood is worthless to you forced. That you need me *willing.*" I tilt my head. "Which means you've spent this whole conversation being very careful not to threaten me directly. Lots of threatening *him*—" I nod at Caedmon "—but never me, never my body, never pain, because you can't risk making me unwilling. You need my consent so badly you've handcuffed yourself with it." I take one step toward her throne, and the warriors tense, and she lifts a hand to still them, because she *can't* let them hurt me. "Here's the problem, Nyx. You've been so focused on what my willing blood can do *for* you that you never once asked what my *unwilling* blood can do *to* you."

Her frost-eyes narrow. "Your gift is mending. Corrupting. It needs your will to—"

"To *mend.* To *corrupt.* The big, clean, willing magic — yes." I let the leaf on my wrist begin to warm, and I see her notice, see the first flicker of doubt. "But I learned something in the Ember courtyard three nights ago, fighting your army. Mending isn't the only thing this blood does." I raise my hand, and the grey — the blight, the fade, the thing that runs from my feet and leaps from my wrist — gathers cold and ready in my palm. "It commands the blight. And the blight, Your Majesty, doesn't care one bit whether I'm willing. *That* part's just instinct. Just a frightened changeling throwing the fade at whatever's threatening the man she loves."

And I throw it.

Not at Nyx — she's too strong, too ready, ringed in thorn-magic. I throw it at the *thorns.* At the living winter-thorns binding Caedmon, the ones rooted in Nyx's own power — and the blight pours off my wrist and hits them and they go grey and brittle and *dead* in an instant, crumbling to ash, and Caedmon is free.

The hall erupts. Nyx shrieks for her warriors. And that's the exact moment — *moonrise*, right on time, Aric's timing flawless — that the back gates of the Thorn Court blow inward on a wave of ember-gold, and the Ember host pours in behind a fire-bright exile with a blade in each hand, and the most dangerous queen in two kingdoms suddenly has a war in her own throne room.

"NOW, EIRA!" Aric roars over the chaos.

I'm already moving — to Caedmon, hauling him up out of the crumbled thorns, his grey-faded weight against me. "Can you walk?"

"For you," he rasps, ember eyes blazing through the grey, "I can do considerably more than walk." And even now, even bleeding and fading in a war he's losing, the impossible man almost smiles. "You walked into the Thorn Court. *Freely.* For me. After everything I did to make sure you'd never have to—"

"Yell at me later." I get his arm over my shoulders and we move, through the chaos, toward the ember-gold and the exit and Aric carving a path. "We're not done. Nyx engineered the whole curse — a hundred years of your court dying was *her*, Caedmon, all of it, murder dressed as law. This doesn't end with us escaping."

"I know." His jaw sets, grey and furious. "But it doesn't end *here*, either, not while she holds the high ground and I'm running on fumes." We reach Aric, the three of us cutting toward the gate, the Ember host closing around us. "We get out. We regroup. And then—" his ember eyes find mine, and there's a hundred years of grief and a fresh century of fury in them "—and then I'm going to tell you the thing I should have told you in the heart-fire chamber. The thing I've been too noble and too frightened to say." A breath, even as we run. "If we live through this — Eira, I'm done being a martyr about it."

We hit the grey wood at a dead run, the Thorn Court burning behind us, and for the first time the Ember King isn't fading away from me.

He's running *toward* something.

chapters.title: The Shield in the Grey
chapters.body:
We don't make it clean. Nyx doesn't lose a war in her own throne room and simply let us walk home.

Halfway across the grey wood her warriors run us down — frost-pale and furious, faster than the fading Ember host, and Aric's wolves turn to hold them while the three of us run for the border. But there are too many, and Caedmon is running on the last fumes of a century, and a Thorn warrior breaks past Aric's line and hurls a lance of winter-thorn straight at me.

I don't see it. I'm watching the ground, watching Caedmon's failing steps.

Caedmon sees it.

And the Ember King — grey, fading, a season from gone, with nothing left to spend — spends it anyway. He throws himself in front of me and throws up a wall of ember-fire, the last great blaze of a dying king, and the winter-lance shatters against it, and the warriors recoil from the heat, and we live.

But it costs him everything he had left.

I feel it through the leaf on my wrist, through the half-formed thread between us — the ember-warmth in him guttering down to almost nothing, the grey rushing in to fill the space where his power was. He's used the last of a hundred years to shield me from a single lance, and now there's almost nothing left of him at all.

He goes down in the ashen moss. I catch him.

"Caedmon. Caedmon, *no*—" I've got my hands on him, pouring everything I can, the leaf blazing, pushing the grey back out of his fading heart — and it helps, it slows the going-out, but it's like bailing the sea with a cup. He spent too much. "Why would you — I was *fine*, I'd have seen it, you didn't have to—"

"I did, actually." His voice is barely there, ember eyes dim, but he's *smiling*, the wretch, the impossible wretch. "Turns out there's exactly one thing the throne never taught me to calculate. The cost of *you* getting hurt. Comes out as infinity. Breaks the whole ledger." His grey hand finds my face. "Don't waste your light on me. Save it. The court—"

"Stop telling me to save my light." I'm crying again, hauling him up, Aric's host closing protectively around us as we limp the last stretch to the border. "Stop deciding what I do with it. You've spent a hundred years and your whole last season making sure I never have to choose anything for you — well, I'm choosing *now*, Caedmon, and you don't get a vote."

We cross the border into the Ember lands as the sun comes up grey behind us. Aric's host holds the Thorn warriors at the boundary; Nyx, it seems, won't cross into Ember territory in force, not yet, not until she's regrouped from the burning I left in her throne room. We have a reprieve. Hours, maybe a day.

And I get Caedmon back to the heart-fire chamber, and I lay him down beside the guttering coals that are all that's left of his court's warmth, and I take stock the way I always do — and what I find empties me out.

He's almost gone. The shield in the grey wood spent the last reserve a king holds back even from death. My light is holding him — barely — but the moment I stop, the moment I sleep, the moment my own strength flags, the grey will take him. I can't pour enough into him to *heal* this. I can only hold the line, hour by hour, while we both run down.

There's only one thing that can actually save him now. I know it. He knows it. We've both known since the gallery.

The third way. The bond, freely chosen. The mending that heals the court *and* the king *and* lets me live — but only if the love is real and freely given, with nothing forced.

And here we are: a dying king who'd rather fade than ask, and a healer who finally knows her own heart, with the whole thing hanging on whether she can convince herself — and the cruel old law — that choosing him *now*, with his life in the balance, is still a free choice and not a desperate one.

"Caedmon." I take his grey hand. His eyes flutter open, dim ember. "You said if we lived through it, you'd tell me the thing. You're alive. Barely, you idiot, but alive. So no more being noble." I hold his fading gaze. "Tell me. And then it's my turn to choose — *freely* — and I need you to trust that I know my own heart, even now, especially now."

chapters.title: Freely, Eyes Open
chapters.body:
"The third way," Caedmon says, grey and fading by the guttering heart-fire, "is a bond. You know that. A true love, freely given on both sides — and the curse mends slowly, drawing on the bond instead of on one fatal outpouring. The court heals. I heal. And you *live.*" His dim ember eyes hold mine. "I hid it because telling you poisons it — because the moment you know your love can save me, you'll never trust whether it's real. And now I'm dying in front of you, which makes it worse, because now any love you offer me looks like mercy." A breath. "So here's the cruelest part, Eira, the part I've dreaded most: I can't ask you. I *still* can't ask you. Even now. Even like this. If you do it, it has to be because you'd have done it anyway — with no court dying, no king fading, no law, nothing in the balance but the two of us in an empty room." His hand tightens weakly on mine. "Can you even know that? With all this? I don't see how you could. Which is why I never meant to tell you. Which is why I'd rather have faded."

And there it is — the whole impossible knot, laid bare. He won't ask. The doubt is real. The stakes are everything. The law won't be fooled.

So I do the only thing that can cut it.

"You're right that I can't *prove* it's free," I say quietly. "Not with you dying, not with the court fading, not with a law watching. There's no version of this moment that's clean. You ruined clean the second you saved me in the grey wood." I move closer, take his grey face in both my hands. "But here's what I figured out walking into the Thorn Court, Caedmon — the thing that finally killed the doubt. When the envoy dragged in a body bound in thorns and I thought for one second it was Aric, I felt *fear.* When I saw it was you, I felt the bottom drop out of the *world.* Those are different feelings. The doubt can't fake the difference. I've been near death and the court fading for weeks and never once felt the floor vanish — until it was *you* in those thorns." My thumbs move over his fading cheekbones. "I don't love you because you're dying. I love you because you opened the gates. Because you ran a mile through the grey wood and *asked* instead of taking. Because you'd fade rather than manipulate me, and tell me the truth even when the lie would save you. I loved you before tonight. The dying just made me brave enough to say it."

His ember eyes are wet, dim, disbelieving. "Eira—"

"So I'm choosing." I lean my forehead against his. "Freely. Eyes open. Not to save the court — let the court hear me, let your law hear me: I would walk away and let it all fade before I'd give you a feeling I didn't mean. I'm choosing *you.* The impossible noble martyr of a king who I'd want in an empty room with nothing in the balance and no curse in the world." A breath, against his mouth. "I love you, Caedmon. Freely. Now stop being noble and let me."

And I kiss him.

It's not like anything. It's not herbs and it's not the blight and it's not the careful held-back light I pour into patients — it's *everything*, freely given, a hundred years of his grief and a season of mine collapsing into one warm impossible chosen thing, and I feel it the instant it takes: a thread, between us, going taut and bright and *true*, the bond the lore promised, love freely chosen on both sides, snapping into place like a key finding a lock that's waited a century to be turned.

The heart-fire blazes.

Not gutters — *blazes*, the dying coals roaring up gold for the first time in a hundred years, and through the high windows I see it spread, the warmth racing out from the heart-fire chamber through the whole fading court — the grey *receding*, towers flushing gold, ashen trees greening, the blight pulling back not three feet around my boots but across the entire Ember Court at once, a hundred years of fade rolling back like a tide finally, finally turning.

And in my arms the Ember King draws a breath that isn't grey — ember-light catching in his chest, the fade in his heart *slowing, reversing*, color flooding back into the ancient beautiful face.

The mending has started.

Caedmon pulls back just enough to look at me, ember eyes blazing gold and alive and astonished, and the smile that breaks across his face is nothing held back at all.

"It's real," he breathes — not a question, a wonder. "The court felt it. The law accepted it. You chose me *freely*, with everything in the balance, and the law — the law that can't be fooled — *believed* you." He laughs, helpless, alive. "Goddess, Eira. You did the impossible thing. You loved me true enough to fool a curse that exists specifically to catch a lie."

But even as the court blooms gold around us, even as the bond hums bright and true between us, I see his face change — because the mending is *slow*, the lore said, gradual, drawing on the bond over time. The court is saved, but not yet healed. The curse is breaking, but not yet broken.

And somewhere across the grey wood, the Queen of Thorns just felt a hundred years of her rival's fade *reverse* in a single night — and she knows, now, exactly what it means, and exactly how little time she has left to stop it.

chapters.title: What Blooms, What Burns
chapters.body:
For three days, the Ember Court remembers how to live.

It's the most beautiful thing I've ever seen — a kingdom waking from a century of dying. The grey rolls back tower by tower. The cathedral-trees green from root to crown. The bruise-colored river runs silver again, actually silver, the way the old songs said. Faded courtiers stand in the new gold light with tears on their ancient faces, and the faded child brings me a whole armful of flowers that bloom bright the moment they leave the greying earth, and Brambleback weeps openly in the long gallery beneath the bright-threaded woman, telling her, over and over, *it's mended, it's finally mended, you can rest now.*

And Caedmon — Caedmon comes back to life in front of me.

The grey recedes from his heart as the bond does its slow work, the ember-light blazing back, the king growing warmer and brighter and more *alive* by the hour. He laughs now, real laughs. He reaches for me without calculating the cost. The cold ancient martyr who'd rather fade than ask becomes, in three golden days, a man so openly, helplessly in love that the whole reblooming court teases him for it, and he doesn't care, because he spent a hundred years frozen and he's done being frozen.

"It's slow," he warns me, the two of us on a balcony watching the green return to the wood. "The bond mends gradually — the court won't be whole for a season yet, maybe a year. And until it's fully healed, the curse isn't fully broken. It's *breaking.* There's a difference." His arm tightens around me. "But it's breaking, Eira. For the first time in a century, it's breaking instead of building. You did that. You loved me true enough to turn the tide."

I should be happy. I am happy. But I'm a healer, and healers don't trust a wound that's only half-closed, and there's a cold thread of worry I can't shake: *it's slow. It's not done. There's a window.*

Nyx finds the window.

She comes on the fourth day, and she doesn't send an envoy, and she doesn't come to take me. She comes with her whole court, her entire ascendant winter host, pouring out of the grey wood toward the reblooming Ember Court — because she's done the same math I have. The mending is slow. The curse isn't broken yet, only breaking. And a court that's half-healed is a court that can still be *stopped* — if she shatters it before the bond finishes its work.

"She knows," Caedmon says grimly, watching the silver tide advance from the battlements. "She felt the mending start. She knows that if she lets it complete, the Ember Court rises whole and her century of feasting ends — her power's built on our fade; a healed Ember Court means a Thorn Court that starts dying instead." His jaw sets. "So she's going to try to kill the mending in its cradle. Break the court, break the bond, before it finishes. And she's bringing everything."

I look out at the winter host — far larger than the force I burned in her throne room, the Thorn Court's full strength, fresh and silver and furious — against an Ember Court that's reblooming but nowhere near its old strength, a kingdom three days out of a century-long deathbed.

"Can we hold?" I ask.

Caedmon is quiet a moment. "A week ago, no. We were too faded; she'd have walked through us." His ember eyes find mine, and there's iron in them, and something else — the thing the bond gave back to him, the will to *fight* instead of fade. "But a week ago I didn't have you, and the court didn't have hope, and the curse was building instead of breaking." He takes my hand. "We're not the dying court she thinks she's coming to finish off, Eira. We're a court that just remembered it's alive — with a king who's no longer fading, an exile-prince who'll fight like fire, and a leaf-marked queen who can throw the blight off her wrist like a spear." A fierce, alive smile, nothing like the cold king who took me in chains. "Let her come. Let's show the Queen of Thorns what a *mending* court can do."

The horns of the Thorn Court sound across the greening wood.

And the Ember Court — reblooming, half-healed, but *alive* for the first time in a hundred years — sounds its own horns back, gold and defiant, and rises to meet them.

chapters.title: The Siege
chapters.body:
The Thorn Court breaks against the Ember walls like winter against a hearth, and for the first time in a hundred years, the hearth holds.

I've never been in a real battle — the courtyard skirmish was a brawl next to this. Nyx's full host floods the greening wood, silver and thorn and cold, and throws itself at the reblooming court, and the noise and the terror of it are beyond anything Ashford's worst plague-winter prepared me for. But I'm not the village healer who flinched at fae horns anymore. I'm something the lore says can mend or corrupt any court alive, and I have spent weeks learning exactly what these hands can do.

So I fight.

I take the walls with Caedmon, and where the Thorn warriors mass thickest I throw the blight — the fade, the grey, leaping off my marked wrist in waves that leach the winter-strength out of whole ranks at once, immortals dropping greyed and gasping. And where the Ember fae fall wounded I drop to my knees and *heal*, the gold light pouring out, *stay, stay, not yet* — the same two gifts, the unmaking and the mending, the spear and the salve, both of them mine. The Ember host fights twice as hard with a leaf-marked queen mending their wounded behind them and unmaking their enemies before them.

Caedmon fights beside me, blazing — actually blazing, ember-fire restored, no longer hoarding a dying king's last reserves but spending freely the warmth the bond keeps renewing. He's magnificent and terrifying and *alive*, and every time our eyes meet across the chaos the bond hums bright between us, and I understand that this is what the Ember Court was always supposed to be: not a fading thing, but *this*, fierce and gold and worth defending.

Aric holds the eastern wall with the exiles who came back for him, fire-bright and laughing even now, and when I catch his eye he throws me a grin that says *told you to make him work for it* and I throw the blight at a Thorn warrior about to take his flank and he laughs harder.

We're winning. Goddess help us, against the full might of the ascendant Thorn Court, we're actually winning — because a mending court fights like nothing the worlds have seen, and Nyx came expecting a deathbed and found a wildfire.

Which is when Nyx stops sending warriors and comes herself.

She comes through her own host like the heart of winter walking, frost-eyed and furious, and she's not aiming for the walls or the gate or the heart-fire. She's aiming for the thing that's beating her — the bond. She can't break a love freely given; the law protects it. But she can break one half of it.

She comes for Caedmon.

I see her cut through the Ember line toward him, silver and terrible, a lance of concentrated thorn-magic forming in her hand — not the winter-thorn of a warrior, but something older and worse, *curse-magic*, the same engineered malice she used to start the fade a century ago, distilled now into a single killing strike. And I understand, with a flash of pure ice, what it's meant to do: not just kill the king, but *finish the curse* — to drive a century of engineered fade into Caedmon's heart all at once, to snuff the ember and break the bond and plunge the half-healed court back into the dark before the mending can complete.

"CAEDMON!" I scream it across the battle. "*BEHIND YOU—*"

He turns. He's fast, he's blazing, he's everything the bond gave back to him.

He's not fast enough.

Nyx drives the lance of curse-magic into the Ember King's chest, into the heart that just spent a hundred years fading and three days healing, and I feel it through the bond like it's gone into my own body — the ember-light *screaming*, the fade rushing back all at once, a century of curse poured into him in a single strike — and Caedmon Ember King, restored, alive, beloved, falls.

The bond between us shudders, and starts, horribly, to go dark.

chapters.title: Finish It
chapters.body:
I'm across the battle before I decide to move. The world narrows to him — Caedmon on the greening stones, the curse-lance buried in his chest, the ember-light guttering out of him faster than anything I've ever seen, the bond between us darkening like a coal going to ash.

I drop beside him and pour everything I have into him at once.

It's not enough. Nyx didn't just wound him — she drove a century of engineered fade into his heart in a single strike, and my healing pours in and the grey *drinks* it and keeps spreading, exactly the way it drank the light from Lord Fenn that first day, except a hundred times worse, because this is the whole curse concentrated into one killing blow.

"Caedmon. *Caedmon.*" His ember eyes are dim, finding me. "Stay. Stay, you impossible man, you don't get to fade *now*, not after everything—"

"The mending." His voice is barely there. The battle rages around us; Aric is fighting toward us; Nyx watches from the silver tide, waiting to see her century of patience pay off. "Eira — the bond was healing me *slowly.* She poured the whole curse in at once. The slow mending can't outrun it." His grey hand finds mine. "There's only — the lore — the slow way and the—"

"The fast way." The old way. The sacrifice. The thing that killed the woman in the tapestry. The full mending, all at once — a single mortal's whole life's light, poured out, to mend the court and break the curse completely in one giving instead of slowly over a season. It would finish the mending *now*, faster than the curse-strike can kill him.

And it would kill *me.* The way it killed her.

I understand, kneeling on the bloody stones with the dying king's hand in mine, that this is the exact choice the woman in the tapestry made. A dying court. A way to save it that costs your life. And no time to agonize.

"No," Caedmon rasps, reading my face, fighting the fade to get the word out. "*No.* Eira — not the old way — I won't have you die, I would rather fade, I have *always* rather faded than have you—"

"I know." I press my forehead to his, the bond flickering between us. "I know you would. That's why I love you, you impossible martyr. And that's exactly why it's *my* choice and not yours." I lift my head and look at him, and I'm crying, and I've never been more sure of anything. "But listen to me, because I'm a healer and I read a wound better than anyone in two kingdoms, and I've just read this one. The old way kills the healer because she pours out *all* her light, alone, into a court with nothing to give back." My voice steadies. "But I'm not alone, Caedmon. I have a *bond.* A true one, freely given, both ways. The slow mending draws on it gently. But what if I don't pour out my light *alone*—" I grip his hand. "—what if I pour it through the *bond*? You and me, together, both halves, all at once? Not one mortal emptying herself into a void. Two souls, bound, mending the court *between* us — your ember and my blood, the bond carrying half the cost each."

His dim eyes widen. "That's — the lore never — no one's ever—"

"No one's ever had a bond *and* needed the fast way at the same time. The woman in the tapestry mended you alone, with no bond, and it killed her. I'm not her. I have *you.*" I squeeze his hand hard. "It might not work. It might kill us both. But the slow way's too slow and the old way kills me and letting you fade isn't a thing I'm willing to survive — so I'm choosing the third thing, the thing that's only possible because of what we are to each other." A breath, against his mouth, the battle roaring, Nyx watching, the bond flickering. "Finish it *with* me, Caedmon. Not me pouring out alone. *Us.* Together. Now."

For one heartbeat the dying king looks at me — terrified, undone, a hundred years of *I'd rather fade* warring with the woman asking him, for once, not to fade alone but to *fight* alongside her.

And Caedmon Ember King, for the first time in his ancient life, chooses not to sacrifice himself nobly — but to *stay*, and spend himself *with* someone, which is so much harder and so much braver.

"Together," he breathes, and grips my hand, and opens the bond all the way.

And I pour everything I am into the light — and feel him pour everything he is back — and the two of us, bound, blazing, mortal and king, reach for the curse together to break it once and for all.

chapters.title: The Mending and the Price
chapters.body:
The light that goes up off the two of us is not like anything the worlds have seen in a hundred years.

I feel my whole life's worth of it pour out — but not alone, not into a void. It pours through the bond, and Caedmon's ember-fire pours back through it to meet me, and the two streams braid together into something neither of us could make alone: a column of gold light that erupts from the heart of the battle and races out across the entire Ember Court, the whole kingdom at once.

And the curse *breaks.*

Not slowly. All at once, completely — a century of engineered fade shattering like ice in spring. The grey doesn't recede this time; it *vanishes*, burned out of every tower and tree and faded fae, the Ember Court blazing back to its full ancient glory in the space of a single blinding moment. The cathedral-trees crown themselves in gold. The river runs molten silver. Every faded courtier blazes whole. And the curse-lance in Caedmon's chest crumbles to nothing, the engineered fade scoured out of his heart by the bonded light, the Ember King gasping back to full blazing life beneath my hands.

It works. The thing no one's ever done. We mend the court *together*, and the bond carries the cost between us, and — 

I'm still alive.

I feel it the moment the light fades: I'm spent, hollowed, shaking — but I'm *breathing.* The old way killed the woman in the tapestry because she poured out everything alone into a court that gave nothing back. I poured out everything too — but Caedmon poured back, and the bond shared the cost, and where she emptied herself into a void, I emptied myself into *him*, and he caught me.

We did the impossible. The court is whole. The curse is broken. And we're both alive.

And the Thorn Court is *screaming.*

I look up, dazed, from the blazing stones. Nyx's century of feasting was built on the Ember Court's fade — her power, her ascendance, all of it leeched from a dying rival. And the Ember Court isn't dying anymore. It's *whole*, blazing, ascendant — which means the thing that fed the Thorn Court for a hundred years has just been ripped away, and across the battlefield I watch the silver host *gutter*, the winter-strength bleeding out of them, the Thorn Court beginning, for the first time in a century, to *fade* — because that's the law too, the wheel turning, the engineered curse rebounding on the one who built it.

Nyx stands in the middle of her failing host, frost-eyes wide with disbelief, watching a hundred years of stolen power drain away in a single moment.

"No," she breathes. "*No*— you can't have— no one's ever survived the full mending, the bloodline *dies*, that's the *price*—"

"No one's ever done it bonded," I say, getting to my feet, swaying, Caedmon's hand in mine — Caedmon, alive, blazing, whole, rising beside me. "You engineered a curse a hundred years ago, Nyx, and you built your whole kingdom on it. And it just broke. And the law you tricked into cursing the Ember Court—" I look at her failing host, her guttering throne-power "—turns out it doesn't like being *used* to break faith any more than it liked the Ember King breaking faith the first time. You're the one who broke faith, all those years ago. The wheel just came back around to you."

But even as I say it — even as the Thorn Court fades and the Ember Court blazes and we win — I feel something *wrong.*

The mending worked. We're alive. But breaking a hundred-year curse, all at once, the way we just did — it left something open. A wound in the world, where the curse used to be. I feel it through the bond, through the leaf on my wrist, through the blazing court: the mending isn't *finished.* It's like a tremendous door has been forced open and now stands ajar, and something in the old law is... *waiting.* A price the lore never named, because no one ever survived the full mending to discover there was more to pay.

Caedmon feels it too. His blazing face turns grave. "Eira. Something's—"

Brambleback reaches us through the chaos, ancient face stricken, and he's holding the oldest scroll, and his deep-well eyes are full of a new dread.

"The full mending," the old steward gasps. "It was always meant to kill the healer — and the death was the *anchor.* The light has to be *held* in place by something, child, or the broken curse won't stay broken. In the tapestry, her *death* was the anchor — her life, spent and gone, sealed the mending forever." He looks between us, horrified. "But you didn't die. You survived it, bonded. Which means the mending has nothing to anchor it. The door is open and there's nothing holding it." His voice drops. "It will stay open — the curse will come *back*, worse, everything we just won undone — unless an anchor is paid. A life, or its equal." His ancient hands tremble. "You cheated the price of dying, Eira. But the price still has to be *paid.* And now the law is waiting to see what you'll give instead."

chapters.title: The Anchor
chapters.body:
"What does it mean," I say carefully, "a life *or its equal.* What's the equal of a death?"

Brambleback's ancient hands tremble on the scroll. "The mending must be *anchored* in something that crosses over — something given up forever, that can't be taken back. Her death anchored it because death is the most permanent crossing there is: mortal to gone." His deep-well eyes meet mine. "But there's one other crossing the old law counts as equal. A crossing just as permanent, just as final, that doesn't require dying." He says it gently, like it's a wound. "Mortal to *fae.*"

The blazing court goes quiet around us.

"If you give up your mortality, child — cross over fully, stop being a mortal who can touch fae magic and become a creature *of* the fae, of this court, permanently, with no way back — that crossing is as final as a death. Your mortal life, ended; a new one begun, bound to the Ember Court forever. *That* could anchor the mending." His voice drops. "You'd live. But you'd never be mortal again. Never age and die as your kind does. Never cross the border home to Ashford and belong there. Never be wholly the woman who walked out of that village. You'd be the Ember Court's, body and blood and years, for as long as it stands — which, mended, is forever."

The price. Not my death. My *home.* My mortality. The whole human life on the other side of the border — the village, the framing of ordinary years, growing old, the small mortal span I was born to. I'd keep my life, but I'd give up *that* life, completely and forever, and become something else, something ageless, something bound to a court of embers until the end of time.

"No." Caedmon's voice, ragged. He's gripping my hand, his blazing face stricken. "No. Brambleback — there has to be another anchor, anything else, my life, I'll—"

"It can't be you, sire. The anchor has to be the one who *did* the mending. The bloodline." Brambleback's voice is heavy with grief. "It's her crossing or it's nothing. And if it's nothing, the curse returns — worse, unmoored, the whole court and everyone in it back into the dark within days. There's no third door this time." He looks at me, ancient and sorrowful. "I'm sorry, child. You cheated the death. You did the impossible. And the law's answer is to offer you the only other crossing it accepts — and make you choose it with your eyes open, the way you've chosen everything."

Caedmon turns to me, and the king who spent a hundred years choosing to fade rather than take anything from me does the bravest and most selfish thing I've ever seen him do — he *begs.*

"Don't," he says, low and wrecked, both my hands in his now. "Eira — don't pay this. Not your home, not your mortality, not the whole human life you were born to, not for me, not for this court. I would rather—" His voice breaks. "I have spent a century saying I'd rather fade than take from you, and I *meant* it, and I mean it now more than I ever have. Let the curse come back. Let me fade. Let the whole court go into the dark — I will take all of it, gladly, before I let you give up being *mortal*, give up your home, give up the ordinary human years you should have had, to anchor my kingdom forever." His ember eyes blaze and break. "Please. This is the one thing. Let me lose everything instead of you losing *that.*"

And there it is — the same impossible nobility, the martyr to the end, offering to take his whole court into the dark rather than let me pay a price.

I take his blazing, terrible, beloved face in both my hands.

"Caedmon," I say softly. "You've spent a hundred years deciding what I'm allowed to give. Opening gates, refusing to ask, choosing to fade *for* me, *at* me, *around* me." I hold his gaze. "And every single time, you've been so busy protecting me from a choice that you never once asked what I actually *want.*" A breath. "So ask me. For once in your ancient noble stubborn life — don't decide for me. *Ask* me. Ask me what I want."

His mouth works. The whole court holds its breath. And the Ember King, who only ever learned to command, who learned to let go but never to ask, finally — finally — asks:

"What do you want, Eira?" Barely a whisper. "Truly. Free. With nothing decided for you. What do you *want?*"

chapters.title: What I Want
chapters.body:
It's the first time anyone's ever asked me that. Not *what will you give*, not *what can you do*, not *what are you* — *what do you want.* Free. With nothing decided for me.

And the strange thing — the thing that surprises even me — is that I already know.

"I want this," I say.

I don't say it for him. I make sure of that, in the saying, the way I made sure walking into the Thorn Court. I turn it over in the cold clear way I turn over a wound, looking for the lie, the duty, the pity, the *I'm only saying this to save everyone.* And it isn't there. What's there is simpler and more selfish and more *mine* than any noble sacrifice.

"I came from a village where I was the healer who couldn't save anyone from a fading world," I say. "I framed ordinary years and lost patients to a grey I couldn't touch, and I told myself the small life was all there was, the way my grandmother's grandmother must have told herself, before she walked across a border and found out her hands could do the impossible." I look around at the blazing court — the towers, the trees, the faded child holding her bright flowers, Brambleback weeping, Aric grinning fierce and bloody, all of it whole because of me. "And then I crossed a border and found out I'm not small. That I can mend what immortals can't. That a whole kingdom lives because I chose it. That I'm *needed* and *fierce* and *more* than a framing shop and a quiet mortal span." My voice steadies, certain. "Why would I give that back? Why would I cross *back* to being the small healer in the fading village, when I could be *this* — when I could be the Ember Queen who broke a hundred-year curse and remade a kingdom?"

Caedmon stares at me. "Eira—"

"I'm not paying the price to save you, Caedmon. I want you to hear that, and so does your law." I hold his gaze, fierce. "I'm paying it because I *want* the life it buys. I want the court — these people, this fire, this fight. I want to be the thing I am here instead of the thing I was there. And yes—" my voice softens "—I want *you*, ageless and impossible and mine, for as long as a mended Ember Court stands, which is forever, and I would be a fool to trade forever-with-you for an ordinary mortal span of growing old alone in a village that's only just stopped fading." A breath. "You asked what I want. *This.* All of it. Freely. Eyes open. Not your sacrifice — *my choice.*"

And I cross.

I don't wait for permission — there's no one left to ask, it's mine — I open the leaf on my wrist and I pour the last of my mortal light not *out* but *in*, into the crossing, mortal to fae, the most final door there is short of death. It doesn't hurt. That's the thing I'll remember. It feels like the held breath I've been holding my whole life finally, fully let go — the small frightened village healer crossing over into the thing she was always going to become.

The light blazes one last time. And it *anchors.*

I feel it lock — the mending sealing into me, into my crossed-over, no-longer-mortal life, the open door in the world swinging shut and *staying* shut, anchored not in a death this time but in a queen who chose to stay. The curse doesn't just break. It's *sealed*, permanently, by the one bloodline that survived it and chose the court with her eyes open.

The Ember Court roars whole around me, and across the wood the Thorn Court gutters out — Nyx's stolen century collapsing all at once, the law she tricked rebounding fully, her ascendant kingdom fading into the grey she condemned her rival to. I see her, far off, frost-eyes wide, screaming at a host that's turning to ash around her, a queen who built everything on a curse watching the wheel come all the way around. The lore will say the Thorn Court faded that day, and its queen with it, undone by the exact law she thought she'd mastered. Faith broken comes back to the breaker. It always does.

And I open my eyes — eyes that catch the fae-light differently now, eyes that are no longer quite mortal — on a blazing court, a sealed mending, a fallen rival, and a king staring at me like I've rewritten the worlds.

"You crossed," Caedmon breathes, disbelieving, reaching for me. "For *yourself.* Because you *wanted* it. Not for me. Not for the court. Because you wanted the life." A laugh breaks out of him, helpless, alive, undone. "Goddess, Eira. A hundred years I spent learning to let you go free — and you used the freedom to choose *staying.* To choose *this.* To choose *me.*"

"To choose me, actually," I correct, stepping into his arms, the new ageless light singing in my blood. "You just happen to be part of the life I want." I look up at him, the Ember Queen, no captive, no cure, no sacrifice — a woman who walked into a fading court in chains and is walking out of it as its queen, by her own free choice. "Now. There's the small matter of the crown you're about to put on my head — and the conditions I'm going to set before I wear it."

chapters.title: The Queen's Conditions
chapters.body:
I set my conditions in the throne room, with the whole reblooming court to witness, because a thing said in front of everyone can't be quietly undone later. I learned that much watching Sterlings and Thorn Queens — power means nothing unless it's witnessed.

"I'll wear the crown," I tell Caedmon, and the court, "on three conditions. And they're not requests. They're the price of *me*, and you've all just seen what my price is worth."

The court listens. Even the oldest fae, who watched a mortal walk in here in chains a season ago, listen to her like a queen.

"First: the tithe ends. Forever." I think of Ashford, of the wagon of grain and the measure of iron and the people taken to the courts and never seen again. "No more villages paying for the privilege of bordering the fae. No more *people* collected like grain. The mortal lands and the Ember Court are neighbors now, not master and tribute. Ashford gets a healer who actually visits — me — and the border becomes a door that opens both ways, not a wall with a toll." I hold Caedmon's gaze. "I was almost a tithe. I won't reign over a court that takes them."

"Done," Caedmon says, without hesitation. "It was always a cruelty. I was too cold to see it. Done."

"Second: no more bargains-in-chains. Ever." This one I say to the whole court, to the law, to the bright-threaded woman in the gallery whose memory started all of this. "The Ember Court fell into a hundred-year curse because it broke faith with someone who gave freely — and the woman who saved it the first time was used and discarded. That ends. This court keeps faith. With everyone. The old law won't have to punish us, because we won't *earn* it. We give freely, and we keep faith with those who give to us, and we never again take in chains what only works when it's freely offered." A breath. "Brambleback — write it into the court's law. The first law of the new Ember Court: *kept faith.* Carve it over the gate."

The old steward bows so deep his bark-rough brow nearly touches the floor, and there are tears in his deep-well eyes, and I think the woman in the tapestry would be glad.

"And the third condition?" Caedmon asks softly.

I turn to Aric.

He's leaning against a pillar, fire-bright and bloody from the siege, the exile who fought his way back, who warned me on the first day not to trust them and then led a dying court to my rescue. He raises an eyebrow, wary.

"The exile ends," I say. "Aric comes home. Not as a threat to the throne — as its sworn shield. The court needs someone who'll tell the King when he's being a noble idiot, and Aric's had a century of practice." I hold his gold gaze. "You fought through the gate to warn me they'd use me, Aric. You were wrong about your brother, in the end — but you were *right* to fight for someone who couldn't fight for herself. That's the kind of wolf a court needs at its walls. Come home. Be the Ember Court's protector. Be your brother's brother again."

Aric is quiet a moment. Then the warm fierce grin breaks across his face, edged now with something gone soft.

"I told you to make him work for it," he says.

"He asked me what I wanted," I say. "Took him a hundred years, but he asked. That's work enough."

"Then I'll come home." Aric pushes off the pillar and crosses the throne room, and clasps his brother's arm — the first time, Brambleback whispers later, that the two of them have touched in a century — and the warmth between them is awkward and real and a hundred years overdue. "Someone has to keep the two of you from being insufferable. Might as well be me." He grins at me. "Welcome to the family, Queen Eira. Such as it is."

"Such as it is," I agree, and mean it, and find that I have one — a court, a king, a brother-in-arms, a steward who weeps, a faded child who isn't faded anymore. A family. The thing the framing shop and the quiet village span could never quite be.

And Caedmon takes the crown — the Ember crown, antlered and ancient and blazing whole for the first time in a century — and instead of setting it on my head, he holds it out, in his open hand, and waits.

Because he's learned. He won't put it on me. He'll let me take it. Freely. Eyes open. The way I've taken everything.

I take the crown from the open hand of the King who learned to ask, and I set it on my own head.

And the Ember Court, whole and blazing and free, kneels to a queen who was once a captive — by her choice, on her terms, and no one's tithe ever again.

chapters.title: The King Who Learned to Ask
chapters.body:
The Ember Court takes a season to fully remember what it is, and I take that same season to learn what *I* am now.

It's strange, being ageless. I keep reaching for the mortal certainties — the count of years, the small span, the quiet ending — and finding them gone, replaced by something vast and open that I'm still learning to live inside. But it's not lonely, the way I feared. I cross the border to Ashford often — the door that opens both ways, my first condition — and I heal there, and old Halloran, who lived after all, grumbles that I've gone strange and fae-eyed and then makes me tea anyway. The village remembers me. I make sure of it. I won't be a savior who vanishes into a court and becomes a story; I'll be the neighbor who visits, the healer who comes, the proof that the border isn't a wall anymore.

And the court — my court — reblooms into something the worlds haven't seen in a century: an Ember Court that keeps faith, that takes no tithes, that opens its gate to a mortal village and carves *kept faith* over the arch. The fae are strange creatures, ancient and proud, and they don't change easily. But they change for a queen who broke their curse and chose their court with her eyes open, and they change for a king who's no longer cold.

Because Caedmon — Caedmon is the thing I'm proudest of, in the end.

The cold King, the martyr who'd rather fade than ask, learns. Slowly, awkwardly, beautifully. He learns to *ask* — for my opinion, for my company, for the things he wants instead of nobly denying himself. He learns to take what's freely given without flinching from the having of it. He laughs now, often, a sound the court had forgotten he could make. He's still proud, still ancient, still occasionally so noble I want to throw a healer's kit at his head — but the ice is gone, melted by a season of being loved by someone who refused to let him fade alone.

"Do you regret it?" he asks me one evening, the two of us on the balcony where we watched the green return, the wood blazing gold below us. "The crossing. Your mortality. The ordinary life. You could have grown old in a village, Eira. Quiet. Human. Yours." His ember eyes hold mine, and even now, even sure of me, there's a flicker of the old fear — *did she pay too much, was it really free.* "Forever is a long time to be sure."

"Ask me properly," I tell him.

A breath. The corner of his mouth — the smile he no longer swallows. "Do you regret choosing this? Choosing the court? Choosing *me?*"

"No," I say, and let him feel down the bond exactly how true it is. "I spent twenty years being told the small life was the only one a girl like me would get. And then I crossed a border and found out I could mend what immortals couldn't, break a curse a hundred years deep, remake a kingdom's cruelest laws, and love an impossible ancient martyr of a king into learning how to ask for what he wants." I lean into him, the ageless light singing between us. "Why would I regret trading a quiet span for *that?* I didn't give up a life, Caedmon. I traded a small one for an enormous one. I'd do it again every single time."

He pulls me close, and the bond hums bright and true and *chosen*, and below us the Ember Court blazes whole in the gold evening, and I think of the woman in the tapestry — my blood, a hundred years gone, who poured out her whole life for a court that betrayed her.

I think she'd be glad it ended differently this time. That her bloodline came back not to die for a court, but to remake it. That the leaf-mark she passed down through a century of quiet mortal years ended up on the wrist of a queen who kept faith, and was kept faith with, and chose — freely, eyes open, every step — to stay.

"What are you thinking?" Caedmon murmurs.

"That she'd be glad," I say. "The woman in the tapestry. That it ended in a crown instead of a grave."

"So am I," says the King who learned to ask, and holds his queen on the balcony of a court that remembered how to live, as the gold evening settles over a kingdom no longer fading, and a forever neither of us has to be afraid of stretches bright and open ahead.

chapters.title: Epilogue: The Door That Opens Both Ways
chapters.body:
A year to the day after a fae host rode into Ashford to collect a tithe, there's a festival at the border — and it's the strangest, brightest thing either world has seen in a thousand years.

It was my idea. Of course it was. The border between the mortal lands and the Ember Court, which was a wall with a toll for as long as anyone could remember, is a *door* now — my first condition, carved into law — and once a year we open it wide and the two worlds meet in the middle, in a meadow that used to be grey and now blooms gold. Mortals from Ashford and the villages beyond, wary and amazed. Fae from the Ember Court, ancient and curious. Children from both worlds, who take to each other instantly the way children do, mortal and fae chasing each other through the bright grass while their elders stand around not quite knowing what to do with a peace this old wound never imagined.

Old Halloran is here, grumbling, fae-eyed-suspicious, eating Ember honey-cakes and pretending not to love them. The faded child who isn't faded — her name is Wren, I learned, which made me smile for reasons I couldn't explain — shows a mortal girl how to make a flower bloom gold, and the mortal girl shrieks with delight, and somewhere a hundred-year-old grief lets go a little more.

Brambleback presides over it all, weeping happily, as is his way. Aric runs the border patrol that's become a border *welcome*, fierce and warm and finally home, and there's a sharp-eyed fae captain who's started finding excuses to patrol beside him, which I am watching with great interest and zero intention of meddling. (Much.)

And Caedmon stands beside me at the heart of it — the Ember King, warm where he was ice, who took a healer in chains a year ago and learned, the hard way, the slow way, the only way that ever works, that the things worth having can't be taken, only given, only chosen, only asked for.

"You did this," he says, watching two worlds tentatively, joyfully mingle in a meadow that used to be dying. "All of it. The mending, the law, the door, the peace. A year ago this border was a wall and my court was a century from gone." His hand finds mine, the bond humming bright and chosen between us. "And then a village healer threw the blight off her wrist and refused to be a tithe, and remade two worlds." He turns to me, ember eyes warm. "I'm glad I took you. I'm gladder I let you go. I'm gladdest of all that you came back — and that I finally learned how to ask you to stay."

"You're getting sentimental in your old age, King." I lean into him. "Your very, very old age."

"I have forever to be sentimental now," he says. "Thanks to you. I intend to use all of it."

The festival blazes on into the gold evening — two worlds, one door, a curse broken, a queen who chose her own crown. The leaf on my wrist, the mark that meant nothing in a quiet village and everything in a dying court, catches the last of the light. My grandmother's grandmother poured out her life for this court and got a grave and a tapestry. I poured out mine and got a kingdom, a king, a family, two worlds learning to keep faith — and a forever I chose with my eyes wide open.

Somewhere across the meadow, a mortal child and a fae child blow on a grey flower together until it blooms.

That's the whole of it, really. That's what we built. A place where the dying things bloom again, and no one's taken in chains, and faith is kept, and the door — the door that was always a wall — opens, now, both ways.

"Together?" Caedmon asks, the old question, the one he finally learned how to ask.

I lace my ageless fingers through his, under a sky that's done with fading, in a world that's done with walls.

"Together," I say. "Freely. For as long as forever turns out to be."

*— The End —*
